<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Data Privacy

**Data Privacy** (also termed *Information Privacy*) is formally defined as the branch of data ethics and information security concerned with the proper handling, processing, storage, and dissemination of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and other sensitive data. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, data privacy encompasses the principles, policies, procedures, and technological mechanisms that govern *what information about an individual may be collected, stored, used, and shared* with other parties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *Data Privacy is the normative framework that empowers individuals to control the collection, retention, dissemination, and utilization of their personal data by defining boundaries between legitimate data processing and intrusive surveillance.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine your home as a container of personal information.

- **The House Walls** represent your privacy boundary.
- **The Curtains on the Windows** represent *selective disclosure* — you allow neighbors to know you exist, but not what you are doing inside.
- **The Locked Safe** represents *data security* — even authorized visitors cannot access everything.
- **The Doorbell Camera** represents *consent and transparency* — visitors know they are being observed.
- **An Open Diary on the Table** represents a *privacy breach* — your inner thoughts are now exposed without your consent.

In this analogy, **Data Privacy is the master architect** that decides where walls go, how thick they are, and who holds the keys.

## 1.3 Scope of the Topic (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 2 Syllabus Outcomes:**
> After completing this topic, the student should be able to:
> - **CO2 (Understand):** Trace the historical evolution of privacy from ancient times to the digital era.
> - **CO2 (Apply):** Classify data into different degrees of privacy sensitivity.
> - **CO3 (Analyze):** Identify modern privacy risks in data-driven ecosystems.
> - **CO3 (Evaluate):** Distinguish between anonymity, pseudonymity, and unlinkability.

## 1.4 Key Terminology — Quick Reference

| Term | Working Definition | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PII** | Personally Identifiable Information | Data that can identify a specific individual |
| **Quasi-Identifier** | Attributes that can re-identify when combined | E.g., ZIP + DOB + Gender |
| **Anonymization** | Irreversible removal of identity | Strong privacy guarantee |
| **Pseudonymization** | Replacement with reversible tokens | Weaker than anonymization |
| **Consent** | Voluntary agreement to data processing | GDPR Article 6/7 cornerstone |
| **Surveillance Capitalism** | Economic model based on extracting behavioral data | Modern privacy antagonist |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant / Standard Metric to Remember:**
> The **GDPR Fine Ceiling** is the higher of **€20 million** or **4% of annual global turnover**. This is a frequently tested KTU numerical value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The History of Privacy — A Chronological Analysis

The concept of privacy is not a modern invention. It has evolved through six distinct historical epochs:

### Epoch 1: Antiquity (~1754 BC)
- The **Hammurabi Code** contained provisions protecting individuals from unjust exposure.
- Greek philosophers distinguished between *oikos* (private sphere) and *polis* (public sphere).

### Epoch 2: Medieval Common Law (1361 AD)
- The **English Justices of the Peace Act of 1361** criminalized eavesdropping (*eavesdroppers* were punishable).
- Privacy violations were treated as disturbances of peace.

### Epoch 3: The Foundational Article (1890)
- **Samuel Warren and Louis Brandeis** published *"The Right to Privacy"* in the **Harvard Law Review (Volume IV, No. 5)**.
- They defined privacy as *"the right to be let alone"* — **the most cited definition in privacy law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** The phrase *"the right to be let alone"* is a guaranteed 3-mark question in KTU examinations whenever history of privacy is covered.

### Epoch 4: Mid-20th Century Codification (1948–1974)
- **1948**: *Universal Declaration of Human Rights* — **Article 12** explicitly forbids arbitrary interference with privacy.
- **1974**: *US Privacy Act* — first federal codification of fair information practices.

### Epoch 5: Digital Era Legislation (1995–2018)
- **1995**: *EU Data Protection Directive* (Directive 95/46/EC).
- **2000**: *Safe Harbor Principles* (US–EU data transfer framework).
- **2018**: *General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)* — became enforceable **25 May 2018**.
- **2018**: *California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)*.

### Epoch 6: AI and Big Data Era (2018–Present)
- Emergence of **Differential Privacy**, **Federated Learning**, **Homomorphic Encryption**.
- Introduction of the **EU AI Act (2024)** — first comprehensive AI regulation.

## 2.2 The Five Degrees of Privacy

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies a hierarchical taxonomy of privacy:

| Degree | Domain | Example | Breach Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Personal Privacy** | Self / Body | Health status, sexual orientation | Social stigma |
| **2. Informational Privacy** | Data / Records | Financial records, browsing history | Identity theft |
| **3. Organizational Privacy** | Corporate / Institutional | Trade secrets, salaries | Competitive loss |
| **4. Spiritual / Philosophical** | Beliefs / Ideology | Religion, political affiliation | Persecution |
| **5. Decisional Privacy** | Choices / Autonomy | Reproductive choices, voting | Coercion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These degrees are **non-orthogonal** — a single data point (e.g., religion) can simultaneously implicate *Spiritual* AND *Decisional* privacy.

## 2.3 Modern Privacy Risks — The Threat Taxonomy

The following risks characterize the contemporary data ecosystem:

1. **Mass Surveillance**: Government and corporate monitoring at scale.
2. **Re-identification Attacks**: Linking anonymized datasets back to individuals.
3. **Inference Attacks**: Deducing sensitive attributes from non-sensitive ones.
4. **Data Brokerage**: Buying and selling personal data without consent.
5. **Side-Channel Attacks**: Extracting secrets from indirect information (e.g., timing, power consumption).
6. **AI Model Inversion**: Recovering training data from ML models.
7. **IoT Eavesdropping**: Smart devices continuously capturing ambient data.
8. **Algorithmic Discrimination**: Privacy violations via biased automated decisions.
9. **Synthetic Data Leakage**: AI-generated data inadvertently revealing originals.

## 2.4 Anonymity, Pseudonymity, Unlinkability — The Triad

| Property | Definition | Reversible? | KTU Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Anonymity** | Identity completely removed | No (irreversible) | Survey with no ID fields |
| **Pseudonymity** | Replaced with a fictitious ID | Yes (with a key) | Database using UUIDs |
| **Unlinkability** | Two actions cannot be tied to same person | Conditional | Tor network browsing |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Law | Boundary / Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Re-identification Probability | $P_{reid} \leq \dfrac{1}{k}$ | $k \geq 2$ |
| 2 | k-anonymity Equivalence Class | $\vert \vert Q_i \vert \vert \geq k$ for all $i$ | $\vert Q_i \vert$ is class size |
| 3 | Entropy of Anonymity Set | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $H \geq \log_2 k$ |
| 4 | Information Loss (Generalization) | $IL = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} g_j}{m}$ | $0 \leq IL \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Differential Privacy | $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]$ | $\epsilon \in [0,1]$ typical |
| 6 | l-diversity | Every equivalence class has $\geq l$ distinct sensitive values | $l \geq 2$ |
| 7 | t-closeness | Earth Mover's Distance $\leq t$ | $t \in [0,1]$ |
| 8 | GDPR Fine Ceiling | $\max(\$20M,\ 4\% \text{ turnover})$ | Applicable per violation |
| 9 | Shannon Information Content | $I(x) = -\log_2 P(x)$ | Measured in bits |
| 10 | K-anonymity with Sensitive Attrs | $S \geq l$ per class | Count-based l-diversity |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These frameworks are not academic abstractions. They are deployed in:
- **Healthcare** (HIPAA-compliant medical records release using k-anonymity).
- **Smart Cities** (anonymized mobility data for traffic planning).
- **Census Bureaus** (US Census 2020 used differential privacy).
- **AdTech** (Google's RAPPOR for aggregated statistics).
- **Genomics** (browsing-genome correlation attacks).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Re-identification Probability in k-Anonymity

We derive the upper bound on the probability that an attacker can uniquely identify an individual inside an equivalence class of size $k$.

**Step 1 — Definition of an Equivalence Class.**

Let $T = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n\}$ be a tabular dataset. Let $Q$ be the set of quasi-identifier attributes. Define equivalence classes as:

$$
Q_i = \{r_j \in T \mid Q(r_j) = Q(r_i)\}
$$

**Step 2 — Application of k-Anonymity Constraint.**

The dataset $T$ satisfies k-anonymity if and only if:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\forall i, \quad \vert Q_i \vert \geq k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Attacker Knowledge Assumption.**

The adversary is assumed to know the quasi-identifier values of the target individual. This is the standard *adversary knowledge model* used in privacy literature.

**Step 4 — Probability of Re-identification.**

Given that the target belongs to an equivalence class of size $\vert Q_i \vert$, the probability that an arbitrary record inside that class is the actual target is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{reid} &= \dfrac{1}{\vert Q_i \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worst-Case Upper Bound.**

Since k-anonymity guarantees $\vert Q_i \vert \geq k$ for all classes, the worst case occurs when $\vert Q_i \vert = k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{reid} &\leq \dfrac{1}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

A larger $k$ implies weaker re-identification but greater information loss. This is the **fundamental privacy-utility tradeoff** in statistical disclosure control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Calculation:** For $k = 5$, the maximum re-identification probability is $P_{reid} \leq 1/5 = 0.20 = 20\%$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Information Loss Metric

When quasi-identifiers are generalized (e.g., age 25 → age range [20,30]), information is lost. We quantify this loss.

**Step 1 — Define the Generalization Function.**

Let $g: D_{original} \rightarrow D_{generalized}$ be a generalization map.

**Step 2 — Define Local Information Loss per Record.**

For record $r_j$ with quasi-identifier $q_j$ generalized to $g(q_j)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IL_{local}(r_j) &= \dfrac{\vert g(q_j) \vert}{\vert D_{q_j} \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\vert g(q_j) \vert$ is the number of records in the generalized group and $\vert D_{q_j} \vert$ is the total domain size.

**Step 3 — Define Global Information Loss.**

Averaging across $m$ quasi-identifier attributes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IL_{global} &= \dfrac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} IL_{local}(r_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Analysis.**

The boundary conditions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \leq IL_{global} \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

- $IL_{global} = 0$ corresponds to no generalization (full utility, no privacy).
- $IL_{global} = 1$ corresponds to complete suppression (full privacy, zero utility).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Entropy of the Anonymity Set

The entropy $H$ of an anonymity set measures its *unpredictability*.

**Step 1 — Probability of Selection.**

Let $p_i$ be the probability that the $i$-th member of the anonymity set is the target.

**Step 2 — Shannon Entropy Formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bound under k-Anonymity.**

Since all $n = \vert Q_i \vert \geq k$ members are equally likely, $p_i = 1/n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{n} \log_2 \dfrac{1}{n} = \log_2 n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Entropy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &\geq \log_2 k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Example.**

For $k = 8$, minimum entropy is $H = \log_2 8 = 3$ bits.

---

## 3.4 Differential Privacy — Formal Derivation

**Step 1 — Adjacent Datasets.**

Two datasets $D$ and $D'$ are *adjacent* if they differ by exactly one record: $\vert D \triangle D' \vert = 1$.

**Step 2 — Randomized Mechanism.**

Let $M$ be a randomized algorithm. $M$ satisfies $\epsilon$-differential privacy if:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[M(D) \in S] &\leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]
\end{aligned}
$$

for all measurable $S$.

**Step 3 — Privacy Budget.**

The parameter $\epsilon$ is the *privacy budget*. Smaller $\epsilon \Rightarrow$ stronger privacy but noisier output.

**Step 4 — Composition Theorem.**

For sequential $k$ queries with budgets $\epsilon_1, \epsilon_2, \dots, \epsilon_k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{total} &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently tests the **Gaussian Mechanism** and **Laplace Mechanism** — these add calibrated noise to query outputs.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Privacy Models Compared

| Model | Adversary | Granularity | Re-id Resistance | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **k-Anonymity** | Linkage attacks | Equivalence class | $\leq 1/k$ | Census data |
| **l-Diversity** | Homogeneity attacks | Sensitive diversity | Stronger | Health records |
| **t-Closeness** | Skewness attacks | Distribution distance | Strongest | Demographic data |
| **Differential Privacy** | Arbitrary attackers | Per-record bound | Mathematically provable | Apple, US Census |
| **Synthetic Data** | Reconstruction attacks | Statistical mimicry | Medium | ML training |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]

**Define the term "Right to Privacy" as introduced by Warren and Brandeis in 1890. Why is this article considered foundational to modern data privacy law?**

**Model Answer:**

Warren and Brandeis, in their seminal 1890 Harvard Law Review article titled *"The Right to Privacy"*, defined privacy as **"the right to be let alone"**. They argued that this right exists even when there is no physical trespass or property damage, and that the growing threat from instantaneous photography and newspaper intrusion necessitated legal recognition of an individual's right to determine the extent to which their thoughts, sentiments, and emotions may be communicated to others.

The article is foundational because it was the **first systematic legal articulation** of informational privacy in the modern era. It established four key principles:
- Privacy is a personal right.
- Privacy protects immaterial harm (psychological, reputational).
- Privacy is distinct from property rights.
- Privacy serves the principle of **inviolate personality**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall (3-Mark Questions):**
> Students often write Warren and Brandeis defined privacy but **omit the exact phrase** "the right to be let alone." The phrase itself carries 1.5 marks. Always quote it verbatim.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]

**Differentiate between Anonymity and Pseudonymity with one suitable example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Anonymity | Pseudonymity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reversibility | Irreversible | Reversible via a key |
| Re-identification | Impossible without external data | Possible with the mapping key |
| GDPR status | Not personal data | Considered personal data |
| Example | A survey respondent with no name | A database row labeled with UUID |

**Anonymity Example:** A patient who undergoes surgery and later submits feedback to a hospital through a paper drop-box with no identifying marks. The hospital cannot link the feedback to the patient.

**Pseudonymity Example:** A hospital assigns a UUID like `P-2024-007812` to each patient for research purposes. The UUID alone does not reveal identity, but the hospital retains a private lookup table mapping UUIDs to names, making re-identification possible internally.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:**
> Many students mistakenly write "pseudonymity is the same as anonymity." Under GDPR, **pseudonymized data still counts as personal data** and is therefore not anonymous. This distinction is a 1-mark differentiator.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question 3A. [KTU End Semester — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the historical evolution of privacy from ancient times to the modern digital era. Highlight at least six milestone events with their respective years.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of the five degrees of privacy with one engineering example for each.

### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Milestone 1 — Hammurabi Code (~1754 BC):** The earliest codified protection of individuals from unjust public exposure, marking the dawn of normative privacy thinking.

**Milestone 2 — Justices of the Peace Act (1361 AD):** The first English statutory protection against eavesdroppers, treating privacy violations as criminal offenses.

**Milestone 3 — Warren and Brandeis Article (1890):** Defined privacy as *"the right to be let alone"*; established privacy as a legal right independent of property law.

**Milestone 4 — Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948):** Article 12 explicitly recognized privacy as a fundamental human right against arbitrary interference.

**Milestone 5 — US Privacy Act (1974):** First comprehensive federal regulation governing the collection, maintenance, use, and dissemination of personally identifiable records by federal agencies.

**Milestone 6 — GDPR Enforcement (25 May 2018):** The most influential modern privacy law, imposing extraterritorial reach, mandatory breach notification within **72 hours**, and fines up to **4% of global turnover**.

**Milestone 7 — Differential Privacy at US Census (2020):** First nationwide statistical deployment of formal privacy guarantees.

### Model Answer — Part (b)

**1. Personal Privacy:** A wearable fitness tracker (e.g., smart band) recording heart rate, sleep patterns, and reproductive health.

**2. Informational Privacy:** A banking app storing transaction history and account balances in encrypted cloud storage.

**3. Organizational Privacy:** A startup protecting its proprietary machine-learning model weights from employee exfiltration.

**4. Spiritual/Philosophical Privacy:** Anonymized political-donation records shielding dissenters in authoritarian regimes.

**5. Decisional Privacy:** Encrypted messaging apps (e.g., Signal) protecting reproductive-health discussions from criminal prosecution.

---

### Question 3B. [Alternative Choice for 14 Marks]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **k-anonymity** mathematically. Derive the upper bound on re-identification probability. Illustrate with a worked numerical example where $k = 4$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Differential Privacy** as a formal privacy guarantee. Define the privacy budget $\epsilon$ and discuss its effect on utility.

### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Mathematical Definition:** A dataset $T$ satisfies k-anonymity if every record in $T$ is indistinguishable from at least $k - 1$ other records with respect to the quasi-identifier attributes $Q$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\forall r \in T, \quad \vert \{r' \in T \mid Q(r') = Q(r)\} \vert &\geq k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of Re-identification Probability:**

The probability that a randomly chosen record inside the equivalence class is the target individual is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{reid} &= \dfrac{1}{\vert Q_i \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the k-anonymity constraint $\vert Q_i \vert \geq k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{reid} &\leq \dfrac{1}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example ($k = 4$):**

Consider a hospital releasing records generalized as in the table below.

| Patient | Age Range | ZIP (First 3) | Disease |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | [20,30] | 682 | Flu |
| B | [20,30] | 682 | Cold |
| C | [20,30] | 682 | Asthma |
| D | [20,30] | 682 | COVID |

Since all four patients share the same quasi-identifiers (Age Range = [20,30], ZIP prefix = 682), the equivalence class size is 4, achieving $k = 4$ anonymity. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{reid} &\leq \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Differential Privacy (DP)** is a rigorous mathematical framework ensuring that the inclusion or exclusion of any single record in a dataset does not significantly affect the output of an analysis.

**Formal Definition:** A randomized mechanism $M$ satisfies $\epsilon$-differential privacy if for all adjacent datasets $D$ and $D'$ differing by one record:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[M(D) \in S] &\leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Privacy Budget $\epsilon$:**

- $\epsilon = 0$: Perfect privacy (output distributions identical with or without any individual).
- $\epsilon \approx 1$: Standard practical bound used by Apple and Google.
- $\epsilon \approx 3$: Weaker but still acceptable for aggregated analytics.
- $\epsilon \rightarrow \infty$: No privacy guarantee.

**Effect on Utility:**

Smaller $\epsilon$ requires **larger noise injection** to satisfy the inequality, reducing the accuracy of the released statistics. The **Privacy–Utility Tradeoff** is the central engineering challenge in DP deployment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall (14-Mark Questions):**
> 1. Students often confuse the *output* of $M$ with the *dataset* $D$. $M$ is a **mechanism** (algorithm), not the dataset itself.
> 2. The exponential $e^{\epsilon}$ is sometimes written as $\exp(\epsilon)$; both are accepted, but **never write $e^{\wedge}\epsilon$**.
> 3. Always cite the **adjacent dataset** definition explicitly — this is a common 2-mark loss area.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **Definition Anchor:** *"The right to be let alone"* (Warren and Brandeis, 1890).
- **GDPR Effective Date:** 25 May 2018.
- **GDPR Fine Cap:** Max(€20M, 4% global turnover).
- **Five Degrees of Privacy:** Personal, Informational, Organizational, Spiritual, Decisional.
- **Anonymity vs Pseudonymity:** Anonymity is irreversible; Pseudonymity is reversible via a key.
- **k-Anonymity Formula:** $P_{reid} \leq 1/k$.
- **Entropy Bound:** $H \geq \log_2 k$.
- **Information Loss Range:** $0 \leq IL \leq 1$.
- **Differential Privacy Inequality:** $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]$.
- **Privacy Budget Typical Value:** $\epsilon \in [0, 1]$.
- **l-Diversity:** Sensitive attribute diversity within each equivalence class.
- **t-Closeness:** Earth Mover's Distance bound on sensitive-attribute distribution.
- **Re-identification Attack:** Linking quasi-identifiers back to individuals.
- **Inference Attack:** Deriving sensitive attributes from non-sensitive ones.
- **Composition Theorem:** $\epsilon_{total} = \sum \epsilon_i$ for sequential queries.
- **Modern Risk Keyword:** *Surveillance Capitalism* (Shoshana Zuboff's framework).
- **Census 2020:** First large-scale differential-privacy deployment.
- **Breach Notification Window:** 72 hours under GDPR.
- **Number of Degrees of Privacy:** 5 (per KTU 2024 syllabus).
- **Universal Declaration:** Article 12 protects privacy.
- **Anonymity Set:** The group of indistinguishable candidates for an individual.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Validity and Choice of Attributes and Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT412 - DATA SCIENCE PRIVACY & ETHICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Validity and Choice of Attributes and Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Data Validity?

**Data Validity** is a foundational pillar of data quality that refers to the degree to which data accurately represents the real-world construct, entity, or phenomenon it is intended to measure. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Data Science, Privacy & Ethics*, validity is not merely about whether data is "correct" in isolation — it assesses whether the data is **fit for its intended analytical purpose**, conforms to defined business rules, and conforms to the structural and semantic constraints of its schema.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> Data Validity is the measure of consistency, accuracy, and conformity of data values against a predefined set of rules, constraints, domain ranges, formats, and business logic at the point of entry, transformation, and consumption.

Validity operates on three primary dimensions:

- **Syntactic Validity** — Data conforms to the expected format, type, and structure (e.g., a phone number field contains exactly 10 digits).
- **Semantic Validity** — Data values are meaningful within the domain context (e.g., a patient's age cannot be `250`).
- **Business Rule Validity** — Data satisfies contextual business logic (e.g., `Order_Date <= Delivery_Date`).

## 1.2 What are Attributes and Measures?

In data science terminology:

- An **Attribute** (also called a *variable*, *feature*, or *field*) is any characteristic or property that can be observed or measured. Attributes are the **columns** of a dataset.
- A **Measure** (also called a *metric* or *quantitative variable*) is a specific type of attribute that is expressed numerically and used for quantitative analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Intuition:**
> Imagine you are maintaining a recipe card for a bakery's inventory.
> - The **attributes** are the column headers: `Flour_Type`, `Quantity_kg`, `Expiry_Date`, `Supplier_Name`.
> - The **measures** are the actual numbers you write down: `25 kg`, `500 g`, `12-Dec-2025`.
> - **Data Validity** is the act of checking — *"Did I write 25 kg of flour, or 25 g? Did I write the expiry date correctly?"*
> If the attributes are wrongly chosen (e.g., you measured flour by *volume* in cups instead of *weight* in kg), no amount of correctness in the numbers will save the analysis. **The choice of attributes and the choice of measures are twin decisions.**

## 1.3 The Link Between Validity, Attributes, and Measures

These three concepts form a tightly coupled triad in any data science pipeline:

| Concept | Role in Pipeline | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Attribute Selection** | Decides *what* to measure | Wrong feature → Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO) |
| **Measure Selection** | Decides *how* to quantify | Wrong scale → Misleading statistics |
| **Data Validity** | Verifies *whether the data is trustworthy* | Invalid data → Flawed models & ethics violations |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Choosing an inappropriate attribute or measure **directly violates data validity**. For example, using a *nominal* category (like "color = red") as if it were an *interval* number (averaging "red" and "blue" to get "purple") is a classic validity breach.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Data Validity

According to the KTU 2024 Data Science curriculum, data validity is assessed along five orthogonal axes:

### 2.1.1 Completeness
- **Definition:** The proportion of stored data that is actually populated (not null, not missing).
- **Formula:**
  $$\text{Completeness} = \frac{\text{Count of Non-Null Values}}{\text{Total Count of Expected Values}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.2 Accuracy
- **Definition:** The closeness of a recorded value to the true real-world value.
- **Formula:**
  $$\text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \frac{\vert \text{Recorded Value} - \text{True Value} \vert}{\text{True Value}}\right) \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.3 Consistency
- **Definition:** The degree to which data values agree across multiple systems, tables, or time points.
- **Test:** Compare the same entity's attribute across `n` systems and verify identical values.

### 2.1.4 Timeliness (Currency)
- **Definition:** The time lag between when data should have been recorded and when it was actually recorded.
- **Formula:**
  $$\text{Currency Lag} = T_{\text{required}} - T_{\text{actual}}$$

### 2.1.5 Uniqueness
- **Definition:** The extent to which an entity is represented only once in the dataset (no duplicates).
- **Formula:**
  $$\text{Uniqueness} = \frac{\text{Count of Distinct Records}}{\text{Total Count of Records}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.2 Stevens' Four Scales of Measurement (Attribute Types)

This is **the most heavily tested topic** on this module in KTU exams. Stanley Smith Stevens (1946) classified all measurement into four scales:

### 2.2.1 Nominal Scale
- **Definition:** Categorical labels with **no inherent order**.
- **Examples:** Blood group (A, B, AB, O), Gender, City name, Color.
- **Valid Operations:** Counting, mode, chi-square test.
- **Invalid Operations:** Mean, median, subtraction (Red − Blue is meaningless).

### 2.2.2 Ordinal Scale
- **Definition:** Categorical labels with a **meaningful order**, but unequal/unknown intervals.
- **Examples:** Customer satisfaction (Poor < Fair < Good < Excellent), Education level, Likert scale ratings.
- **Valid Operations:** Median, percentiles, rank correlation (Spearman).
- **Invalid Operations:** Mean (since the gap between "Good" and "Excellent" is not necessarily equal to "Fair" and "Good").

### 2.2.3 Interval Scale
- **Definition:** Numeric scale with **equal intervals but no true zero point**.
- **Examples:** Temperature in Celsius/Fahrenheit, Calendar year, IQ scores.
- **Valid Operations:** Mean, standard deviation, Pearson correlation, addition, subtraction.
- **Invalid Operations:** Ratios (20°C is not "twice as hot" as 10°C).

### 2.2.4 Ratio Scale
- **Definition:** Numeric scale with **equal intervals AND a true absolute zero**.
- **Examples:** Height, Weight, Age, Income, Time duration, Temperature in Kelvin.
- **Valid Operations:** All arithmetic operations including ratios, geometric mean, harmonic mean.
- **Key Property:** Ratios are meaningful (40 kg is twice as heavy as 20 kg).

## 2.3 Choice of Measures: Central Tendency

The choice of which central tendency measure to use **depends entirely** on the scale of measurement:

| Scale | Best Central Tendency | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal | **Mode** | Only categorical labels; no ordering exists |
| Ordinal | **Median** | Order exists, but gaps are unequal |
| Interval | **Mean** or Median | Equal intervals; zero is arbitrary |
| Ratio | **Mean** (Arithmetic/Geometric) | All operations valid; true zero present |

## 2.4 Choice of Measures: Dispersion

| Scale | Best Dispersion Measure |
|---|---|
| Nominal | Frequency count, Entropy |
| Ordinal | Range, Interquartile Range (IQR) |
| Interval | Variance, Standard Deviation |
| Ratio | Variance, Std Dev, Coefficient of Variation |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table — Memorize for ESE:**

| Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Applicable Scale | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Completeness Ratio | $\frac{N_{\text{non-null}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | All scales | Percentage |
| Accuracy Ratio | $\left(1 - \frac{\vert V_r - V_t \vert}{V_t}\right) \times 100$ | All scales | Percentage |
| Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Interval, Ratio | Same as data |
| Median | Middle value of sorted data | Ordinal, Interval, Ratio | Same as data |
| Mode | Most frequent value | Nominal, Ordinal | Categorical |
| Variance | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Interval, Ratio | Squared unit |
| Std Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Interval, Ratio | Same as data |
| Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | Ratio only | Percentage |
| Pearson Skewness | $Sk = \frac{3(\bar{x} - \text{Median})}{s}$ | Interval, Ratio | Dimensionless |
| Spearman Rank Corr. | $\rho = 1 - \frac{6\sum d_i^2}{n(n^2-1)}$ | Ordinal | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule:** Always check the scale of measurement **before** choosing any statistic. Using the mean on nominal data is a guaranteed loss of 2-3 marks in KTU valuation.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Healthcare:** Validating patient records ensures correct diagnosis (life-or-death implications).
- **Finance:** Choosing ratio-scale measures (₹ amounts) vs nominal (account type) prevents incorrect risk modeling.
- **Ethics & Privacy:** Validating consent attributes (Yes/No) prevents unauthorized data usage.
- **Machine Learning:** Feature selection (attribute choice) and encoding (measure transformation) directly determine model validity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Completeness Calculation

**Problem:** A hospital database has `Patient_Records = 5000`. Out of these, `380` records have a missing `Blood_Pressure` field. Calculate the completeness percentage.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values**

$$N_{\text{total}} = 5000, \quad N_{\text{missing}} = 380$$

**Step 2 — Compute Non-Null Count**

$$N_{\text{non-null}} = N_{\text{total}} - N_{\text{missing}} = 5000 - 380 = 4620$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Completeness Formula**

$$\text{Completeness} = \frac{N_{\text{non-null}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values**

$$\text{Completeness} = \frac{4620}{5000} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Simplify**

$$\text{Completeness} = 0.924 \times 100\% = 92.4\%$$

**Interpretation:** The dataset is 92.4% complete for the `Blood_Pressure` attribute. Industry standard for clinical data is ≥ **99.5%**, so this dataset is **invalid** for clinical decision-making and requires imputation or re-collection.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Mean vs Median Selection Based on Scale

**Problem:** A survey of 7 customers rates service on an ordinal Likert scale: `1 = Poor, 2 = Fair, 3 = Good, 4 = Very Good, 5 = Excellent`.
Responses: `{5, 4, 5, 3, 5, 4, 5}`. Should the analyst compute the **mean** or the **median**?

**Step 1 — Identify the Scale**
The Likert scale is **ordinal** (ordered, but gaps between labels are subjective and unequal).

**Step 2 — Apply the KTU Selection Rule**
For ordinal data → Use **Median**, not Mean.

**Step 3 — Sort the Data**
Sorted array: `{3, 4, 4, 5, 5, 5, 5}`

**Step 4 — Locate the Middle Value**
Since $n = 7$ (odd), the median is the $\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)$-th value = 4th value.

$$\text{Median} = 5$$

**Step 5 — Demonstrate Why Mean is Invalid Here**
Compute mean (forbidden operation, shown for contrast only):

$$\bar{x} = \frac{5+4+5+3+5+4+5}{7} = \frac{31}{7} \approx 4.43$$

The value `4.43` lies between "Very Good" and "Excellent" — a label that **does not exist** on the original scale. This is a **semantic validity violation**.

**Conclusion:** Use Median = 5 (Excellent). The mean `4.43` is mathematically defined but **operationally meaningless** and invalid.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Choice of Attribute for Validity Testing

**Problem:** A retail company wants to measure "Customer Loyalty". Which attribute should they choose — `Total_Visits` (ratio) or `Loyalty_Rating` (ordinal: Low/Med/High)?

**Solution — Analytical Matrix:**

| Criterion | `Total_Visits` (Ratio) | `Loyalty_Rating` (Ordinal) |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | High (numeric count) | Low (3 buckets) |
| Statistical Power | Supports mean, std dev, ratios | Supports median, mode only |
| Subjectivity | Objective (POS data) | Subjective (self-reported) |
| Temporal Validity | Always current | Snapshot — becomes stale |
| Construct Validity | Weak (many visits ≠ loyalty) | Stronger (captures intent) |
| Best Use Case | Frequency analysis | Segmentation & qualitative reports |

**KTU Exam Insight:** A common 7-mark question asks students to *justify* their attribute choice. The expected answer should mention **construct validity** — the attribute must actually measure what it claims to measure.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Coefficient of Variation Calculation

**Problem:** Two products have:
- Product A: Mean price = ₹50, Std Dev = ₹5
- Product B: Mean price = ₹500, Std Dev = ₹50

Which product has higher relative variability?

**Step 1 — Recall CV Formula (Ratio Scale only)**

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute CV for Product A**

$$CV_A = \frac{5}{50} \times 100\% = 10\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute CV for Product B**

$$CV_B = \frac{50}{500} \times 100\% = 10\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpret**

$$CV_A = CV_B = 10\%$$

Both products have **equal relative variability**, even though their absolute standard deviations differ by a factor of 10. This demonstrates why CV is essential when comparing datasets with different units or magnitudes.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Why Mode is the Only Valid Central Tendency for Nominal Data

**Premise:** Nominal data has labels $\{L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_k\}$ with no order relation.

**Step 1:** Ordering requires a relation $\leq$ such that $L_i \leq L_j$ is meaningful.
For nominal data, $L_i \leq L_j$ is **undefined**, so median is invalid.

**Step 2:** Arithmetic mean requires addition: $\sum x_i$.
For nominal labels, $L_1 + L_2$ has no semantic interpretation, so mean is invalid.

**Step 3:** Mode = $\arg\max_i \text{freq}(L_i)$, requires only counting.
Counting is defined for all data types, so mode is **always valid** for nominal data.

$$\therefore \text{Mode is the uniquely valid central tendency for nominal data.}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Validity Testing Methods

| Validity Type | What It Tests | Method | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Content Validity** | Coverage of all relevant dimensions | Expert review of attribute set | Hospital data includes BP, Sugar, Cholesterol |
| **Construct Validity** | Attribute truly measures the abstract concept | Factor analysis, correlation with theory | "Engagement" attribute correlates with time-on-page |
| **Criterion Validity** | Matches a gold-standard external measure | Compare prediction vs ground truth | Predicted churn vs actual churn |
| **Internal Validity** | Causal relationship is genuine, not confounded | A/B testing, randomization | New UX design caused conversion lift |
| **External Validity** | Generalizability to other populations | Cross-validation, hold-out testing | Model trained on Kerala data tested on Tamil Nadu data |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Differentiate between the nominal and ordinal scales of measurement with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Nominal Scale | Ordinal Scale |
|---|---|---|
| **Order** | No inherent order among categories | Categories have a meaningful order |
| **Example** | Blood Group (A, B, AB, O) | Customer Rating (Poor, Fair, Good, Excellent) |
| **Valid Central Tendency** | Mode only | Median, Mode |
| **Mathematical Operations** | Only equality check ($=$) and counting | Equality, counting, and ranking |
| **Statistical Tests** | Chi-square, frequency analysis | Spearman rank, Mann-Whitney U |

**(1 mark for each clear distinguishing point + 1 mark for examples.)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Define data validity. List any four dimensions used to measure it."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Data validity is the degree to which data values conform to defined business rules, formats, domain constraints, and accurately represent the real-world construct they intend to measure.

**Four Dimensions (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Completeness** — Are all required values present?
2. **Accuracy** — Do values match the true real-world state?
3. **Consistency** — Do values agree across systems?
4. **Timeliness** — Is the data current enough for its intended use?

*(Alternative valid answers: Uniqueness, Conformity, Precision, Integrity.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain Stevens' four scales of measurement with suitable examples. Discuss the implications of choosing the wrong scale for statistical analysis.

**(b) [7 marks]** A banking dataset contains 10,000 loan records. The `Income` field is missing in 250 records, 40 records have income values less than zero (invalid), and 15 records are duplicates. Compute the completeness, accuracy proxy, and uniqueness percentages. Comment on the validity of this dataset for credit risk modeling.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Stanley Smith Stevens (1946) proposed a classification of measurement scales based on the mathematical properties of the measurement. These scales determine the type of statistical operations that can be validly performed.

**The Four Scales (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

**1. Nominal Scale:**
Categories with no inherent order or ranking.
- *Examples:* Gender (Male, Female, Other), Blood Group, Marital Status, City.
- *Properties:* Only equality ($=$) and counting are valid.
- *Permissible Statistics:* Mode, frequencies, chi-square tests.

**2. Ordinal Scale:**
Categories with a meaningful order but unequal or unknown intervals between categories.
- *Examples:* Education (High School < Bachelor's < Master's < PhD), Likert ratings (Strongly Disagree → Strongly Agree).
- *Properties:* Equality and ordering ($<$, $>$) are valid; addition/subtraction are not.
- *Permissible Statistics:* Median, percentiles, Spearman's rank correlation.

**3. Interval Scale:**
Numeric scale where intervals between values are equal and meaningful, but there is no true (absolute) zero.
- *Examples:* Temperature in Celsius, Calendar Year, IQ scores.
- *Properties:* Addition and subtraction are valid, but ratios are meaningless (30°C is not "twice as hot" as 15°C).
- *Permissible Statistics:* Mean, standard deviation, Pearson correlation.

**4. Ratio Scale:**
Numeric scale with equal intervals AND a true absolute zero point.
- *Examples:* Height, Weight, Age, Income, Time duration, Temperature in Kelvin.
- *Properties:* All arithmetic operations including multiplication and ratios are meaningful (40 kg is twice as heavy as 20 kg).
- *Permissible Statistics:* Mean, geometric mean, harmonic mean, coefficient of variation.

**Implications of Choosing Wrong Scale (2 marks):**
- Computing the **mean of nominal labels** (e.g., averaging "Red" and "Blue" to get "Purple") produces a semantically invalid result.
- Treating **ordinal data as interval** (e.g., computing the mean Likert score of 4.3 when no such label exists) violates construct validity.
- Treating **interval as ratio** (e.g., claiming 80°C is "twice as hot" as 40°C) leads to false physical claims.
- **Ethical implication:** Wrong scale choices produce misleading visualizations and reports, breaching the *truthfulness* principle of data ethics.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Total records: $N = 10{,}000$
- Missing `Income`: $250$ records
- Invalid (negative) `Income`: $40$ records
- Duplicates: $15$ records

**Step 1 — Completeness Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\text{Completeness} = \frac{N_{\text{non-null}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{10{,}000 - 250}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = \frac{9750}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = 97.5\%$$

**Step 2 — Accuracy Proxy Calculation (2 marks):**
Since ground truth is unavailable, we use validity rate as a proxy:

$$\text{Accuracy Proxy} = \frac{N_{\text{valid}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{10{,}000 - 40}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = \frac{9960}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = 99.6\%$$

**Step 3 — Uniqueness Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\text{Uniqueness} = \frac{N_{\text{distinct}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{10{,}000 - 15}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = \frac{9985}{10{,}000} \times 100\% = 99.85\%$$

**Comment on Validity (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Score | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Completeness | 97.5% | **Borderline** — banking standards demand ≥ 99% |
| Accuracy Proxy | 99.6% | Acceptable |
| Uniqueness | 99.85% | Acceptable |

**Conclusion:** The dataset is **NOT fully valid** for credit risk modeling because completeness falls below the 99% threshold required by banking regulatory standards (e.g., RBI, Basel norms). The 250 missing income values must be addressed via imputation or record removal before the model can be ethically and legally deployed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a) [7 marks]** What is the role of attribute selection in ensuring data validity? Explain with a case study of choosing attributes for a healthcare patient monitoring system.

**(b) [7 marks]** Two machines produce bolts. Machine A produces bolts with mean diameter = 10.00 mm and standard deviation = 0.05 mm. Machine B produces bolts with mean diameter = 10.00 mm and standard deviation = 0.10 mm. Which machine is more consistent? Justify using an appropriate measure and explain why that measure is valid for this scale.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Role of Attribute Selection in Data Validity (3 marks):**

Attribute selection determines *what* is measured. If the wrong attribute is chosen, the data will have **high accuracy but low validity** — a phenomenon called the "precisely wrong" measurement problem.

Key principles:

1. **Construct Alignment:** The attribute must align with the abstract construct being studied.
2. **Operationalizability:** The attribute must be measurable in practice.
3. **Completeness of Coverage:** All relevant dimensions of the construct must be included.
4. **Non-Redundancy:** Duplicate or highly correlated attributes add noise without information.

**Case Study: Healthcare Patient Monitoring System (4 marks):**

| Construct to Measure | Good Attribute (Valid) | Poor Attribute (Invalid) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Patient Acuity | Heart Rate (Ratio, bpm) | "Severity Score" assigned subjectively | Objective vs subjective bias |
| Recovery Progress | Walking Distance (Ratio, meters) | "Feels Better" (Ordinal) | Standardized metric vs vague |
| Treatment Response | Blood Test Values (Ratio) | Doctor's Gut Feeling (Nominal) | Reproducible vs idiosyncratic |

**Justification:** Choosing `Heart Rate` (ratio scale) over `Severity Score` (ordinal) gives the doctor a continuous, standardized, internationally comparable metric. This supports better diagnosis, valid statistical analysis, and ethical patient care.

**Ethical Implication (1 mark):** Wrong attribute selection in healthcare can lead to misdiagnosis — a direct violation of the *beneficence* and *non-maleficence* principles of biomedical ethics.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Machine A: $\bar{x}_A = 10.00$ mm, $\sigma_A = 0.05$ mm
- Machine B: $\bar{x}_B = 10.00$ mm, $\sigma_B = 0.10$ mm
- Both measure **diameter** (a ratio scale, with true zero).

**Step 1 — Identify the Appropriate Measure (2 marks):**

Standard deviation alone is misleading when comparing machines, because both machines have the **same mean**. However, if we needed to compare consistency **relative to the target value**, we should use the **Coefficient of Variation (CV)**. Even when means are equal, CV normalizes variability to a dimensionless percentage, allowing direct comparison.

Since diameter is on a **ratio scale** (true zero exists), CV is a valid choice.

**Step 2 — Compute CV for Machine A (2 marks):**

$$CV_A = \frac{\sigma_A}{\bar{x}_A} \times 100\% = \frac{0.05}{10.00} \times 100\% = 0.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute CV for Machine B (2 marks):**

$$CV_B = \frac{\sigma_B}{\bar{x}_B} \times 100\% = \frac{0.10}{10.00} \times 100\% = 1.0\%$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**

$$CV_A = 0.5\% < CV_B = 1.0\%$$

**Machine A is more consistent** (has lower relative variability). Even though both machines produce bolts with the same mean diameter, Machine A's output varies by only ±0.5% from the target, while Machine B's varies by ±1.0%. For precision manufacturing, Machine A is preferred.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing scale levels:** Many students write "ordinal data can be averaged" — this is a **3-mark penalty**. Always state: *"ordinal data permits median but not mean."*
> 2. **Forgetting true zero:** Failing to distinguish interval from ratio scale costs 2-3 marks. Memorize: *"Celsius = Interval, Kelvin = Ratio."*
> 3. **Skipping justifications:** Just stating "Machine A is better" without showing the CV calculation and explaining why CV (not std dev) is valid costs 4-5 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring ethics angle:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes ethics. Failing to mention an ethical implication in the case study part loses 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Formula without units:** Writing "$\sigma = 0.05$" without specifying mm loses 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High Density Summary:**

- **Data Validity** = Data is accurate, complete, consistent, timely, and unique for its intended purpose.
- **Stevens' Four Scales** (in increasing order of power): **Nominal → Ordinal → Interval → Ratio.**
- **Nominal:** No order. Examples: Blood group, gender, city. Statistic: **Mode.**
- **Ordinal:** Ordered but unequal gaps. Examples: Likert, ranks, education. Statistic: **Median.**
- **Interval:** Equal gaps, no true zero. Examples: °C, IQ, year. Statistic: **Mean, Std Dev.**
- **Ratio:** Equal gaps + true zero. Examples: Age, salary, mass, Kelvin. Statistic: **All operations, CV, Geometric Mean.**
- **Rule of Thumb:** *"Always match your statistic to your scale."* Wrong match = Invalid analysis.
- **Five Validity Pillars:** Completeness, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness, Uniqueness.
- **Completeness Formula:** $\frac{\text{Non-null count}}{\text{Total count}} \times 100\%$
- **Accuracy Formula:** $\left(1 - \frac{\vert V_r - V_t \vert}{V_t}\right) \times 100\%$
- **CV Formula:** $\frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$ — valid only for ratio scale.
- **Attribute Selection** must ensure **Construct Validity** — the attribute truly captures the intended concept.
- **Ethical Imperative:** Invalid data leads to harmful decisions. Validity is not just statistical — it is moral.
- **Pearson Correlation** → Interval/Ratio data only.
- **Spearman Correlation** → Ordinal data.
- **Chi-Square Test** → Nominal data.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always write the scale identification step before computing any statistic — it scores easy marks and prevents calculation mistakes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Errors in Data Processing: Errors in Model Design, Algorithmic Fairness, Data Ownership
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT412 - DATA SCIENCE PRIVACY & ETHICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Errors in Data Processing: Errors in Model Design, Algorithmic Fairness, Data Ownership

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Errors in Data Processing: Errors in Model Design, Algorithmic Fairness, Data Ownership

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 2 Anchor Topic:** This module is the *heart* of the Data Science Privacy & Ethics paper because it directly connects **technical machine-learning decisions** with **legal, ethical, and social consequences**. KTU examiners routinely test definitions, the **bias-variance tradeoff**, **fairness metrics**, and the **four models of data ownership** as full 14-mark structured questions.

---

## 1.1 What are "Errors in Data Processing"?

In the context of Data Science, **errors** are not limited to computational mistakes. They represent **systematic deviations** — in data, in model design, in deployment, or in human judgment — that compromise the **utility, fairness, reliability, and ethical integrity** of a data-driven system.

A modern data pipeline looks like this:

$$\text{Raw Data} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Pre-processing} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Model Design} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Deployment} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Societal Impact}$$

An error introduced at **any** stage propagates downstream. Module 2 zooms into the three most critical ethical-technical error classes:

| Error Class | Where it occurs | What it damages |
|---|---|---|
| **Errors in Model Design** | Training / Hypothesis selection | Predictive accuracy & generalisation |
| **Algorithmic (Un)fairness** | Predictions on protected groups | Social justice & equality |
| **Data Ownership Disputes** | Data acquisition & licensing | Privacy, consent & sovereignty |

---

## 1.2 Errors in Model Design — Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):** *Errors in Model Design* refer to **structural, statistical, or inductive mistakes** made by the data scientist when **selecting features, choosing a hypothesis class, setting hyperparameters, or evaluating a model** — leading to **systematic underfitting, overfitting, biased inference, or poor out-of-sample generalisation**.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a tailor sewing a wedding suit:
- **Underfitting** = the tailor only glances at the customer and produces a generic medium-sized suit. It fits nobody perfectly.
- **Overfitting** = the tailor takes 1,000 measurements (including the customer's mood, the weather, and the taxi ride), memorises every fold, and produces a suit that fits **only that exact moment** — the customer cannot even sit down in it.
- **Good Model Design** = the tailor measures the *right* features (chest, shoulder, inseam) using a *sensible* number of samples (a few careful measurements) and produces a suit that **generalises** to similar body types.

In machine learning, the *suit* is the model, the *measurements* are features, the *training data* is the customer's body, and the *wedding* is the real-world deployment.

---

## 1.3 Algorithmic Fairness — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Algorithmic Fairness* is the **mathematical and procedural study** of whether a model's predictions are **equitable across protected demographic groups** (defined by attributes such as gender, race, caste, religion, age, or disability). A *fair* algorithm does not systematically advantage or disadvantage any group.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **college entrance exam**:
- If the exam is **fair**, two students of equal ability should have an **equal probability of selection**, regardless of which school or city they come from.
- If students from City A are **systematically selected at 60%** while equally able students from City B are selected at **only 20%**, the algorithm (or the exam) is **unfair**, even if its overall accuracy is 95%.

The 2018 ProPublica study on the **COMPAS recidivism algorithm** is the textbook KTU example — the model had equal accuracy across groups but assigned higher "risk" scores to African-American defendants, violating *demographic parity*.

---

## 1.4 Data Ownership — Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Data Ownership* refers to the **legal, economic, and ethical framework** that determines **who has the rights to control, access, use, profit from, and delete** a given piece of data. It is the foundational question of digital sovereignty in the 21st century.

### 🎯 Intuitive Analogy
Think of data as **digital body heat**:
- A thermal camera on a public street captures everyone's heat signature.
- **Who owns that image?** The person whose heat it is? The camera owner? The city that installed it? The cloud server storing it?
- Data ownership theory answers this question through **four competing models** — and the answer changes depending on whether you are in the EU, the US, or India.

---

## 1.5 Why These Three Errors are *Interlinked*

```
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │  DATA OWNERSHIP     │  ← Ethical/legal foundation
   │  (Who controls data)│
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │ (determines consent, bias in labels)
              ▼
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │  MODEL DESIGN ERROR │  ← Technical/inductive layer
   │  (How we learn)     │
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │ (propagates bias into predictions)
              ▼
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │  ALGORITHMIC        │  ← Societal consequence
   │  FAIRNESS           │
   └─────────────────────┘
```

This *interlinkage* is exactly why KTU lumps them into a single module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

---

## 2.1 Errors in Model Design — Theoretical Breakdown

Model design errors are classified along **four** dimensions. Examiners love asking students to *list and explain* them.

### 2.1.1 Bias–Variance Decomposition (★ High-Yield)

For any learned model $\hat{f}$ predicting true function $f(x)$ with noise $\varepsilon$:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; \underbrace{\left(\text{Bias}\!\left[\hat{f}(x)\right]\right)^{2}}_{\text{Systematic Error}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Var}\!\left[\hat{f}(x)\right]}_{\text{Instability}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sigma^{2}}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

Where:

$$\text{Bias}\!\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] - f(x)$$

$$\text{Var}\!\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}\!\left[\hat{f}(x)\right]\right)^{2}\right]$$

| Error Type | Bias | Variance | Real-world Symptom |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underfitting** | **High** | **Low** | Model is too simple; misses trends |
| **Overfitting** | **Low** | **High** | Model memorises noise; fails on new data |
| **Well-fit** | Moderate | Moderate | Generalises correctly |

### 2.1.2 The Four Sub-Categories of Model-Design Error

1. **Feature Engineering Errors**
   - *Feature leakage:* using a feature that is unavailable at inference time (e.g., using "post-surgery status" to predict surgery success).
   - *Irrelevant features:* curse of dimensionality increases variance.
   - *Biased features:* a feature that encodes historical discrimination (e.g., ZIP code as a proxy for race).

2. **Hypothesis Class Errors**
   - Choosing a linear model for an inherently non-linear problem → underfitting.
   - Choosing a 100-layer deep network for a 50-sample dataset → overfitting.

3. **Evaluation Errors**
   - Data leakage between train and test sets.
   - Sampling bias (e.g., training a facial recognition model on 80% lighter-skinned faces — see *Gender Shades*, Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018).
   - Wrong metric: optimising accuracy on a 99:1 imbalanced dataset while missing the minority class.

4. **Hyperparameter & Optimisation Errors**
   - Inadequate cross-validation.
   - Local minima in non-convex loss landscapes.
   - Poor learning-rate scheduling.

---

## 2.2 Algorithmic Fairness — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Three Pillars of Fairness Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners **love** asking the difference between *Group Fairness* and *Individual Fairness*. Memorise this:

#### 🅰️ Group Fairness (a.k.a. *Statistical Parity*)
The model's outcomes are **independent of the sensitive attribute** $A$.

$$P\!\left(\hat{Y} = 1 \,\vert\, A = a\right) \;=\; P\!\left(\hat{Y} = 1 \,\vert\, A = b\right) \quad \forall\, a,b \in A$$

This is **Demographic Parity**.

#### 🅱️ Equalised Odds (Hardt et al., 2016)
The model has **equal true-positive and false-positive rates across groups**:

$$P\!\left(\hat{Y} = 1 \,\vert\, A = a,\, Y = y\right) \;=\; P\!\left(\hat{Y} = 1 \,\vert\, A = b,\, Y = y\right) \quad \forall\, a,b \in A,\; y \in \{0,1\}$$

#### 🅲️ Equal Opportunity
A *relaxed* version of equalised odds — only **true-positive rates** must match:

$$P\!\left(\hat{Y} = 1 \,\vert\, A = a,\, Y = 1\right) \;=\; P\!\left(\hat{Y} = 1 \,\vert\, A = b,\, Y = 1\right)$$

### 2.2.2 The Impossibility Theorem (★ KTU Favourite)

> [!WARNING]
> **Chouldechova (2017) & Kleinberg et al. (2016)** proved that **except in degenerate cases**, it is **mathematically impossible to simultaneously satisfy** all three of:
> 1. Demographic Parity
> 2. Equalised Odds
> 3. Calibration ($P(Y=1 \vert \hat{Y}=p, A=a) = p$ for all $a$)
>
> *You must pick which fairness to prioritise.* This is a KTU 14-mark discussion question waiting to happen.

### 2.2.3 Quantitative Fairness Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Statistical Parity Difference** | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=\text{unpriv}) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=\text{priv})$ | Closer to **0** = fairer |
| **Disparate Impact Ratio** | $\dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=\text{unpriv})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=\text{priv})}$ | Value between **0.8 – 1.25** = legally fair (US EEOC "4/5 rule") |
| **Equal Opportunity Difference** | $TPR_{A=\text{unpriv}} - TPR_{A=\text{priv}}$ | Should be ≈ 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **4/5 rule** (or **80% rule**) is the most-tested legal benchmark: if the selection rate for the disadvantaged group is less than **80%** of that for the advantaged group, the algorithm is considered *disparate impact*.

### 2.2.4 Sources of Algorithmic Unfairness (ProPublica & Friedman)

1. **Skewed Sample** — training data under-represents a group.
2. **Label Bias** — historical labels themselves are biased (e.g., arrests ≠ actual crime rate).
3. **Feature Selection Bias** — proxy features for protected attributes.
4. **Optimisation Bias** — loss function ignores group errors.
5. **Deployment Context** — humans over-trust algorithmic scores.

---

## 2.3 Data Ownership — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.3.1 The Four Models of Data Ownership (★ Mandatory for KTU)

| # | Model | Owner | Key Argument | KTU-Exam Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Individualistic / Property** | The data subject (person) | "It's *my* data, just like my house" | GDPR Right to Erasure |
| 2 | **Collective / Communal** | The community / group | "Data is shared heritage — e.g., genetic data belongs to the tribe" | Indigenous data sovereignty (CARE Principles) |
| 3 | **Custodial / Fiduciary** | A trusted trustee (e.g., bank, hospital) | "I hold it for your benefit, not mine" | Doctor-patient medical records |
| 4 | **Public / State Ownership** | The state / public commons | "Aggregate data belongs to society for the public good" | Census data, traffic data |

### 2.3.2 Real-World Frameworks

- **GDPR (EU, 2018)** → Individual ownership with **six lawful bases** for processing.
- **CCPA (California, 2020)** → Opt-out & deletion rights for the consumer.
- **DPDP Act (India, 2023)** → "Data Principal" owns personal data, "Data Fiduciary" processes.
- **CARE Principles** (Indigenous data) → *Collective benefit, Authority to control, Responsibility, Ethics*.

### 2.3.3 Why Data Ownership is the *Root* of Privacy

$$\text{Privacy Violation} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Use of data without valid consent from the rightful owner}$$

If ownership is *undefined* (e.g., IoT sensor data), there can be no *meaningful* consent — and the privacy right collapses.

---

## 2.4 🧾 KTU Formula & Definition Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Symbol / Formula | One-line meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bias of estimator | $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$ | Systematic offset |
| 2 | Variance of estimator | $\mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]$ | Sensitivity to training set |
| 3 | Bias-Variance-Noise | $E[(y-\hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var} + \sigma^2$ | Total expected error |
| 4 | Demographic Parity | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=b)$ | Equal positive rates |
| 5 | Equalised Odds | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=a,Y=y)=P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=b,Y=y)$ | Equal TPR **and** FPR |
| 6 | Equal Opportunity | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=a,Y=1)=P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=b,Y=1)$ | Equal TPR only |
| 7 | Disparate Impact Ratio | $\frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert \text{unpriv})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert \text{priv})}$ | Must be ≥ 0.8 |
| 8 | Statistical Parity Diff. | $SPD = P(\hat{Y}=1\vert\text{unpriv}) - P(\hat{Y}=1\vert\text{priv})$ | Must be ≈ 0 |
| 9 | Accuracy | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| 10 | F1-Score | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall |

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section follows the **Exhaustive Content Mandate** — every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No skips.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: The Bias–Variance Tradeoff

**Goal:** Show that the *expected* squared prediction error decomposes into three meaningful terms.

**Step 1 — Start with expected squared error at a single point $x$:**

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Add and subtract the expected prediction** $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ and the true function $f(x)$:

$$= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right]$$

where $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ and $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^{2}$.

**Step 3 — Insert the identity** $\hat{f}(x) - f(x) = \big(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\big) + \big(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\big)$:

Let $A = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x) = \text{Bias}$, and $B = \hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$.

Then the squared error becomes:

$$= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(A - B + \varepsilon\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Expand using the algebraic identity** $(A - B + \varepsilon)^2 = A^2 + B^2 + \varepsilon^2 - 2AB + 2A\varepsilon - 2B\varepsilon$:

$$= A^2 + \mathbb{E}[B^2] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] \underbrace{- 2A\,\mathbb{E}[B]}_{=0} + 2A\,\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon]}_{=0} - 2\,\mathbb{E}[B\varepsilon]$$

**Step 5 — Recognise the three terms:**

- $A^2 = \big(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\big)^{2}$
- $\mathbb{E}[B^2] = \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]$ *(since $\mathbb{E}[B]=0$)*
- $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] = \sigma^{2}$
- Cross-terms vanish because $\varepsilon$ is independent of $\hat{f}$ and has zero mean.

**Step 6 — Final decomposed form:**

$$\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; \left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^{2} \;+\; \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] \;+\; \sigma^{2}\;}$$

**Engineering utility:** This decomposition is the **foundation of model selection** — the entire field of regularisation (Ridge, Lasso, Dropout, Early Stopping) is essentially different ways to *trade bias for variance* in this equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The 4/5 Rule of Disparate Impact

**Given:** A binary classifier $\hat{Y} \in \{0,1\}$, a protected attribute $A \in \{\text{priv}, \text{unpriv}\}$.

**Selection rate of group $g$:**

$$SR_g \;=\; P(\hat{Y} = 1 \vert A = g)$$

**Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR):**

$$DIR \;=\; \frac{SR_{\text{unpriv}}}{SR_{\text{priv}}}$$

**Step 1 — Express in terms of confusion-matrix counts.**

Let $N_g^+$ be the number predicted positive in group $g$ and $N_g$ be the total in group $g$:

$$SR_g \;=\; \frac{N_g^{+}}{N_g}$$

**Step 2 — Therefore:**

$$DIR \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{unpriv}}^{+}\,/\,N_{\text{unpriv}}}{N_{\text{priv}}^{+}\,/\,N_{\text{priv}}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 4/5 rule:**

$$\text{Fair} \;\iff\; 0.8 \;\le\; DIR \;\le\; 1.25$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (★ KTU-style):**

- Privileged group: 1000 applicants, 400 selected → $SR_{\text{priv}} = 0.40$
- Unprivileged group: 1000 applicants, 250 selected → $SR_{\text{unpriv}} = 0.25$

$$DIR = \frac{0.25}{0.40} = 0.625 < 0.8 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \textbf{DISPARATE IMPACT — UNFAIR}$$

**Engineering utility:** Used in **US EEOC audits** and forms the basis of algorithmic-audit lawsuits (e.g., *Griggs v. Duke Power*, 1971).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Why Demographic Parity, Equalised Odds, and Calibration Cannot All Hold

**Setup:** Let $\hat{Y}$ be a binary classifier, $Y$ the true label, $A \in \{0,1\}$ the protected attribute.

**Goal:** Show a contradiction between *equalised odds* and *demographic parity* when the **base rates** differ across groups.

**Step 1 — Assume Equalised Odds holds:**

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=0, Y=y) \;=\; P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=1, Y=y) \quad \forall\, y$$

Define the common value as $t_y$.

**Step 2 — Compute the marginal positive rate using the law of total probability:**

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=a) \;=\; \sum_{y \in \{0,1\}} P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=a, Y=y)\,P(Y=y \vert A=a)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the equalised-odds assumption:**

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \vert A=a) \;=\; t_1\,P(Y=1 \vert A=a) \;+\; t_0\,P(Y=0 \vert A=a)$$

**Step 4 — For Demographic Parity to also hold**, we need this expression to be the same for $a=0$ and $a=1$:

$$t_1\,P(Y=1 \vert A=0) + t_0\,P(Y=0 \vert A=0) \;=\; t_1\,P(Y=1 \vert A=1) + t_0\,P(Y=0 \vert A=1)$$

**Step 5 — Re-arrange:**

$$t_1\!\left[P(Y=1 \vert A=0) - P(Y=1 \vert A=1)\right] \;=\; t_0\!\left[P(Y=0 \vert A=0) - P(Y=0 \vert A=1)\right]$$

**Step 6 — The right-hand side is the *negative* of the left-hand side** (since $P(Y=0 \vert A=a) = 1 - P(Y=1 \vert A=a)$):

$$t_1 \cdot \Delta \;=\; t_0 \cdot (-\Delta) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (t_1 + t_0)\,\Delta = 0$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**

$$\Delta = P(Y=1 \vert A=0) - P(Y=1 \vert A=1) = 0 \;\;\text{or}\;\; t_0 + t_1 = 0$$

Since $t_0, t_1 \ge 0$ (they are probabilities), $t_0 + t_1 = 0$ is impossible, so we **must** have $\Delta = 0$. In plain English:

> **The base rates between groups must be identical** for demographic parity and equalised odds to co-exist. In the real world, base rates are virtually never identical (e.g., default rates differ across demographics), so **the two fairness criteria conflict**.

---

| **Fixes** | Regularisation, more data, better features | Re-weighting, fairness constraints, post-processing | Clear consent, GDPR/DPDP compliance |

| **Example** | Loan model overfits to outliers | Recidivism model biased vs. African-Americans | Smartwatch health data sold without consent |

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📝 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. Define algorithmic bias and list any four sources of unfairness in a machine-learning model.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Algorithmic bias* is a systematic and repeatable error in a computer system that creates unfair outcomes, such as privileging one arbitrary group of users over others. In machine learning, it arises when a model's predictions systematically disadvantage certain demographic groups defined by protected attributes (e.g., race, gender, age).

**Four sources (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Skewed training data** — under-representation of a subgroup.
2. **Label bias** — historical labels carry human prejudice (e.g., biased arrest records).
3. **Feature selection bias** — proxy variables for protected attributes.
4. **Optimisation bias** — loss function ignores group-level disparities.

---

### **Q2. State the four models of data ownership with one-line justification for each.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Individual Property Model** — Data belongs to the person it describes; *justification*: preserves personal autonomy (e.g., GDPR).
2. **Collective Communal Model** — Data belongs to the community; *justification*: protects shared heritage and indigenous rights (e.g., CARE Principles).
3. **Custodial/Fiduciary Model** — Data is held in trust by an institution; *justification*: requires informed handling, not ownership (e.g., hospital records).
4. **Public/State Model** — Data belongs to the public; *justification*: enables governance, transparency, and public-good research (e.g., census).

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 14-mark questions must have **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each**. They often combine a *theory* part with a *computation / case-study* part. Below are TWO independent 14-mark choices.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Errors in Model Design + Fairness*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
>
> **(a) (7 marks)** Explain the *bias-variance tradeoff* in machine-learning model design. Derive the expected squared-error decomposition and illustrate, with a diagram, how underfitting, optimal fit, and overfitting relate to model complexity.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Conceptual Introduction (2 marks):**
The bias-variance tradeoff is a central problem in supervised learning. *Bias* is the error from wrong assumptions in the learning algorithm (underfitting), while *variance* is the error from sensitivity to small fluctuations in the training set (overfitting). A good model minimises **both** while accepting an irreducible noise term.

**Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks):**

For prediction $\hat{f}(x)$ of true $f(x)$ with noise $\varepsilon$, $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon]=0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon)=\sigma^2$:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right]$$

Inserting $\hat{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] + (\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])$ and expanding gives the standard decomposition:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] = \underbrace{\left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^{2}}_{\text{systematic}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{instability}} + \underbrace{\sigma^{2}}_{\text{noise}}$$

**Step 3 — Behaviour with Model Complexity (2 marks):**

- As model complexity $\uparrow$ → **Bias** $\downarrow$ but **Variance** $\uparrow$.
- As model complexity $\downarrow$ → **Bias** $\uparrow$ but **Variance** $\downarrow$.
- The total error has a **U-shape**; the minimum is the *optimal complexity*.

**Step 4 — Diagram (to be drawn by student):** A plot with model complexity on the x-axis, and three curves: Bias² (decreasing), Variance (increasing), Total Error (U-shaped), with a shaded *irreducible noise* floor.

---

> **(b) (7 marks)** A bank's loan-approval classifier is evaluated on two demographic groups. The privileged group (PG) has 2,000 applicants with 1,000 approvals. The unprivileged group (UG) has 1,000 applicants with 300 approvals. Calculate the *Disparate Impact Ratio*. Is the model fair under the **4/5 rule**? Suggest TWO mitigation strategies if it is unfair.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Selection Rates (2 marks):**

$$SR_{PG} = \frac{1000}{2000} = 0.50$$

$$SR_{UG} = \frac{300}{1000} = 0.30$$

**Step 2 — Compute Disparate Impact Ratio (2 marks):**

$$DIR = \frac{SR_{UG}}{SR_{PG}} = \frac{0.30}{0.50} = 0.60$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 4/5 rule (1 mark):**

$$DIR = 0.60 < 0.80 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \textbf{MODEL IS UNFAIR (Disparate Impact Detected)}$$

**Step 4 — Mitigation Strategies (2 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Re-weighting / Re-sampling** — Increase the weight of UG samples during training to balance outcomes.
2. **Fairness-constrained optimisation** — Add a regularisation term that penalises disparity in positive prediction rates across groups.
3. *(Alternative)* Post-processing threshold adjustment per group; or remove proxy features correlated with the protected attribute.

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Data Ownership + Errors*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
>
> **(a) (7 marks)** Discuss in detail the **four models of data ownership**. With suitable examples, explain how each model allocates the *rights to control, access, profit, and delete* data. Mention one real-world statute that aligns with each.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Data ownership is the legal, economic, and ethical determination of who controls a piece of data. The answer varies by jurisdiction, technology, and cultural context, giving rise to **four competing models**.

**Model 1 — Individual Property Model (1.5 marks):**
- Allocates **all** rights (control, access, profit, delete) to the individual.
- Justification: personal autonomy, dignity, and labour theory of value (your data is *your* work).
- **Example:** EU's **GDPR (2018)** and India's **DPDP Act (2023)** grant the *Data Principal* the rights of access, correction, erasure, and portability.
- *Pitfall:* ignores collective interests and is hard to enforce against large platforms.

**Model 2 — Collective Communal Model (1.5 marks):**
- Rights vest in a community or group, especially for data that has shared or ancestral meaning.
- Justification: certain data (genetic, cultural, linguistic) cannot be reduced to a single person's property.
- **Example:** **CARE Principles** for Indigenous Data Governance (*Collective benefit, Authority to control, Responsibility, Ethics*); the *Māori* data sovereignty movement in New Zealand.
- *Pitfall:* hard to define "community" in global data flows.

**Model 3 — Custodial / Fiduciary Model (1.5 marks):**
- A trusted institution (bank, hospital, university) *holds* the data on behalf of the subject — analogous to a legal trustee.
- Justification: subjects cannot practically manage sensitive data, so a fiduciary with a duty of care holds it.
- **Example:** Hospital records held by a hospital under doctor-patient confidentiality; the *HIPAA Privacy Rule* in the US.
- *Pitfall:* fiduciary abuse (e.g., Facebook–Cambridge Analytica).

**Model 4 — Public / State Ownership Model (1.5 marks):**
- Data is held by the state as a public good; individuals have transparency & access rights but not exclusive ownership.
- Justification: aggregate data fuels public policy, scientific research, and democratic accountability.
- **Example:** **Indian Census** data; the US *Freedom of Information Act (FOIA)*; Open Government Data portals.
- *Pitfall:* surveillance risk and concentration of state power.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** In practice, modern data-protection law is a **hybrid** of these four models — e.g., GDPR fuses *individual* and *fiduciary* models, while India's DPDP adds a *state regulatory* overlay.

---

> **(b) (7 marks)** A hospital deploys a deep-learning model to predict 30-day readmission risk. List **five** possible *errors in model design* that could compromise the model, and explain how each can be detected.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Introduction (½ mark):** Model design errors in healthcare AI can be life-threatening. Five specific errors are:

1. **Feature Leakage (1.25 marks):** A feature unavailable at prediction time (e.g., *number of medications prescribed after discharge*) is used. *Detection:* perform *temporal train-test split*; inspect *permutation importance*; remove features computed *after* the prediction timestamp.

2. **Sampling Bias (1.25 marks):** Training data over-represents urban, insured, English-speaking patients. *Detection:* compute *subgroup performance metrics* (accuracy, recall per demographic); use a *fairness dashboard*; compare feature distributions across subgroups.

3. **Inappropriate Hypothesis Class (1.25 marks):** A 50-million-parameter deep network on 500 patient records will memorise. *Detection:* plot *learning curves*; if training error is far below validation error → overfitting; if both are high → underfitting; use the *bias-variance decomposition* $E = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var} + \sigma^2$.
