4. **Wrong Evaluation Metric (1.25 marks):** Optimising 99%-accuracy when only 1% of patients are readmitted. *Detection:* report *precision, recall, F1, PR-AUC*; never rely on accuracy for *imbalanced* outcomes; use *calibration plots* and *Brier score*.

5. **Insufficient Cross-Validation (1.25 marks):** Single train-test split gives a noisy estimate. *Detection:* apply *stratified k-fold CV* (k=5 or 10); for time-series clinical data, use *forward-chaining CV*; report *confidence intervals* of the metric.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Each error has a *signature* in either the bias-variance plot, the confusion matrix, or the subgroup analysis. Routine *model cards* and *datasheets for datasets* (Gebru et al., 2018) institutionalise these checks.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. ❌ **Writing formulas without units / context.** Always state *what* the formula measures (e.g., "DIR measures the ratio of selection rates between unprivileged and privileged groups").
> 2. ❌ **Confusing *bias* in statistics with *bias* in ethics.** Model bias ≠ algorithmic bias. They are different concepts sharing a name. *State this distinction explicitly* in answers.
> 3. ❌ **Forgetting the bias-variance-noise decomposition has THREE terms.** Students often write only two (Bias² and Variance) and miss the *irreducible noise* $\sigma^2$ — a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. ❌ **Treating data ownership as a "legal" question only.** KTU wants the *ethical* lens too: communal model is rooted in indigenous ethics, not just statute law.
> 5. ❌ **Not naming the statute for each ownership model.** Always anchor with a real example (GDPR, DPDP, HIPAA, FOIA, CARE).
> 6. ❌ **Skipping diagrams.** A labelled *U-shaped bias-variance curve* or a *fairness-pillars diagram* earns easy 1–2 marks and demonstrates understanding.
> 7. ❌ **Mishandling the 4/5 rule.** The threshold is **0.8 ≤ DIR ≤ 1.25**, not just "greater than 0.8". Memorise both bounds.
> 8. ❌ **Forgetting the *Impossibility Theorem* of fairness.** This is the *highest-order* learning outcome from this module — a single sentence citing it transforms a 7-mark answer into a 10-mark one.

---

## 5.4 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Use this list in the last 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Errors in Data Processing** = systematic deviations across the pipeline (data, model, deployment, society).
- [x] **Model Design Errors** = statistical/inductive mistakes in feature selection, hypothesis class, evaluation, and optimisation.
- [x] **Bias-Variance Decomposition:** $E = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var} + \sigma^2$ — **three** terms, *not two*.
- [x] **Underfitting** ↔ High Bias, Low Variance. **Overfitting** ↔ Low Bias, High Variance.
- [x] **Algorithmic Fairness** = mathematical study of equitable predictions across protected groups.
- [x] **Group Fairness pillars:** Demographic Parity, Equalised Odds, Equal Opportunity.
- [x] **Individual Fairness:** "Similar individuals → similar predictions" (Dwork et al., 2012).
- [x] **Causal Fairness:** Counterfactual fairness — outcome unchanged if protected attribute were flipped.
- [x] **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** = $SR_{\text{unpriv}} / SR_{\text{priv}}$; fair if **0.8 ≤ DIR ≤ 1.25** (4/5 rule).
- [x] **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** = $SR_{\text{unpriv}} - SR_{\text{priv}}$; fair if **≈ 0**.
- [x] **Impossibility Theorem:** Demographic Parity + Equalised Odds + Calibration **cannot all hold** unless base rates are equal.
- [x] **Data Ownership — Four Models:** Individual Property, Collective Communal, Custodial Fiduciary, Public State.
- [x] **Real-world anchors:** GDPR (Individual), CARE Principles (Communal), HIPAA (Fiduciary), FOIA/Census (Public).
- [x] **Indian context:** **DPDP Act 2023** — "Data Principal" owns, "Data Fiduciary" processes.
- [x] **Classic case studies:** COMPAS (ProPublica, 2016), Gender Shades (Buolamwini & Gebru, 2018), Cambridge Analytica (2018).
- [x] **Mitigation toolkit:** pre-processing (re-weighting, sampling), in-processing (fairness constraints), post-processing (threshold adjustment).
- [x] **Documentation tools:** Model Cards (Mitchell et al., 2019), Datasheets for Datasets (Gebru et al., 2018).
- [x] **The three error classes are interlinked:** Ownership → Design → Fairness → Societal harm. Always draw this pipeline.
- [x] **Be precise with terminology:** *algorithmic bias* ≠ *statistical bias* ≠ *cognitive bias*.
- [x] **In 14-mark answers:** always close with a *real-world example* and a *one-line recommendation* to examiners.

---

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** Module 2 questions in the 2024 scheme are **application-oriented** — expect a *case study* (loan, hiring, healthcare, recidivism) and *at least one fairness computation*. Memorise the DIR formula, the 4/5 rule, and the four ownership models. They appear in **every** university exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Integrity: Biased and Unbiased Data, Fairness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT412 - DATA SCIENCE PRIVACY & ETHICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Integrity: Biased and Unbiased Data, Fairness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Integrity

**Data Integrity** is the foundational pillar of ethical data science. It refers to the *accuracy, consistency, completeness, and trustworthiness* of data throughout its lifecycle — from collection, storage, processing, to analysis and decision-making. In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, data integrity is mapped to **CO2 (Understand the principles of ethical data handling)** and is a prerequisite for any fair AI/Data Science pipeline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (ISO 8000 Standard aligned):** Data Integrity is the property of data wherein it remains unaltered, accurate, and consistent across its operational lifecycle, ensuring that the information is fit for its intended purpose and decision-making context.

**Four Pillars of Data Integrity:**
1. **Accuracy** — Data correctly represents the real-world entity/event.
2. **Consistency** — Same data yields the same result across systems.
3. **Completeness** — No critical values are missing.
4. **Validity** — Data conforms to defined formats, types, and rules.

---

### 1.2 Biased Data

**Biased Data** refers to datasets that contain *systematic skews* — non-random, structured distortions that cause the data to misrepresent the true population, leading to discriminatory, inaccurate, or ethically compromised outcomes when used to train Machine Learning (ML) models.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** Biased data is a dataset in which the underlying distribution $P_{\text{data}}(X, Y)$ deviates from the true population distribution $P_{\text{true}}(X, Y)$ in a non-random, systematic manner, often correlated with sensitive attributes such as gender, race, age, caste, or socioeconomic status.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Broken Weighing Scale:**
Imagine a vegetable shop owner uses a weighing scale that always shows **200 grams extra** for tomatoes purchased from Farmer A (his friend) and the *true* weight for Farmer B. Every time he calculates price, he systematically over-charges for Farmer B's produce. The scale is "accurate" but "biased". Similarly, a dataset can look clean and valid (no missing values) but contain a *systematic skew* that misleads every downstream model trained on it.

---

### 1.3 Unbiased Data

**Unbiased Data** is data that is *representative* of the true underlying population, where the statistical distribution of features and labels is not systematically distorted, and the dataset does not encode discrimination against any subgroup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unbiased Data Condition (Mathematical Form):** A dataset $\mathcal{D}$ is said to be unbiased with respect to a protected attribute $A$ if:
> $$P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a') \quad \forall a, a' \in A$$
> where $\hat{Y}$ is the model outcome and $A$ is the sensitive/protected attribute (e.g., gender, caste).

**Intuitive Analogy — The Representative Survey:**
A good opinion poll samples voters *proportionally* from every district, age group, gender, and economic class. An unbiased dataset works the same way: it represents *every subgroup* of the real population in proportion to their actual occurrence. If women are 50% of the population, they must constitute approximately 50% of the dataset.

---

### 1.4 Fairness

**Fairness in Data Science** is the ethical and mathematical principle that algorithmic decisions must not systematically discriminate against individuals or groups based on protected attributes (race, gender, religion, caste, age, disability, etc.). Fairness ensures **equitable treatment, opportunity, and outcome distribution** across all demographic groups.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Fairness in ML/Data Science is the absence of prejudice or favoritism toward an individual or group based on their inherent or acquired protected characteristics. It encompasses both *group fairness* (parity across groups) and *individual fairness* (similar treatment for similar individuals).

**Intuitive Analogy — The Referee of a Football Match:**
A fair referee does not favor either team. Two players committing the *same* foul must receive the *same* card, regardless of their team, jersey number, or popularity. A "fair" ML model is the referee: equal rules, equal treatment, and equal scrutiny for every input individual.

---

### 1.5 The Interconnection: Data Integrity → Bias → Fairness

The relationship is **causal and sequential**:

$$\text{Poor Data Integrity} \longrightarrow \text{Bias in Data} \longrightarrow \text{Unfair Model} \longrightarrow \text{Discriminatory Decisions}$$

Ensuring data integrity is the *first* line of defense against unfairness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Bias — A Structured Taxonomy

Biases in datasets are not random — they emerge from human, systemic, statistical, and computational sources. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following bias categories:

#### A. Data Collection Biases
- **Selection Bias:** Data is collected non-randomly; certain groups are over/under-represented.
  - *Example:* A medical AI trained only on patients from urban hospitals fails on rural populations.
- **Sampling Bias:** The sampling method (e.g., convenience sampling, voluntary response) skews the dataset.
- **Coverage Bias:** Certain segments of the population are systematically excluded from data collection.
- **Participation Bias (Non-response Bias):** Willing respondents differ systematically from non-respondents.

#### B. Measurement & Labeling Biases
- **Measurement Bias:** Features are measured inconsistently across groups (e.g., faulty sensors in one region).
- **Label Bias (Historical Bias):** The ground-truth labels themselves encode historical discrimination.
  - *Example:* A hiring dataset from the 1980s labeled "successful" candidates predominantly as male.
- **Observer Bias / Annotation Bias:** Human annotators inject their own prejudices during labeling.

#### C. Algorithmic & Model Biases
- **Algorithmic Bias:** The model architecture or optimization objective itself amplifies existing data bias.
- **Aggregation Bias:** A single global model is forced on diverse sub-populations.
- **Confirmation Bias:** Modelers interpret results to confirm pre-existing beliefs.

#### D. Societal & Systemic Biases
- **Historical Bias:** Pre-existing societal inequities embedded in the data generation process.
- **Representation Bias:** Underrepresentation of minority groups in datasets.
- **Evaluation Bias:** Benchmarks and test sets over-represent majority groups.

---

### 2.2 Fairness Definitions — Mathematical Formulations

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats fairness as a *multi-dimensional* concept. No single definition fits all scenarios, and the choice depends on the application context.

| Fairness Type | Formal Definition | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Group Fairness (Statistical Parity / Demographic Parity)** | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)$ | Positive outcome rate is equal across groups. |
| **Equal Opportunity (True Positive Rate Parity)** | $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=1)$ | Qualified individuals have equal chance of being correctly selected. |
| **Predictive Parity (Precision Parity)** | $P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=0) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=1)$ | Among those predicted positive, equal accuracy across groups. |
| **Equalized Odds** | Both TPR **and** FPR equal across groups | Equal true and false positive rates for all groups. |
| **Counterfactual Fairness** | $\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a}(X) = \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'}(X)$ | Decision unchanged if protected attribute were flipped. |
| **Individual Fairness** | $d(\hat{Y}(x_i), \hat{Y}(x_j)) \leq L \cdot d(x_i, x_j)$ | Similar individuals receive similar outcomes. |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all high-yield formulas** for solving Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions on Bias and Fairness. Memorize this section thoroughly.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** | $SPD = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)$ | $\hat{Y}$: prediction, $A$: protected attribute |
| 2 | **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** | $DIR = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{unprivileged})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{privileged})}$ | Acceptable range: $0.8 \leq DIR \leq 1.25$ (the **80% Rule** / *four-fifths rule*) |
| 3 | **Equal Opportunity Difference (EOD)** | $EOD = TPR_{A=1} - TPR_{A=0}$ | $TPR$ = True Positive Rate |
| 4 | **Average Odds Difference (AOD)** | $AOD = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[(FPR_{A=1} - FPR_{A=0}) + (TPR_{A=1} - TPR_{A=0})\right]$ | Average of TPR & FPR gaps |
| 5 | **Theil Index (Inequality)** | $T = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{\hat{y}_i}{\bar{\hat{y}}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\hat{y}_i}{\bar{\hat{y}}}\right)$ | Measures inequality in outcomes; $T=0$ is perfect fairness |
| 6 | **KL Divergence (Bias Detection)** | $D_{KL}(P \mid\mid Q) = \sum_{x} P(x) \log\!\left(\dfrac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\right)$ | Measures divergence between two distributions; $D_{KL}=0 \Leftrightarrow P=Q$ |
| 7 | **Shannon Entropy (Information Content)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Higher entropy $\Rightarrow$ less predictable (often correlated with diversity) |
| 8 | **Sampling Bias Ratio (SBR)** | $SBR = \dfrac{p_{\text{sample}}(a_i)}{p_{\text{population}}(a_i)}$ | $SBR = 1$: balanced; $SBR \neq 1$: biased |
| 9 | **Statistical Parity (binary form)** | $P(\hat{Y}=1, A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=1, A=1)$ | Joint probability equality |
| 10 | **Fairness through Unawareness (FTU)** | $\hat{Y} = f(X)$ where $A \notin X$ | Naive — but easily broken by proxy features |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bias and fairness framework is **not abstract philosophy** — it has direct, mission-critical engineering applications:

- **Healthcare AI:** Diagnostic models trained on biased demographic data can misdiagnose diseases in minority populations (e.g., dermatology AI failing on darker skin tones).
- **Credit Scoring & Loan Approval:** RBI and global regulators (EU AI Act, 2024) mandate fairness audits before deploying any credit ML model.
- **Criminal Justice (COMPAS Recidivism):** A real-world KTU case study — ProPublica's analysis showed COMPAS was biased against African-American defendants.
- **HR & Hiring Automation:** Amazon scrapped an AI recruiting tool (2018) because it was biased against women.
- **Generative AI & LLMs:** Bias in training corpora leads to stereotyping and toxic outputs in ChatGPT-style models.
- **Kerala State Use-Case:** Fairness in the *Kerala K-Smart* digital governance and public grievance systems requires group-fair AI to ensure equitable service delivery across districts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The 80% Rule (Disparate Impact Threshold)

The **80% Rule** (also called the *four-fifths rule*) is a legally binding fairness threshold used in U.S. employment law (Uniform Guidelines on Employee Selection Procedures, 1978) and adopted by KTU's ethics module as a standard fairness benchmark.

**Step 1 — Start with the Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR):**

$$DIR = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{unprivileged})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{privileged})}$$

**Step 2 — Assume the privileged group is hired at rate $r_p$ and the unprivileged group at rate $r_u$:**

$$DIR = \dfrac{r_u}{r_p}$$

**Step 3 — The "four-fifths" rule states that the selection rate for any group must be at least 80% of the rate for the group with the highest selection rate:**

$$r_u \geq 0.8 \cdot r_p$$

**Step 4 — Substituting into the DIR formula:**

$$\dfrac{r_u}{r_p} \geq 0.8$$

**Step 5 — Therefore, the acceptable fairness band is:**

$$0.8 \leq DIR \leq 1.25$$

If $DIR < 0.8$, the unprivileged group is *disparately impacted* (under-selected). If $DIR > 1.25$, the unprivileged group is *disparately favored* (over-selected). Both extremes indicate unfairness.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: From Bias to Unfairness — A Numerical Example

**Problem Setup:** A bank deploys a loan-approval ML model on a dataset of 10,000 applicants, split equally between two groups: $A=0$ (Group X) and $A=1$ (Group Y).

**Given Data:**

| Group | Applied | Approved ($\hat{Y}=1$) | Actually Repaid ($Y=1$) | Actually Defaulted ($Y=0$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A=0$ (Group X) | 5,000 | 3,000 | 2,400 | 600 |
| $A=1$ (Group Y) | 5,000 | 1,500 | 1,200 | 300 |

**Step 1 — Calculate the approval rate for each group:**

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) = \dfrac{3000}{5000} = 0.60$$

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1) = \dfrac{1500}{5000} = 0.30$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Statistical Parity Difference (SPD):**

$$SPD = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)$$

$$SPD = 0.60 - 0.30 = 0.30$$

Since $SPD \neq 0$, **statistical parity is violated**.

**Step 3 — Calculate the Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR):**

$$DIR = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.60} = 0.50$$

Since $DIR = 0.50 < 0.80$, the model **violates the 80% rule**. Group Y is being approved at only 50% of the rate of Group X — a *clear* case of disparate impact.

**Step 4 — Calculate the True Positive Rate (TPR) for each group:**

$$TPR_{A=0} = \dfrac{TP_{A=0}}{\text{Actual Positives}_{A=0}} = \dfrac{2400}{2400+600} = 0.80$$

$$TPR_{A=1} = \dfrac{TP_{A=1}}{\text{Actual Positives}_{A=1}} = \dfrac{1200}{1200+300} = 0.80$$

Here $TPR$ is equal, so **Equal Opportunity holds**, but **Demographic Parity fails**. This illustrates a critical KTU point: *no single fairness metric can be satisfied in all situations; trade-offs are unavoidable.*

**Step 5 — Conclude with the fairness trade-off:**

This numerical example demonstrates the famous **Fairness Impossibility Theorem** (Chouldechova, 2017; Kleinberg et al., 2016): *when the base rates of two groups differ, it is mathematically impossible to simultaneously satisfy all three fairness criteria — Demographic Parity, Equal Opportunity, and Predictive Parity — unless the model is perfect.*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bias Detection Workflow

| Phase | Action | Tool / Metric | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Audit** | Identify protected attributes | Dataset schema review | List of $A_i$ |
| **2. Quantify Representation** | Compare $p_{\text{sample}}(A)$ vs $p_{\text{pop}}(A)$ | Sampling Bias Ratio (SBR) | Bias report |
| **3. Measure Outcome Disparity** | Compare $\hat{Y}$ rates across $A$ | SPD, DIR, EOD | Disparity score |
| **4. Pre-process** | Rebalance, reweight, resample | SMOTE, Reweighing | Balanced $\mathcal{D}'$ |
| **5. In-process** | Add fairness constraints to loss | Adversarial debiasing, Equalized Odds loss | Fair model $\hat{f}$ |
| **6. Post-process** | Adjust thresholds per group | Calibrated equalized odds | Calibrated $\hat{Y}'$ |
| **7. Validate** | Repeat Steps 1–6 on test set | All metrics | Certified model |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bias Taxonomy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Data Bias Sources] --> node2[Collection Bias]
    node1 --> node3[Measurement Bias]
    node1 --> node4[Algorithmic Bias]
    node1 --> node5[Societal Bias]

    node2 --> node2a[Selection Bias]
    node2 --> node2b[Sampling Bias]
    node2 --> node2c[Coverage Bias]
    node2 --> node2d[Non-Response Bias]

    node3 --> node3a[Measurement Error]
    node3 --> node3b[Label Bias]
    node3 --> node3c[Annotation Bias]

    node4 --> node4a[Optimization Bias]
    node4 --> node4b[Aggregation Bias]
    node4 --> node4c[Confirmation Bias]

    node5 --> node5a[Historical Bias]
    node5 --> node5b[Representation Bias]
    node5 --> node5c[Evaluation Bias]

    node1 --> node6[Impacts Data Integrity]
    node6 --> node7[Leads to Unfair Model]
    node7 --> node8[Discriminatory Decisions]

    style node1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style node8 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.2 Fairness-Aware ML Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Data D] --> B[Data Integrity Audit]
    B --> C{Bias Detected?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Pre-Processing Mitigation]
    C -- No --> E[Model Training]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Trained Model f-hat]
    F --> G[Fairness Evaluation]
    G --> H{Fairness Threshold Met?}
    H -- No --> I[In-Processing / Post-Processing]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Deployment]
    J --> K[Continuous Monitoring]
    K --> B

    style A fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC
    style B fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF9900
    style D fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#CC0000
    style E fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900
    style G fill:#F0E6FF,stroke:#6600CC
    style J fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:3px
    style K fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#999900
```

---

### 4.3 Fairness Trade-off Diagram (Three-Way Impossibility)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Demographic Parity] --> nodeX[F1 Metric]
    nodeB[Equal Opportunity] --> nodeX
    nodeC[Predictive Parity] --> nodeX
    nodeX --> nodeD{F1 Equal to 100 percent?}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[All three satisfied]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF[At most two can be satisfied]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Engineer must choose the right fairness notion for the domain]

    style nodeX fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeG fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram visually represents the **Fairness Impossibility Theorem**. When a model is imperfect (and all real-world models are), no more than two of the three group-fairness criteria can be simultaneously satisfied.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Biased Data" with a suitable real-world example.**

**Model Answer:**

Biased data is a dataset in which the underlying distribution deviates systematically from the true population distribution, often correlated with protected attributes like gender, race, or socioeconomic status.

*Example:* The Amazon AI recruiting tool (2018) was trained on résumés submitted to Amazon over 10 years, predominantly from male candidates (tech industry historical bias). The model learned to *down-rank résumés containing the word "women's"* (e.g., "women's chess club captain"), thereby encoding and amplifying gender bias.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for example, 1 for explanation of bias mechanism.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between "Group Fairness" and "Individual Fairness".**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Group Fairness | Individual Fairness |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit of analysis** | Demographic groups | Individual persons |
| **Requirement** | Equal statistical outcomes across groups | Similar individuals receive similar treatment |
| **Metric example** | Statistical Parity, Equal Opportunity | Lipschitz condition: $d(\hat{Y}_i, \hat{Y}_j) \leq L \cdot d(x_i, x_j)$ |
| **Limitation** | May mask intra-group inequality | Requires a meaningful distance metric $d(\cdot,\cdot)$ |

*(3 marks: 1 for each definition + 1 for contrast.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **types of biases** that can affect a dataset used for training a Machine Learning model. Provide at least one example for each category.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hiring ML model is evaluated on two demographic groups. For Group A (privileged), the selection rate is 0.70. For Group B (unprivileged), the selection rate is 0.45. **Calculate the Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR) and comment on the fairness of this model using the 80% rule. Suggest TWO pre-processing mitigation strategies.**

---

##### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Definition:** Bias in data refers to systematic errors or skews in the dataset that cause the model to learn and perpetuate unfair patterns.

**1. Selection Bias (2 marks):**
Data is collected non-randomly; certain groups are over/under-represented.
*Example:* A face-recognition model trained primarily on light-skinned faces fails on darker-skinned faces (NIST 2019 study).

**2. Sampling Bias (1 mark):**
The sampling method skews the dataset.
*Example:* Online survey data over-represents tech-savvy youth.

**3. Label/Historical Bias (2 marks):**
Ground-truth labels encode past discrimination.
*Example:* A recidivism dataset labeled "high-risk" predominantly for one racial group.

**4. Measurement Bias (1 mark):**
Inconsistent feature measurement across groups.
*Example:* A medical sensor calibrated for adults under-reads pediatric data.

**5. Algorithmic Bias (1 mark):**
The model itself amplifies data bias.
*Example:* Word embeddings associating "programmer" with "male".

---

##### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Step 1 — Write the DIR formula:**

$$DIR = \dfrac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{unprivileged})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=\text{privileged})}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$DIR = \dfrac{0.45}{0.70} = 0.643$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the 80% rule:**

The acceptable range is $0.8 \leq DIR \leq 1.25$. Since $DIR = 0.643 < 0.80$, the model **violates the 80% rule** and exhibits **disparate impact** against Group B.

**Step 4 — Comment:**

Group B is being selected at only 64.3% of Group A's rate. This is a **legally and ethically significant** fairness violation. Approximately 1 in 3 qualified candidates from Group B is being unfairly rejected.

**Step 5 — Two Pre-Processing Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **Reweighing:** Assign sample weights to training instances so that the protected attribute becomes statistically independent of the label, balancing the importance of each subgroup's examples.

2. **Resampling (Oversampling/Undersampling):** Either oversample Group B (using SMOTE — Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) or undersample Group A to achieve demographic parity in the training set.

3. *(Optional, bonus)* **Disparate Impact Remover:** A feature-transformation technique that edits feature values to remove correlation with the protected attribute while preserving rank-order within groups.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Data Integrity** and explain its four pillars. How does poor data integrity lead to biased data? Use a real-world case study.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With suitable formulas, explain any THREE mathematical definitions of fairness used in Machine Learning. Discuss the **Fairness Impossibility Theorem** and its practical implications for a data scientist deploying a loan-approval model.

---

##### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Definition (2 marks):**
Data Integrity refers to the accuracy, consistency, completeness, and validity of data across its entire lifecycle. It is a foundational requirement for trustworthy AI systems.

**Four Pillars (3 marks):**
1. **Accuracy** — Data correctly reflects the real-world state.
2. **Consistency** — Identical inputs yield identical outputs across systems.
3. **Completeness** — No critical values are missing.
4. **Validity** — Data conforms to defined schemas, formats, and types.

**Poor Data Integrity → Bias (2 marks):**
When data is inaccurate, inconsistent, or incomplete, the dataset's distribution drifts away from the true population. This drift is *systematic*, not random, and encodes biases that are then learned by ML models. For instance, if a hospital's patient record system consistently fails to record tribal-community visits (completeness failure), the resulting dataset is biased against tribal health needs.

**Case Study:** The **UK A-level Algorithm Controversy (2020):** The Ofqual algorithm used inconsistent historical grade distributions to standardize results, leading to systematic down-grading of students from state schools (where data integrity was lower due to inconsistent teacher assessment), unfairly favoring private-school students. The model was scrapped within days.

---

##### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Three Fairness Definitions (4 marks):**

1. **Demographic Parity (Statistical Parity):**
$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)$$
The model's positive prediction rate is equal across protected groups. Suitable for: hiring, ad-targeting, college admissions.

2. **Equal Opportunity:**
$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=1)$$
Among those who *should* succeed (Y=1), the model correctly approves them at equal rates across groups. Suitable for: loan approval, college admissions.

3. **Predictive Parity:**
$$P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=0) = P(Y=1 \mid \hat{Y}=1, A=1)$$
Among those predicted positive, the precision is equal across groups. Suitable for: medical diagnosis, criminal recidivism.

**Fairness Impossibility Theorem (2 marks):**
The theorem (Chouldechova 2017; Kleinberg, Mullainathan & Raghavan 2016) proves that **no non-trivial classifier can simultaneously satisfy all three of the above criteria** when the base rates of the protected groups differ (which is almost always). This is a *mathematical* result, not an engineering limitation.

**Practical Implication for a Loan-Approval Data Scientist (1 mark):**
A data scientist must make an **explicit ethical choice** about *which* fairness notion aligns with the application's values. For loan approval, Equal Opportunity is often preferred (qualified applicants should not be denied based on group membership). The choice must be **documented, justified, and audited** as part of model governance under the EU AI Act, RBI guidelines, and KTU 2024 OBE ethics framework.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Bias & Fairness Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Bias with Variance:** Bias here means *systematic skew*, not the statistical bias-variance tradeoff. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for confusing these.
> 2. **Forgetting units & threshold:** In DIR calculations, students often forget the *80% rule threshold*. Always state the comparison.
> 3. **Single fairness metric trap:** Never claim a model is "fair" based on one metric. Always mention the Fairness Impossibility Theorem and the trade-off.
> 4. **No real-world example:** KTU 2024 mandates application-oriented answers. Pure definitions without an example lose 1 mark minimum.
> 5. **Writing $\hat{Y}$ as $Y$:** Predictions $\hat{Y}$ ≠ actual labels $Y$. Mixing them in fairness formulas leads to incorrect expressions and full mark deduction.
> 6. **Skipping the "Why":** When listing bias types, explain *how* they affect outcomes, not just the names.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Memorize This Block:**

- ☐ **Data Integrity =** Accuracy + Consistency + Completeness + Validity (4 pillars).
- ☐ **Biased Data** = Systematic (not random) deviation from true population distribution.
- ☐ **Unbiased Data** = Representative; Sampling Bias Ratio $SBR = 1$ for all groups.
- ☐ **Fairness =** Absence of discrimination based on protected attributes ($A$).
- ☐ **Group Fairness** = Equal outcome rates across groups (SPD ≈ 0).
- ☐ **Individual Fairness** = Similar inputs → similar outputs (Lipschitz condition).
- ☐ **Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR)** = $\frac{r_u}{r_p}$; Acceptable range: $[0.8, 1.25]$.
- ☐ **Statistical Parity Difference (SPD)** = $P(\hat{Y}=1\mid A=1) - P(\hat{Y}=1\mid A=0)$; ideal = 0.
- ☐ **80% Rule (Four-Fifths Rule)** = A group must be selected at ≥ 80% of the highest rate.
- ☐ **Equal Opportunity** = Equal TPR across groups: $TPR_{A=0} = TPR_{A=1}$.
- ☐ **Predictive Parity** = Equal precision across groups.
- ☐ **Fairness Impossibility Theorem** = Cannot satisfy all 3 fairness criteria simultaneously if base rates differ.
- ☐ **Bias Types to Remember** = Selection, Sampling, Label, Measurement, Historical, Algorithmic, Aggregation.
- ☐ **Mitigation Stages** = Pre-processing (Reweighing, SMOTE) → In-processing (Adversarial Debiasing) → Post-processing (Threshold Adjustment).
- ☐ **Real-World Cases** = Amazon Hiring AI (2018), COMPAS Recidivism, UK A-Levels 2020, Apple Card 2019 Gender Bias.
- ☐ **Golden Rule:** No model is *inherently* fair — fairness is a *design choice* aligned with the application's ethical priorities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Five C's of Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT412 - DATA SCIENCE PRIVACY & ETHICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Five C's of Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Five C's of Data** is a structured, lifecycle-based framework that classifies the essential phases through which data must pass to deliver reliable, ethical, and actionable value in any data science initiative. The framework decomposes the data pipeline into five interdependent pillars: **Collection, Cleaning, Curation, Consumption, and Culture**. Each "C" represents a distinct governance and processing stage that determines whether raw signals can be transformed into trusted intelligence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The Five C's are a foundational taxonomy used to evaluate the readiness, quality, governance, and ethical footprint of data within an organization. They form the prerequisite vocabulary for topics like data ownership, privacy controls, consent, and bias mitigation that follow later in the module.

### 1.2 The Five C's — At a Glance

| # | Phase | Core Question It Answers |
|---|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Collection** | *Where does the data come from, and how is it captured?* |
| 2 | **Cleaning** | *Is the data accurate, complete, and free of errors?* |
| 3 | **Curation** | *Is the data discoverable, documented, and preserved?* |
| 4 | **Consumption** | *Who uses the data, and for what decisions?* |
| 5 | **Culture** | *Does the organization value and govern data ethically?* |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a **professional restaurant** producing a meal for a customer:

- **Collection** is the **grocery shopping** — the chef goes to the market, the farm, and the fishery to gather raw ingredients (data sources: sensors, surveys, logs).
- **Cleaning** is the **washing, peeling, and sorting** — rotten tomatoes are thrown out, dirt is scrubbed off, and ingredients are inspected for quality.
- **Curation** is the **organizing the pantry** — labeling jars, storing them at the right temperature, dating them, so the right ingredient is always findable later.
- **Consumption** is the **actual cooking and serving** — the chef uses the prepared ingredients to make the dish (analytics, ML models, dashboards) that the customer eats (decisions).
- **Culture** is the **head chef's philosophy and the kitchen's rules** — the hygiene standards, the taste philosophy, the discipline of every cook. Without it, even the best ingredients produce an inedible or unsafe meal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Lock:** Just as a single rotten ingredient can ruin a dish, a single poorly governed C (e.g., dirty data, missing consent) can corrupt the entire downstream data product. The Five C's are a *chain of custody* — its strength equals its weakest link.

### 1.4 Why the Framework Matters for Privacy & Ethics

In the context of **PEADT412**, the Five C's are not merely operational steps — they are **ethical checkpoints**:

- *Collection* raises consent and proportionality issues.
- *Cleaning* raises questions about silent alteration of evidence.
- *Curation* raises retention and access control issues.
- *Consumption* raises fairness, bias, and re-identification risks.
- *Culture* is the meta-layer where ethics, governance, and accountability are institutionalized.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Memory Trick:** Remember the order using the phrase **"C³ C²"** — *Collection, Cleaning, Curation, Consumption, Culture*. The first three C's (C³) are the **preparation** stage; the last two C's (C²) are the **delivery** stage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each "C"

#### **C1 — Collection**

- **Definition:** The systematic acquisition of raw data from identified sources through legitimate, consent-driven mechanisms.
- **Sources:** Sensors, IoT devices, transactional databases, social media APIs, web scraping, surveys, third-party data brokers, public datasets.
- **Key Sub-Activities:**
  - Source identification and authentication
  - Consent capture (notice & purpose limitation)
  - Schema definition and metadata tagging
  - Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity assessment
  - **PII (Personally Identifiable Information)** flagging
- **Ethical Concern:** Surveillance creep, scope creep, dark patterns in consent forms, collection without lawful basis.

#### **C2 — Cleaning**

- **Definition:** The detection, correction, removal, or imputation of erroneous, incomplete, duplicated, or inconsistent records.
- **Key Sub-Activities:**
  - Missing value treatment (mean, median, mode, KNN imputation, deletion)
  - Outlier detection (Z-score, IQR, Isolation Forest)
  - Deduplication
  - Type conversion and unit normalization
  - Standardization of formats (dates, encodings, casing)
  - Validation against business rules
- **Ethical Concern:** Altering data can erase evidence of bias; cleaning must be **reproducible and auditable**, not a hidden pre-processing step.

#### **C3 — Curation**

- **Definition:** The long-term stewardship of data — organizing, documenting, preserving, and governing it so it remains **FAIR** (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable).
- **Key Sub-Activities:**
  - Cataloging and metadata enrichment
  - Versioning and lineage tracking
  - Retention scheduling and secure archival
  - Access control lists (ACLs) and role-based access
  - Data licensing and reuse terms
  - Linkage to provenance records
- **Ethical Concern:** Premature deletion erases accountability; indefinite retention violates the *right to erasure* (e.g., GDPR Art. 17).

#### **C4 — Consumption**

- **Definition:** The application of curated data to derive insights, train models, generate reports, or drive automated decisions.
- **Key Sub-Activities:**
  - Descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics
  - Machine learning model training and inference
  - Dashboarding and KPI reporting
  - Sharing with internal/external stakeholders
  - Monetization or third-party sharing
- **Ethical Concern:** Re-identification attacks, discriminatory outputs, model opacity (black boxes), purpose limitation violations.

#### **C5 — Culture**

- **Definition:** The organizational values, governance structures, incentives, training, and leadership behaviors that shape how data is treated as an asset and a responsibility.
- **Key Sub-Activities:**
  - Data ethics committees and review boards
  - Data stewardship roles (CDO, data stewards, data custodians)
  - Training and literacy programs
  - Internal policies (acceptable use, breach reporting)
  - Whistle-blower protection
- **Ethical Concern:** Without culture, even the best tools and policies are bypassed; culture is the **institutional immune system** against data misuse.

### 2.2 High-Yield Relationship Map

| Relationship | Explanation |
|--------------|-------------|
| Collection → Cleaning | Garbage In → Garbage Out; cleaning cannot fix fundamental absence of consent. |
| Cleaning → Curation | A clean dataset is a prerequisite for trusted curation. |
| Curation → Consumption | Undocumented data cannot be safely consumed. |
| Consumption → Culture | Repeated misuse shapes (or corrupts) culture. |
| Culture → Collection | Culture dictates *what is permissible to collect in the first place*. |
| **Cycle** | The Five C's are **iterative** — consumption outcomes feed back into collection redesign. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Quick Reference Table

| # | Phase | One-Word Essence | Ethical Watchword | Key Deliverable |
|---|-------|------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Collection | Gathering | Consent | Raw dataset with provenance |
| 2 | Cleaning | Refining | Integrity | Validated, de-duplicated table |
| 3 | Curation | Preserving | Retention | FAIR, catalogued data product |
| 4 | Consumption | Acting | Fairness | Insight / model / decision |
| 5 | Culture | Believing | Accountability | Governed, ethical data org |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No mathematical formulas apply to this conceptual topic.** The "examinable rigor" here is in (a) ordering, (b) phase identification given a scenario, and (c) ethical mapping. Students must NOT expect a numerical derivation in the university exam for this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

Because the Five C's of Data is a **conceptual governance framework**, the "derivation" here is a **logical, scenario-based decomposition** rather than a mathematical one. The KTU 2024 examiner tests this topic through **scenario mapping** — given a real-world situation, the student must identify which C is at risk and what ethical principle is violated.

### 3.1 Analytical Decision Procedure — "The Five C Diagnostic"

**Step 1 — Read the scenario carefully and extract the *actor* and the *action*.**
The actor is the entity performing the action (company, government, researcher). The action is the verb (collected, sold, deleted, trained on, ignored).

**Step 2 — Match the action to one of the five phases using the diagnostic table below.**

$$
\text{Diagnostic Function:} \quad f(\text{action}) = \begin{cases}
\text{Collection} & \text{if action} \in \{\text{gather, scrape, capture, record}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Cleaning} & \text{if action} \in \{\text{filter, impute, normalize, deduplicate}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Curation} & \text{if action} \in \{\text{archive, document, version, license}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Consumption} & \text{if action} \in \{\text{analyze, train, report, share, sell}\} \\[4pt]
\text{Culture} & \text{if action} \in \{\text{policy, train staff, appoint CDO, audit}\}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Map the ethical concern to the corresponding principle.**

$$
\text{Principle} = g(\text{Phase}) = \begin{cases}
\text{Consent \& Lawfulness} & \text{Collection} \\[4pt]
\text{Integrity \& Reproducibility} & \text{Cleaning} \\[4pt]
\text{Retention \& Erasure} & \text{Curation} \\[4pt]
\text{Fairness \& Purpose Limitation} & \text{Consumption} \\[4pt]
\text{Accountability \& Governance} & \text{Culture}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — State the remediation in one sentence.**
Every ethical concern must be matched with a corrective measure (e.g., "obtain fresh consent," "publish cleaning audit log," "enforce retention schedule," "run fairness audit," "establish data ethics board").

### 3.2 Worked Scenario Walkthroughs

**Scenario A — A hospital shares anonymized patient records with a pharmaceutical startup.**

- **Step 1:** Actor = Hospital. Action = "shares ... records with a startup."
- **Step 2:** The action is *sharing for analytical use* → maps to **Consumption**.
- **Step 3:** Ethical principle = **Fairness & Purpose Limitation**. Re-identification risk via quasi-identifiers (age, ZIP, diagnosis date) is high.
- **Step 4:** Remediation → Apply differential privacy, conduct a re-identification risk assessment, obtain a Data Processing Agreement, and ensure the purpose of use matches the original patient consent.

**Scenario B — A bank erases all loan application data after 30 days to "protect privacy."**

- **Step 1:** Actor = Bank. Action = "erases ... data after 30 days."
- **Step 2:** The action is *deleting/organizing data* → maps to **Curation** (specifically, retention scheduling).
- **Step 3:** Ethical principle = **Retention & Erasure**. Premature deletion violates accountability and anti-discrimination laws (regulators cannot investigate bias complaints).
- **Step 4:** Remediation → Apply a *risk-tiered* retention schedule: minimum required by regulators (e.g., 7 years for financial records) with secure deletion thereafter.

**Scenario C — An analytics team replaces missing income values with the column mean without documenting the step.**

- **Step 1:** Actor = Analytics team. Action = "replaces ... without documenting."
- **Step 2:** The action is *imputation* → maps to **Cleaning**.
- **Step 3:** Ethical principle = **Integrity & Reproducibility**. Mean imputation can systematically bias downstream models and hide data gaps.
- **Step 4:** Remediation → Maintain an immutable cleaning log, declare imputation method in model cards, and assess downstream fairness impact.

### 3.3 The Iterative Loop

The Five C's are not a waterfall — they form a closed loop governed by Culture:

$$
\text{Collection} \rightarrow \text{Cleaning} \rightarrow \text{Curation} \rightarrow \text{Consumption} \rightarrow \text{Insight} \rightarrow \text{Culture} \rightarrow \text{Collection (refined)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examinable Insight:** The arrow from *Consumption* back to *Collection* is the **feedback loop** in continuous learning systems. Without this loop, organizations repeat the same data mistakes at scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Five C's Lifecycle with Ethical Checkpoints

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Organizational Need]) --> A
    A[/C1 Collection/]:::col --> B[/C2 Cleaning/]:::clean
    B --> C[/C3 Curation/]:::cur
    C --> D[/C4 Consumption/]:::con
    D --> E[/C5 Culture/]:::cul
    E --> F([Insight, Decisions, ML Models])
    F -.feedback.-> A
    A -.consent gate.-> A1[Lawful basis, Notice, Purpose]
    B -.integrity gate.-> B1[Audit log, Reproducibility]
    C -.retention gate.-> C1[FAIR, Right to Erasure, ACLs]
    D -.fairness gate.-> D1[Bias audit, Purpose limitation]
    E -.accountability gate.-> E1[Ethics board, CDO, Training]

    classDef col fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    classDef clean fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#8a6d00,color:#000
    classDef cur fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f6f1f,color:#000
    classDef con fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#7a1f1f,color:#000
    classDef cul fill:#e6d6ff,stroke:#4b1f7a,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Diagnostic Map for Exam Scenarios

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Read Scenario]) --> T{Identify Primary Action Verb}
    T -->|Gather, Scrape, Record| P1[Phase: Collection]
    T -->|Filter, Impute, Normalize| P2[Phase: Cleaning]
    T -->|Archive, Document, Version| P3[Phase: Curation]
    T -->|Analyze, Share, Train, Sell| P4[Phase: Consumption]
    T -->|Policy, Appoint, Train Staff| P5[Phase: Culture]
    P1 --> R1[Ethical Risk: Lack of Consent]
    P2 --> R2[Ethical Risk: Hidden Data Alteration]
    P3 --> R3[Ethical Risk: Retention Mismanagement]
    P4 --> R4[Ethical Risk: Bias and Re-identification]
    P5 --> R5[Ethical Risk: Governance Vacuum]
    R1 --> X[Propose Remediation]
    R2 --> X
    R3 --> X
    R4 --> X
    X([Final KTU-Style Answer])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — C³ C² Grouping (High-Yield Visual Memory Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PREP[Preparation Stage - The First Three C's]
        direction LR
        K1[Collection] --> K2[Cleaning] --> K3[Curation]
    end
    subgraph DEL[Delivery Stage - The Last Two C's]
        direction LR
        K4[Consumption] --> K5[Culture]
    end
    PREP --> DEL
    PREP -.- note1[Touches data before value is extracted]
    DEL -.- note2[Touches people and decisions]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
Define the **Five C's of Data** and state the *single ethical concern* most closely associated with each phase.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Five C's of Data is a lifecycle framework comprising:

1. **Collection** — gathering raw data from sources; ethical concern: **lawful consent and purpose limitation**.
2. **Cleaning** — correcting errors and handling missing values; ethical concern: **integrity and auditability** of the cleaning process.
3. **Curation** — preserving and documenting data for long-term reuse; ethical concern: **balanced retention vs. right to erasure**.
4. **Consumption** — using data for analysis and decisions; ethical concern: **fairness, bias, and purpose limitation**.
5. **Culture** — institutional values around data; ethical concern: **accountability and governance**.

*(1 mark for correct listing of all five phases, 1 mark for phase-ethics pairing, 1 mark for clarity.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
Why is **Culture** considered the *meta-phase* in the Five C's framework? Give two examples of organizational cultural practices that strengthen data ethics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Culture is the meta-phase because it does not act on data directly — it **governs how the other four C's are executed**. It sets the rules, incentives, and leadership tone that determine whether collection is consensual, cleaning is transparent, curation is fair, and consumption is ethical. **(1 mark)**

Two examples of cultural practices: **(2 marks)**
- Establishment of a **Data Ethics Review Board** with authority to reject non-compliant projects.
- Mandatory **data ethics training and certification** for every data-handling employee, with periodic re-certification.

---

### **Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> The most common reason students lose marks on the Five C's is *memorizing the list but failing to apply it*. A 14-mark question on this topic is **always a scenario-based application**, not a definition. If your answer merely lists the five C's, you will score at most **4–5 marks**. To score full marks, you must: (a) identify which C is breached in the scenario, (b) name the specific ethical principle violated, (c) propose a concrete remediation, and (d) explain how the loop feeds back into Culture. **Do not skip the remediation step.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample End-Semester Question, Module 2]**

*(a)* A large e-commerce company scrapes social media posts to infer the political leanings of users and uses this to micro-target them with political advertisements during election season. The company states in its privacy policy that it "may use publicly available data for personalization." Identify the **specific phase(s) of the Five C's** that are ethically compromised. For each compromised phase, name the principle violated and propose a remediation. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain how the breach in part (a) is fundamentally a failure of the **Culture** phase, even though the surface-level problem appears at Collection and Consumption. How could a strong data culture have prevented this scenario? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Phase 1 — Collection (3 marks):**
- **Principle violated:** *Consent and Purpose Limitation.* The act of inferring sensitive political attributes from public posts is a form of **secondary use** that goes beyond what users implicitly consented to when posting publicly. "Publicly available" ≠ "consent to commercial political profiling."
- **Remediation:** Implement a **purpose-limitation check** at ingestion. Disallow collection of inferred sensitive attributes (political opinion, religion, health) without explicit opt-in consent. Conduct a **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** before any new sensitive-attribute collection.

**Phase 2 — Consumption (4 marks):**
- **Principle violated:** *Fairness, Non-discrimination, and Purpose Limitation.* Micro-targeting political ads based on inferred attributes can manipulate voters, create filter bubbles, and is prohibited in many jurisdictions (e.g., EU Digital Services Act, Indian IT Rules 2021).
- **Remediation:**
  1. **Prohibition** of sensitive-attribute-based ad targeting for political/election content.
  2. **Algorithmic audit** by an independent third party before deployment.
  3. **Transparency labels** on every political ad: who paid, who was targeted, why.
  4. **Right to opt-out** of profiling with one click.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

This is fundamentally a **Culture failure** because the technical safeguards (consent banners, opt-out toggles) are downstream of leadership decisions. **(1 mark)**

Three cultural failures enabled this: **(6 marks)**

1. **No ethical north star:** The leadership treated privacy policy as a *legal shield* rather than a *value statement*. A healthy data culture would have a publicly stated ethical stance: *"We do not profile users for political purposes, period."*
2. **No veto power for ethics:** The product team could ship a politically manipulative feature without review. A strong culture has a **Data Ethics Board with binding veto power** over high-risk launches.
3. **Misaligned incentives:** Engineers and PMs were rewarded for engagement metrics, not for ethical outcomes. A strong culture ties bonuses to *ethical KPIs* (e.g., complaint rate, opt-out rate, fairness audit results).

**Conclusion (implicit):** The Five C's are only as strong as the Culture phase that governs them. Technical fixes are necessary but insufficient — institutional culture is the *immune system* that prevents unethical data practices from being designed in the first place.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample End-Semester Question, Module 2]**

*(a)* A machine learning team at a fintech firm notices that their loan-approval model has a 30% higher false-rejection rate for female applicants. Investigation reveals the training data was missing income information for 40% of self-employed women, which the team silently imputed with the global median income. Identify the **Cleaning and Curation phase failures**, and explain why the cleaning choice caused a fairness violation. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Design a **Culture-level intervention** that would have prevented this scenario from occurring. Your answer should specify the role, the policy, and the feedback mechanism. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Cleaning Phase Failure (4 marks):**
- The team used **global median imputation** to fill missing income for a demographically non-representative subgroup (self-employed women). Median imputation systematically **shrinks the imputed group's income toward the population center**, understating high earners and overstating low earners, but in this case the net effect was downward bias.
- The cleaning was **undocumented and unreproducible** — a violation of data integrity principles. There was no audit log, no model card disclosure, and no demographic-stratified evaluation.
- **Ethical principle violated:** *Integrity, Reproducibility, and Fairness.* Cleaning must never silently mask structural data gaps that reflect real-world inequities.

**Curation Phase Failure (3 marks):**
- The dataset was not catalogued with **lineage metadata** indicating the source of the missingness. A properly curated dataset would have flagged the 40% missingness as a *data quality alert* before it reached modeling.
- **Ethical principle violated:** *Documentation and Accountability.* Good curation ensures that downstream consumers understand the limitations of the data, including who is under-represented and why.

**Why this caused a fairness violation:** Because imputed values are *fabricated* values that are not distinguishable from real ones, the model treats a systematically under-estimated group as if their (under-estimated) income were true, leading to lower credit limits, higher rejections, and a self-reinforcing bias loop. **Bias in the data, amplified by careless cleaning, becomes bias in the model, which becomes bias in society.**

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

A complete Culture-level intervention has **three components:**

**1. The Role — Data Steward for High-Risk Pipelines (2 marks):**
Every ML pipeline that consumes personal data must have a named **Data Steward** with formal authority. The Data Steward signs off on the *Data Sheet* and the *Model Card*. In this scenario, a Data Steward would have reviewed the cleaning step before the dataset moved to the modeling team.

**2. The Policy — Mandatory Disaggregated Evaluation (3 marks):**
The firm must adopt a policy that **all models touching individuals are evaluated for performance disaggregated by sex, age, geography, and other protected attributes**. The pre-deployment gate is: *no model ships if any subgroup's error rate exceeds 1.25× the best group's error rate (the "four-fifths rule" heuristic).* In this scenario, the 30% gap would have been caught at this gate.

**3. The Feedback Mechanism — Continuous Post-Deployment Monitoring (2 marks):**
Culture is not a one-time audit. The firm must implement **monthly fairness dashboards** that monitor real-world rejection rates by demographic group, with automatic re-training triggers if drift exceeds thresholds. A **whistle-blower channel** must exist for engineers to flag ethical concerns without retaliation.

**Conclusion:** Culture converts *individual good intentions* into *systemic safeguards*. The role gives authority, the policy gives a standard, and the feedback mechanism gives accountability — together they make the Five C's an operational reality, not a poster on the wall.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- 🔑 The **Five C's** are: **Collection, Cleaning, Curation, Consumption, Culture** — in that strict order.
- 🔑 Use the memory aid **"C³ C²"** — first three are *Preparation*; last two are *Delivery*.
- 🔑 Each C has a **unique ethical watchword**: *Consent, Integrity, Retention, Fairness, Accountability.*
- 🔑 The Five C's form a **closed loop with feedback** to Collection — it is not a waterfall.
- 🔑 **Culture is the meta-phase** — it governs the execution of the other four.
- 🔑 In KTU 2024 exams, questions are **scenario-based** — never just list the C's without applying them.
- 🔑 A complete answer always includes: **Phase → Principle violated → Concrete remediation → Cultural lesson.**
- 🔑 Remember the diagnostic verbs: *gather/record → Collection; impute/normalize → Cleaning; archive/version → Curation; analyze/share → Consumption; policy/appoint → Culture.*
- 🔑 The framework is **iterative**, not one-shot — a single insight can trigger re-collection.
- 🔑 Connect this topic to later Module 2 themes: *consent, anonymization, fairness, accountability, governance* — examiners love cross-linking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anonymization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT412 - DATA SCIENCE PRIVACY & ETHICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Anonymization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anonymization** is the irreversible process of transforming personal data in such a way that the data subject can no longer be identified, directly or indirectly, by any means reasonably likely to be used by the data controller or by any other person, while preserving the analytical utility of the dataset.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, anonymization is classified as a **Privacy-Enhancing Technology (PET)** that falls under the broader discipline of **Statistical Disclosure Control (SDC)**. It operates by severing the link between an individual (the *data subject*) and their corresponding record in a released dataset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Regulatory Distinction (GDPR Article 4(5) & Recital 26)**
> Once data is truly anonymized, it falls **outside the scope of data protection laws** (such as the GDPR or the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 of India). This is the central reason anonymization is the most coveted privacy technique in modern data science.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Masked Ball

Imagine a grand charity ball where every guest (a *data subject*) donates a sealed medical file to a public health researcher. To prevent embarrassment, the organizers do the following:

1. **Remove the name tag** from each file (this is *identifier removal*).
2. **Replace the exact age "27" with "20s"** and the exact pincode "682011" with "682***" (this is *generalization*).
3. **Group people with similar features** so the researcher cannot tell who has what (this is *k-anonymity*).

The researcher can still study broad patterns ("40% of people in the 30s from Kerala have a vitamin D deficiency"), but cannot walk up to a specific guest and say, *"You have diabetes."* The guest's **identity is structurally hidden** within a crowd of similar-looking faces.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Classes of Attributes in a Dataset**

| Attribute Class | Definition | Examples | Action in Anonymization |
|-----------------|------------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Direct Identifiers** | Uniquely identify an individual on their own | Name, Aadhaar, SSN, Email, Phone | **Removed or hashed** |
| **Quasi-Identifiers (QI)** | Attributes that, when combined, can re-identify a person | Age, ZIP, Gender, DOB, Education | **Generalized or suppressed** |
| **Sensitive Attributes** | The actual data we want to study, but never link to a person | Disease, Salary, Religion, Vote | **Retained unaltered** (utility preserved) |

## 1.4 Anonymization vs. Pseudonymization vs. De-identification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This distinction is a **favourite KTU Part A question** (3-mark definition).

| Property | Anonymization | Pseudonymization | De-identification |
|----------|---------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Reversibility** | **Irreversible** | Reversible (via key/token) | Sometimes reversible |
| **Linkability to person** | Broken permanently | Temporarily obscured | Obscured |
| **GDPR status** | Exempt from law | **Still personal data** | **Still personal data** |
| **Risk of re-identification** | Minimal (theoretically negligible) | High if key leaks | High |
| **Method example** | k-anonymity, Differential Privacy | Tokenization, Hashing | Masking initials |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Threat Model: Why Anonymization is Non-Trivial

The **naïve belief** is that simply removing the *Name* column makes a dataset anonymous. The **Governor Latimer case (1997)** and the **Sweeney re-identification of the Massachusetts Governor (1997)** proved otherwise. Using only *ZIP + DOB + Gender* (three quasi-identifiers), **87% of Americans could be uniquely identified** in any de-identified medical dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Insight:** Quasi-identifiers are the *Achilles heel* of anonymization. Modern anonymization models exist to **handle QI leakage**, not direct identifiers.

## 2.2 The k-Anonymity Model (Samarati & Sweeney, 1998)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Board-Exam Standard):** A dataset $D$ satisfies **k-anonymity** if, for every combination of quasi-identifier values, at least $k$ records share that combination. In other words, every record is *indistinguishable* from at least $k-1$ other records with respect to the quasi-identifiers.

### 2.2.1 Formal Logical Steps

- **Step 1 — Define the QI-set:** Let $Q = \{Q_1, Q_2, \dots, Q_a\}$ be the set of quasi-identifiers in $D$.
- **Step 2 — Define an Equivalence Class (EC):** $EC(Q) = \{r \in D : r[Q_i] = r'[Q_i] \text{ for all } i\}$.
- **Step 3 — Apply the k-condition:** $\forall EC, \; \vert EC \vert \geq k$.
- **Step 4 — Re-identification probability:** Since an adversary knows only the QI values, the probability of singling out any one record is bounded by $1/k$.

### 2.2.2 The Two Principal Mechanisms to Achieve k-Anonymity

- **Generalization (Hierarchical):** Replace precise values with broader categorical ranges. *Example: Age 28 → Age [20–30], ZIP 682011 → 682***.*
- **Suppression (Tuple-level):** Replace specific cells or entire records with a wildcard character `*`. *Example: ZIP 682011 → *.*

## 2.3 Beyond k-Anonymity: l-Diversity and t-Closeness

### 2.3.1 l-Diversity (Machanavajjhala et al., 2007)

The **homogeneity attack** breaks k-anonymity: if all $k$ records in an EC share the *same* sensitive value (e.g., all have "Cancer"), the privacy guarantee is illusory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A dataset satisfies **l-diversity** if every equivalence class contains at least $l$ "well-represented" values for the sensitive attribute. The standard *entropy l-diversity* condition is:
> $$\text{Entropy}(EC) = -\sum_{s \in S} p(s \mid EC) \cdot \log_2 p(s \mid EC) \geq \log_2 l$$

### 2.3.2 t-Closeness (Li et al., 2007)

The **similarity attack** breaks l-diversity: even if values are diverse, they may be semantically close (e.g., "Stomach Ulcer" and "Gastric Cancer").

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A dataset satisfies **t-closeness** if, for every equivalence class $EC$, the distance between the distribution of the sensitive attribute within $EC$ and its distribution in the entire dataset $D$ is at most $t$. The standard distance metric is the **Earth Mover's Distance (EMD)**.

## 2.4 Differential Privacy (Dwork, 2006) — The Gold Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A randomized algorithm $M$ satisfies **$\varepsilon$-differential privacy** if, for any two adjacent datasets $D$ and $D'$ that differ in exactly one record, and for any output subset $S$:
> $$\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]$$

- $\varepsilon$ is the **privacy budget** (smaller = stronger privacy).
- The bound holds **regardless of the adversary's prior knowledge**, making it the strongest provable guarantee.

### 2.4.1 The Laplace Mechanism

For a numerical query $f$, the **global sensitivity** is:
$$\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert$$

The Laplace mechanism releases $M(D) = f(D) + \eta$, where $\eta \sim \text{Laplace}(0, b)$ and:
$$b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every semester.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Key Variables & Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | k-Anonymity Re-ID Risk | $\Pr[\text{Re-ID}] = \dfrac{1}{k}$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ (group size) |
| 2 | Entropy l-Diversity | $-\sum p(s) \log_2 p(s) \geq \log_2 l$ | $l$ = well-represented values |
| 3 | Differential Privacy Bound | $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]$ | $\varepsilon$ = privacy budget (nats) |
| 4 | Laplace Scale Parameter | $b = \dfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$ | $b$ = noise scale |
| 5 | Global Sensitivity | $\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert$ | Scalar quantity |
| 6 | Information Loss (IL) | $IL = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot a} \sum_{j=1}^{a} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{h_{ij}}{H_j}$ | $n$=rows, $a$=QI, $h$ = gen. level, $H$ = max level |
| 7 | Discernibility Metric (DM) | $DM = \sum_{EC} \vert EC \vert^2$ | Lower = better utility |
| 8 | Composition Rule | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i} \varepsilon_i$ | Sequential queries |
| 9 | Data Utility | $U = 1 - IL$ | Bounded in $[0, 1]$ |
| 10 | Record Suppression Rate | $R_s = \dfrac{\text{Suppressed records}}{n}$ | Dimensionless ratio |

## 2.6 The Privacy–Utility Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Law of Anonymization (Board Worthy)**
> $$\uparrow k, \uparrow l, \uparrow \varepsilon^{-1} \;\; \Longrightarrow \;\; \uparrow \text{Privacy} \;\;\text{BUT}\;\; \downarrow \text{Utility}$$
> A *higher* $k$ provides more privacy but *destroys* analytical value. Choosing the right $k$ is an engineering decision.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Anonymization

- **Healthcare:** Hospitals share patient datasets with research institutions (e.g., MIMIC-III, NHANES) for epidemiological studies.
- **Census Data:** The U.S. Census Bureau uses k-anonymity to release demographic tables.
- **Apple & Google:** Both use differential privacy (with $\varepsilon \approx 1$ to $10$) to collect emoji usage statistics and typing data.
- **Telecommunications:** Call Detail Records (CDRs) are anonymized before being shared with traffic-engineering planners.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Worked Example: From Raw Data to 2-Anonymity

Consider a micro-dataset of 6 patients released by a clinic:

| Name | Age | ZIP | Gender | Disease |
|------|-----|-----|--------|---------|
| Alice | 25 | 682011 | F | Flu |
| Bob | 27 | 682012 | M | Fever |
| Carol | 35 | 682023 | F | Diabetes |
| Dave | 36 | 682024 | M | Diabetes |
| Eve | 42 | 682035 | F | Asthma |
| Frank | 43 | 682036 | M | Cancer |

The clinic wants to publish this with $k = 2$ for QI = {Age, ZIP, Gender}.

### Step 1 — Identify Attribute Classes

Direct Identifier: *Name* (remove it entirely).
Quasi-Identifiers: *Age, ZIP, Gender*.
Sensitive Attribute: *Disease* (retain for medical research).

### Step 2 — Choose the Generalization Hierarchies

For Age:
- Level 0: exact (e.g., 25)
- Level 1: decade (20s, 30s, 40s)
- Level 2: range (20–40, 40–60)

For ZIP:
- Level 0: full 6 digits
- Level 1: first 3 digits (682***)
- Level 2: first 2 digits (68****)

For Gender: *Binary*, so only Level 0 (exact) or Level 1 (suppressed `*`).

### Step 3 — Apply Minimal Generalization for k = 2

Generalize Age to decade and ZIP to first 3 digits. The transformed dataset is:

| Age | ZIP | Gender | Disease |
|-----|-----|--------|---------|
| 20s | 682*** | F | Flu |
| 20s | 682*** | M | Fever |
| 30s | 682*** | F | Diabetes |
| 30s | 682*** | M | Diabetes |
| 40s | 682*** | F | Asthma |
| 40s | 682*** | M | Cancer |

### Step 4 — Form Equivalence Classes (ECs) and Validate

| EC | QI Signature | Size |
|----|--------------|------|
| $EC_1$ | (20s, 682***, F/M) | **2** (Alice, Bob) |
| $EC_2$ | (30s, 682***, F/M) | **2** (Carol, Dave) |
| $EC_3$ | (40s, 682***, F/M) | **2** (Eve, Frank) |

All ECs have size $\vert EC \vert = 2 \geq k = 2$. **2-Anonymity is satisfied.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Re-identification Probability

In the anonymized table, the adversary sees the QI signature (e.g., 20s, 682***, F) and knows the person is Alice. The probability that Alice is correctly singled out is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[\text{Re-ID} \mid \text{Alice}] &= \frac{1}{\vert EC_1 \vert} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \\
&= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A random guess among the two candidates is the best the adversary can do, *unless* they possess background knowledge that breaks the tie. This bound is the foundation of k-anonymity's guarantee.

## 3.3 Derivation: Information Loss (IL) Metric

> [!NOTE]
> The Information Loss (IL) metric quantifies the *utility cost* of generalization. Lower is better.

For our example, compute $h_{ij}$ (generalization level used) and $H_j$ (max possible level):

| Attribute $j$ | Used level $h$ | Max level $H$ | $h/H$ |
|---------------|----------------|----------------|--------|
| Age | 1 (decade) | 2 (range) | $1/2$ |
| ZIP | 1 (3-digit) | 2 (2-digit) | $1/2$ |
| Gender | 0 (exact) | 1 (suppressed) | $0/1$ |

With $n = 6$ records and $a = 3$ QI attributes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IL &= \frac{1}{n \cdot a} \sum_{j=1}^{a} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{h_{ij}}{H_j} \\
&= \frac{1}{6 \cdot 3} \times \left[ 6 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + 6 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + 6 \cdot \frac{0}{1} \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{18} \times (3 + 3 + 0) \\
&= \frac{6}{18} \\
&= 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $IL = 0.333$ (i.e., $\approx 33.3\%$ information loss). Data utility $U = 1 - 0.333 = 0.667$.

## 3.4 Derivation: The Homogeneity Attack Against k-Anonymity

Suppose we *strengthen* $k$ to 4 by over-generalizing. New table:

| Age | ZIP | Gender | Disease |
|-----|-----|--------|---------|
| 20–40 | 68**** | * | Flu |
| 20–40 | 68**** | * | Fever |
| 20–40 | 68**** | * | Diabetes |
| 20–40 | 68**** | * | Diabetes |
| 40–60 | 68**** | * | Asthma |
| 40–60 | 68**** | * | Cancer |

For $EC_1 = $ {(20–40, 68****, *)}, the **entropy l-diversity check** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p(\text{Flu}) &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \\
p(\text{Fever}) &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \\
p(\text{Diabetes}) &= \frac{2}{4} = 0.50 \\
H(EC_1) &= -\left( 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.50 \log_2 0.50 \right) \\
&= -[(-0.5) + (-0.5) + (-0.5)] \\
&= 1.5 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $l$-diversity with $l = 2$:

$$
\log_2 l = \log_2 2 = 1.0
$$

Since $1.5 \geq 1.0$, the condition holds — but **only barely**. A dataset with $EC = \{$Diabetes, Diabetes, Diabetes, Diabetes$\}$ would have $H = 0$, **failing** l-diversity catastrophically. This is precisely the homogeneity attack.

## 3.5 Derivation: Laplace Mechanism for Differential Privacy

Suppose a hospital wants to release the *count* of diabetic patients: $f(D) = $ count.

- Real count: $f(D) = 42$.
- Adding/removing one record changes the count by at most $1$, so $\Delta f = 1$.
- Privacy budget chosen: $\varepsilon = 0.5$.

The Laplace scale parameter is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.5} \\
&= 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

The released value is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(D) &= f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Laplace}(0, 2.0) \\
\Pr[M(D) \in S] &\leq e^{0.5} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S] \\
&\approx 1.649 \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]
\end{aligned}
$$

A simulated release might be $M(D) = 44$ (a true count of 42 plus noise of +2). The adversary cannot tell if the real count was 42, 43, 44, or 45 — the *adjacent-dataset* privacy bound guarantees this for *any* background knowledge.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | k-Anonymity | l-Diversity | t-Closeness | Differential Privacy |
|----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Guards against linkage** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Guards against homogeneity** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Guards against similarity** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Guards against background knowledge** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Provable guarantee** | Heuristic | Heuristic | Heuristic | Mathematical |
| **Year proposed** | 1998 | 2007 | 2007 | 2006 |
| **Practical use** | Census, EMR | Healthcare | Healthcare | Apple, Google, US Census |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Anonymization Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flow diagram depicts the *full functional pipeline* of a k-anonymization system, from raw ingestion to safe release.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset D] --> B[Schema Inspection]
    B --> C[Classify Attributes]
    C --> D[Direct Identifiers e.g. Name SSN Aadhaar]
    C --> E[Quasi Identifiers e.g. Age ZIP Gender]
    C --> F[Sensitive Attributes e.g. Disease Salary]
    D --> G[Hashing or Removal Module]
    E --> H[Generalization Engine]
    E --> I[Suppression Engine]
    H --> J[Anonymized Dataset D Prime]
    I --> J
    G --> J
    F --> J
    J --> K[Validation Module k Anonymity Check]
    K --> L{All EC Size greater than or equal to k}
    L -->|Yes| M[Safe to Release]
    L -->|No| N[Increase Generalization or Suppress Tuples]
    N --> H
    N --> I
    M --> O[Utility Assessment IL and DM]
    O --> P{Utility Acceptable}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Publish]
    P -->|No| R[Adjust k or relax privacy]
    R --> H
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Anonymization Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anonymization Techniques] --> B[Syntactic Models]
    A --> C[Probabilistic Models]
    A --> D[Algorithmic Models]
    B --> B1[k Anonymity]
    B --> B2[l Diversity]
    B --> B3[t Closeness]
    C --> C1[Differential Privacy]
    C --> C2[Randomized Response]
    D --> D1[Microaggregation]
    D --> D2[Data Swapping]
    D --> D3[Perturbation Noise Injection]
```

## 4.3 Attack Model Schematic (Adversary's View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Adv[Adversary] --> AK[Background Knowledge]
    AK --> Pub[Public Anonymized Dataset D Prime]
    AK --> OS[External Sources e.g. Voter List]
    Pub --> Match[Linkage Attack]
    OS --> Match
    Match -->|Success| ReID[Re Identify Individual]
    Match -->|Fail| Safe[Privacy Preserved]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between anonymization and pseudonymization. Why is anonymization legally preferred for data sharing?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Anonymization** is the irreversible process of stripping identifying information so that re-identification is *impossible* by any reasonably likely means. It is one-way: once done, the link to the individual is destroyed forever. (1 Mark)
- **Pseudonymization** replaces identifiers with tokens or hashes that can be *reversed* if one possesses the secret key. The data subject remains identifiable to whoever controls the key. (1 Mark)
- **Legal preference:** Under GDPR Recital 26 and the DPDP Act 2023, truly anonymized data is **not "personal data"** and therefore lies outside the regulatory regime. Pseudonymized data is still personal data and must comply with all processing rules. This regulatory exemption is why anonymization is preferred for public data sharing. (1 Mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define quasi-identifier. Why is it a critical concept in anonymization?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- A **quasi-identifier (QI)** is a set of attributes (e.g., Age + ZIP + Gender) that, on their own, do not uniquely identify an individual but **can re-identify a person when combined** with external data. (1 Mark)
