# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**"Define ISO/IEC 27001 and explain its significance in Information Security Management."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

ISO/IEC 27001:2022 is an international standard published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** and the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)** that specifies the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an **Information Security Management System (ISMS)** within the context of an organization.

**Significance:**
1. Provides a systematic, risk-based approach to managing information security.
2. Enables certification by accredited third-party bodies, enhancing stakeholder trust.
3. Globally recognized, helping organizations meet legal, regulatory, and contractual obligations.
4. Supports the three pillars of information security: **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (3 Marks):** Definition — 1 Mark, Significance (any 2 points) — 1 Mark, CIA Triad reference — 1 Mark.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**"List the 11 privacy principles defined in ISO/IEC 29100:2011."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The 11 privacy principles are:

1. Consent and choice
2. Purpose legitimacy and specification
3. Data minimization
4. Collection limitation
5. Use, retention, and disclosure limitation
6. Accuracy and quality
7. Openness, transparency, and notice
8. Individual participation and access
9. Accountability
10. Information security
11. Privacy compliance

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (3 Marks):** All 11 principles — 3 Marks. Listing only 7–8 → 2 Marks. 5–6 → 1 Mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of **ISO/IEC 27001:2022**, highlighting its clauses and the PDCA cycle. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **11 privacy principles of ISO/IEC 29100** and explain how they map to the **GDPR requirements**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Structure of ISO/IEC 27001:2022 (7 Marks)**

ISO/IEC 27001:2022 follows a **10-clause structure**, with Clauses 0–3 being introductory and Clauses 4–10 being mandatory requirements. It also contains **Annex A** with 93 controls in 4 themes.

**Clause-wise Breakdown:**

- **Clause 1 — Scope:** Specifies the applicability of the standard.
- **Clause 2 — Normative References:** References ISO/IEC 27000.
- **Clause 3 — Terms and Definitions:** Vocabulary used throughout the standard.
- **Clause 4 — Context of the Organization:** Internal/external issues, interested parties, scope of ISMS.
- **Clause 5 — Leadership:** Top management commitment, ISMS policy, roles and responsibilities.
- **Clause 6 — Planning:** Risk assessment methodology, risk treatment, Statement of Applicability (SoA), objectives.
- **Clause 7 — Support:** Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information.
- **Clause 8 — Operation:** Operational planning and control, risk assessment execution, risk treatment implementation.
- **Clause 9 — Performance Evaluation:** Monitoring, measurement, internal audit, management review.
- **Clause 10 — Improvement:** Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement.

**PDCA Cycle:**

- **Plan (Clause 6):** Establish ISMS objectives, processes, and risk treatment plans.
- **Do (Clause 8):** Implement and operate the ISMS policies and controls.
- **Check (Clause 9):** Monitor, measure, audit, and review ISMS performance.
- **Act (Clause 10):** Take corrective actions and pursue continual improvement.

**Annex A — 93 Controls (4 Themes):**
1. Organizational controls (37)
2. People controls (8)
3. Physical controls (14)
4. Technological controls (34)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** Clause breakdown — 4 Marks, PDCA cycle — 2 Marks, Annex A structure — 1 Mark.

**(b) 11 Privacy Principles of ISO/IEC 29100 and Mapping to GDPR (7 Marks)**

| # | ISO/IEC 29100 Principle | GDPR Article | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Consent and choice | Art. 6, 7 | Explicit consent before processing PII. |
| 2 | Purpose legitimacy | Art. 5(1)(b) | Purpose must be specified, explicit, legitimate. |
| 3 | Data minimization | Art. 5(1)(c) | Only data necessary for the purpose. |
| 4 | Collection limitation | Art. 5(1)(b), 6 | Collect only with lawful and fair means. |
| 5 | Use, retention, disclosure limitation | Art. 5(1)(e) | Data must be erased when no longer needed. |
| 6 | Accuracy and quality | Art. 5(1)(d) | Data must be accurate and kept up to date. |
| 7 | Openness, transparency, notice | Art. 12–14 | Clear privacy notices to data subjects. |
| 8 | Individual participation and access | Art. 15, 17, 18 | Right to access, rectify, erase. |
| 9 | Accountability | Art. 5(2), 24 | Data controller must demonstrate compliance. |
| 10 | Information security | Art. 32 | Appropriate technical and organizational measures. |
| 11 | Privacy compliance | Art. 28, 37 | Demonstrate compliance with applicable laws and DPO. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** 11 principles — 2 Marks, GDPR mapping table — 4 Marks, Concluding statement — 1 Mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Alternate]**

**(a)** Describe the **ISO/IEC 42001:2023 AI Management System (AIMS)** standard, its structure, and its importance in governing ethical AI. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain **ISO/IEC 23894:2023 AI Risk Management** and discuss how AI bias as outlined in **ISO/IEC TR 24027** can be identified and mitigated in a data science project. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) ISO/IEC 42001:2023 AIMS (7 Marks)**

ISO/IEC 42001:2023 is the **world's first certifiable AI Management System standard**. It specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an **AIMS** within the context of an organization.

**Importance:**
1. Provides a structured way to govern AI development and deployment responsibly.
2. Enables third-party certification, increasing stakeholder and customer trust.
3. Aligns with global regulations (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF).
4. Addresses ethical concerns — bias, transparency, accountability, human oversight.

**Structure (Mirrors ISO 27001's High-Level Structure):**

- **Clause 4:** Context of the organization (AI mission, interested parties, scope).
- **Clause 5:** Leadership (top management commitment, AI policy).
- **Clause 6:** Planning (AI risk assessment, AI impact assessment, objectives).
- **Clause 7:** Support (resources, competence, awareness, documentation).
- **Clause 8:** Operation (AI lifecycle management, data governance for AI).
- **Clause 9:** Performance evaluation (monitoring, internal audit, management review).
- **Clause 10:** Improvement (corrective action, continual improvement).

**Annex A Controls (6 Categories):**
1. Policies related to AI
2. Internal organization for AI
3. Resources for AI systems
4. Impact assessment of AI systems
5. AI system lifecycle controls
6. Data for AI systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** Definition + importance — 3 Marks, Clauses — 2 Marks, Annex A — 1 Mark, Example — 1 Mark.

**(b) ISO/IEC 23894 AI Risk Management & ISO/IEC TR 24027 AI Bias (7 Marks)**

**ISO/IEC 23894:2023 — AI Risk Management:**

This standard provides guidance on **identifying, analyzing, evaluating, treating, monitoring, and communicating** AI-specific risks throughout the AI lifecycle.

**Key AI Risk Categories:**
- **Bias and discrimination** — unfair outcomes for protected groups.
- **Lack of transparency** — black-box model decisions.
- **Safety failures** — autonomous system errors causing harm.
- **Security threats** — adversarial attacks, data poisoning.
- **Privacy violations** — model inversion, membership inference.
- **Societal risks** — job displacement, misinformation.

**Risk Management Process:**
1. Context establishment
2. Risk identification
3. Risk analysis
4. Risk evaluation
5. Risk treatment (mitigate, transfer, accept, avoid)
6. Risk monitoring and review

**ISO/IEC TR 24027:2021 — Bias in AI Systems:**

This technical report provides an overview of **bias in AI systems** and AI-aided decision making, covering sources of bias, methods for measurement, and approaches to mitigation.

**Sources of AI Bias:**
- **Data bias** — unrepresentative training data.
- **Algorithmic bias** — model design choices.
- **Confirmation bias** — interpretability choices.
- **Pre-existing bias** — societal bias encoded in data.
- **Measurement bias** — faulty sensors or labels.

**Bias Identification Techniques:**
- **Fairness metrics:** demographic parity, equalized odds, predictive parity.
- **Disparate impact analysis:** comparing outcomes across groups.
- **Confusion matrix breakdown per subgroup.**
- **Counterfactual fairness testing.**

**Bias Mitigation Strategies:**
- **Pre-processing:** re-sampling, re-weighting, synthetic data generation.
- **In-processing:** adversarial debiasing, fairness constraints in loss function.
- **Post-processing:** calibration, threshold adjustment per group.
- **Governance:** diverse teams, ethics review boards, AI impact assessments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation (7 Marks):** 23894 explanation — 3 Marks, 24027 bias sources — 2 Marks, Mitigation — 2 Marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Conflating ISO 27001 (ISMS) with ISO 27701 (PIMS):** Students often write about both interchangeably. **Remember:** 27701 is a *privacy extension* of 27001, not a replacement. Clearly state this in every answer.
> 2. **Writing outdated year references:** Use **27001:2022** and **27701:2019** correctly. Writing just "ISO 27001" without the year is a **0.5 mark deduction** in KTU.
> 3. **Forgetting ISO/IEC 42001:** This is a **2023 standard** and the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly includes AI governance. Missing this is a guaranteed mark loss for 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Listing principles without explaining:** In a 7-mark question, listing the 11 ISO 29100 principles alone gives only **2 marks**. You must *explain* or *map* to get full marks.
> 5. **Mixing ISO with NIST:** NIST (US National Institute of Standards and Technology) is **not** an international standards body. NIST AI RMF is a *framework*, not an ISO/IEC standard. Examiners will deduct marks if you call NIST an "international standard."
> 6. **Skipping diagrams:** A flow diagram in a 14-mark answer adds at least **1–2 marks** of "presentation bonus" per KTU valuation patterns.
> 7. **Ignoring the PDCA cycle:** Every ISMS answer must reference PDCA — it is the structural backbone.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Checklist for International Standards: ISO/IEC**

- **ISO** = International Organization for Standardization (1947, Geneva, 167+ member countries).
- **IEC** = International Electrotechnical Commission (1906, Geneva).
- **JTC 1** = Joint Technical Committee 1 (ISO + IEC for ICT).
- **SC 27** governs Information Security, Cybersecurity, and Privacy.
- **SC 42** governs Artificial Intelligence.
- Standards are **voluntary** but become binding when referenced in law/contracts.
- Standards follow a **6-stage process**: NP → WD → CD → DIS → FDIS → IS.
- **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** — ISMS, 10 clauses (4–10 mandatory), 93 Annex A controls in 4 themes.
- **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act.
- **ISO/IEC 27701:2019** — PIMS, extension of 27001, adds PII controller/processor roles.
- **ISO/IEC 29100:2011** — 11 privacy principles (memorize all 11).
- **ISO/IEC 29134:2020** — Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA).
- **ISO/IEC 29184:2020** — Online privacy notices and consent.
- **ISO/IEC 27018:2019** — PII protection in public clouds.
- **ISO/IEC 27017:2015** — Cloud-specific security controls.
- **ISO/IEC 42001:2023** — World's first certifiable AI Management System (AIMS).
- **ISO/IEC 23894:2023** — AI risk management guidance.
- **ISO/IEC TR 24027:2021** — Bias in AI systems (sources, measurement, mitigation).
- **ISO/IEC TR 24028:2020** — Overview of trustworthiness in AI.
- **ISO/IEC 5259** — Data quality for analytics and ML (Parts 1–4).
- **Risk Equation (conceptual):** Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Asset Value × Likelihood × Impact.
- **Risk Treatment Options:** Mitigate, Transfer, Accept, Avoid.
- **Residual Risk:** Original Risk − Control Effectiveness.
- **GDPR ↔ ISO 27701/29100 mapping** is a frequent 14-mark question.
- **EU AI Act ↔ ISO 42001 ↔ NIST AI RMF** is a high-yield integration topic.
- **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) is the foundation of all 27000-series standards.
- **ISO standards are reviewed every 5 years** to remain current.
- **Membership:** ISO has **168 national member bodies** (BIS represents India).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for 11 ISO 29100 Principles — "CAPCO DAIAAIPC":**
> **C**onsent → **A**ccuracy → **P**urpose legitimacy → **C**ollection limitation → **O**penness → **D**isclosure limitation → **A**ccountability → **I**nformation security → **A**ccess → **I**ndividual participation → **P**rivacy compliance → **C**ompliance check.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-World Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT412 - DATA SCIENCE PRIVACY & ETHICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-World Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Real-World Case Study** in the context of Data Science Privacy & Ethics is a structured, evidence-based analytical examination of an actual event, deployment, or organizational practice in which data collection, processing, algorithmic decision-making, or surveillance technologies produced demonstrable **privacy harms, ethical violations, regulatory non-compliance, or societal harm**. These case studies serve as pedagogical instruments that convert abstract ethical theory (deontology, consequentialism, virtue ethics) and legal frameworks (GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA, DPDP Act 2023) into concrete, analyzable incidents.

Under the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, case studies are evaluated as instruments that map a real incident to:
- **Course Outcomes (CO)** – typically CO4 and CO5 of the PEADT412 syllabus.
- **Program Outcomes (PO)** – particularly PO6 (Ethics), PO8 (Ethics), and PO12 (Life-long learning).
- **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels** – *Analyze (L4)*, *Evaluate (L5)*, and *Create (L6)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A case study is NOT a news summary. It is a *structured forensic analysis* that identifies the **stakeholders, data flows, harm vectors, legal violations, ethical principles breached, and remediation strategies** of a privacy/ethics incident.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a real-world case study like a **medical post-mortem report**:

| Medical Post-Mortem | Data Science Case Study |
|---------------------|-------------------------|
| Patient's body | Organization's data ecosystem |
| Cause of death | Privacy breach / ethical harm |
| Symptoms | Data leaks, biased outcomes, user complaints |
| Pathology report | Forensic audit trail, logs, regulatory findings |
| Preventive prescription | New policies, technical safeguards, legal reforms |

Just as a doctor learns what causes heart attacks by studying deceased patients, a data ethics student learns what causes privacy disasters by studying real organizations. The case study is the **"tissue sample"** that reveals the structural pathology of the data-driven world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to analyze *at least three* landmark case studies, identifying stakeholders, ethical dimensions, applicable laws, and lessons learned. The university examiner expects structured answers — not narrative storytelling.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Case Study Analysis

The following quantitative/qualitative metrics are routinely applied when analyzing a privacy/ethics case:

- **PII Records Exposed** — measured in **millions** (e.g., 147M in Equifax, 2017)
- **Regulatory Fine Amount** — measured in **USD/EUR** (e.g., €1.2B for Meta, 2023)
- **Time-to-Discovery (TTD)** — days between breach occurrence and detection
- **Time-to-Disclosure (TTDc)** — days between detection and public notification
- **Consent Opt-In Rate** — percentage of users agreeing to data processing
- **Bias Disparity Ratio** — ratio of adverse outcomes between protected and unprotected groups
- **Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) Volume** — number of GDPR Article 15 requests filed

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant:** Under GDPR Article 4, a personal data breach is defined as *a breach of security leading to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorized disclosure of, or access to, personal data transmitted, stored, or otherwise processed*. The **72-hour notification window** is the global benchmark.

## 1.4 Classification of Case Studies (KTU Taxonomy)

Case studies in this module are typically classified along three axes:

1. **By Sector:** Healthcare, Finance, Social Media, Government, Retail, Technology
2. **By Harm Type:** Privacy breach, Surveillance overreach, Algorithmic bias, Consent failure, Data monetization abuse
3. **By Resolution:** Litigation, Regulatory fine, Self-remediation, Legislative reform, No action

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, students must be able to classify any case study into this taxonomy and justify the classification in **2-3 sentences** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Forensic Analysis Framework (FAF) — The Standard KTU Structure

Every KTU Board examiner expects a case study answer to follow this six-stage analytical sequence:

### Stage 1 — Incident Cartography
Identify **what happened**, **when**, **where**, and **to whom**. This stage establishes the temporal and geographic perimeter of the case.

### Stage 2 — Stakeholder Mapping
Enumerate all parties with a stake:
- **Data Subjects** (users, customers, citizens)
- **Data Controllers** (organizations collecting data)
- **Data Processors** (third-party vendors)
- **Regulators** (Data Protection Authorities)
- **Society at large** (the public interest)
- **Affected minorities / vulnerable groups**

### Stage 3 — Data Flow Reconstruction
Trace the data lifecycle within the incident:
$$\text{Collection} \rightarrow \text{Storage} \rightarrow \text{Processing} \rightarrow \text{Sharing} \rightarrow \text{Deletion}$$

Identify at which stage the failure occurred. This is the **"ground zero"** of the breach.

### Stage 4 — Harm Vector Identification
Classify the harm:
- **Privacy Harm** (loss of control over personal information)
- **Economic Harm** (financial loss, fraud)
- **Psychological Harm** (anxiety, shame, chilling effect)
- **Social Harm** (discrimination, exclusion)
- **Autonomy Harm** (manipulation, loss of agency)

### Stage 5 — Legal & Ethical Principle Mapping
Map the incident to:
- **GDPR Articles** (e.g., Article 5 — Lawfulness; Article 6 — Consent; Article 32 — Security)
- **DPDP Act 2023** (India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act)
- **HIPAA** (US healthcare)
- **OECD Privacy Principles**
- **The Four Bioethics Principles** (Beauchamp & Childress): Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice

### Stage 6 — Remediation & Lessons Learned
Identify corrective actions, policy reforms, and technical safeguards implemented post-incident.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The Stages 1–6 framework is the *de facto* valuation key. Skipping any stage results in a 2-3 mark deduction. The Stage 3 (Data Flow) and Stage 5 (Legal Mapping) stages carry the **highest weightage** in the 14-mark Part B questions.

## 2.2 The Ethical Triage Triangle

When analyzing a case, apply this three-axis ethical diagnostic:

```
                  AUTONOMY
                    /\
                   /  \
                  /    \
                 /  ??? \
                /--------\
         JUSTICE  ----  NON-MALEFICENCE
```

- **Autonomy axis:** Did the organization respect user choice and informed consent?
- **Non-maleficence axis:** Did the system cause avoidable harm?
- **Justice axis:** Were the benefits and burdens distributed fairly across demographic groups?

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While case studies are largely qualitative, KTU examiners award marks for **quantitative reasoning**. The following equations and metrics appear in high-scoring answers.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | **Regulatory Fine Ceiling (GDPR)** | $F_{max} = \max(0.04 \cdot R, 20M)$ | Maximum fine = greater of 4% global annual turnover or €20M | EUR |
| 2 | **Regulatory Fine Ceiling (GDPR Tier 2)** | $F_{max,2} = \max(0.02 \cdot R, 10M)$ | Tier 2 violations = 2% turnover or €10M | EUR |
| 3 | **Disclosure Delay Penalty** | $P_{delay} = D \cdot k$ | D = days delayed; k = jurisdiction-specific penalty rate | Days |
| 4 | **Breach Severity Index (BSI)** | $BSI = \frac{N \cdot V \cdot S}{T_{detect}}$ | N = records exposed, V = sensitivity, S = exploitability, T = time-to-detect | Index |
| 5 | **Bias Disparity Ratio** | $DR = \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=minority)}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=majority)}$ | Ratio of positive outcomes across demographic groups | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Consent Validity Score** | $C_v = f \cdot c \cdot u$ | f = freely given, c = clear, u = unambiguous (each 0 or 1) | 0 to 1 |
| 7 | **Population Impact Ratio** | $PIR = \frac{N_{affected}}{N_{total}}$ | Fraction of user base impacted by the incident | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Time-to-Notify Compliance** | $C_{tn} = \mathbb{1}(T_{notify} \le 72h)$ | Indicator = 1 if GDPR Article 33 complied, else 0 | Boolean |
| 9 | **Compound Annual Growth of Breach Cost** | $C_{n} = C_{0}(1 + r)^{n}$ | r = annual growth rate of breach cost (IBM report: ~7-15%) | USD |
| 10 | **False Positive Parity Difference** | $\Delta FPR = FPR_{group_A} - FPR_{group_B}$ | Algorithmic fairness metric for binary classifiers | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Absolute values are written with `\vert` and `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to prevent markdown table breakage. Never use `|` inside the table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

This analytical framework is not academic ornamentation. It is the **industry-standard playbook** used by:
- **Data Protection Officers (DPOs)** conducting internal audits
- **Regulatory bodies** (ICO, CNIL, DPC Ireland) issuing enforcement decisions
- **Forensic cybersecurity firms** (Mandiant, CrowdStrike) in post-incident reports
- **Academic researchers** publishing in journals like *Nature Machine Intelligence* and *IEEE Security & Privacy*
- **Corporate boards** drafting AI ethics governance frameworks

A KTU graduate who masters this framework is **directly employable** as a privacy analyst, GRC (Governance-Risk-Compliance) associate, or AI ethics reviewer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Derivation — Applying the GDPR Fine Formula to a Real Case

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked example is a **hallmark of KTU 14-mark answers**. Examiners allocate up to 4 marks specifically for quantitative legal reasoning.

### Case: Meta (Facebook) — May 2023 GDPR Fine by Irish DPC

**Given data:**
- Global annual turnover of Meta Platforms, Inc. (2022): $R$ = **\$116.6 billion USD**
- USD to EUR conversion (May 2023): $\approx 0.92$
- Fine levied: **€1.2 billion**

**Step 1 — Convert turnover to EUR for GDPR comparison.**

$$R_{EUR} = \$116.6 \times 10^{9} \times 0.92 = €107.272 \times 10^{9}$$

This gives us a turnover of approximately **€107.27 billion**.

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum fine under Article 83(5).**

The maximum fine for a Tier 1 GDPR violation is the greater of:

$$F_{max} = \max(0.04 \cdot R_{EUR}, 20{,}000{,}000)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the first candidate.**

$$0.04 \cdot R_{EUR} = 0.04 \times 107.272 \times 10^{9} = 4.29088 \times 10^{9}$$

This equals approximately **€4.29 billion**.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second candidate.**

$$20{,}000{,}000 = €20 \times 10^{6}$$

This equals **€20 million**.

**Step 5 — Take the maximum of the two candidates.**

$$F_{max} = \max(€4.29 \times 10^{9}, €20 \times 10^{6}) = €4.29 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the actual fine as a fraction of the ceiling.**

$$\text{Fraction Levied} = \frac{€1.2 \times 10^{9}}{€4.29 \times 10^{9}} \approx 0.2797$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result.**

$$\text{Fraction Levied} \approx 27.97\%$$

**Interpretation:** The Irish DPC imposed a fine equal to roughly **28% of the statutory maximum**. In KTU Board language, this represents a *severe but not maximum-tier* enforcement — typically indicating aggravating factors (the volume of EU data subjects affected) without reaching the theoretical ceiling reserved for the most egregious violations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners reward students who show ALL seven steps.** A common student error is stopping at Step 5. Always include a **Step 7 — Interpretation** because the examiner must see *applied ethical-legal reasoning*, not just arithmetic.

## 3.2 The 14-Mark Analytical Matrix — Mapping a Case to All Six Stages

The following matrix is the **gold standard structure** for a KTU 14-mark case study answer. Memorize the column headers.

| Stage | Analytical Lens | Key Output Required | Mark Weight |
|-------|----------------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1. Incident Cartography | Chronological + geographic perimeter | 4 W's summary (What/When/Where/Whom) | 2 marks |
| 2. Stakeholder Mapping | Power-and-interest grid | List of 5+ stakeholders with roles | 2 marks |
| 3. Data Flow Reconstruction | Lifecycle forensics | Identify the failure node in the data pipeline | 3 marks |
| 4. Harm Vector Identification | Multi-dimensional harm taxonomy | Classify into 2+ harm categories with evidence | 2 marks |
| 5. Legal & Ethical Mapping | Statute + principle crosswalk | Map to 2+ laws and 2+ ethical principles | 3 marks |
| 6. Remediation & Lessons | Forward-looking governance | Propose 3+ technical/organizational controls | 2 marks |
| **Total** | — | — | **14 marks** |

## 3.3 Comparative Case Analysis — Bias Disparity Ratio Application

### Case: COMPAS Recidivism Algorithm (ProPublica, 2016)

**Context:** The COMPAS algorithm predicted re-offending risk. ProPublica found it was biased against African-American defendants.

**Given (illustrative, derived from public ProPublica data):**

- $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=\text{African-American}) = 0.45$
- $P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=\text{Caucasian}) = 0.23$

**Step 1 — Apply the Bias Disparity Ratio formula.**

$$DR = \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=\text{African-American})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=\text{Caucasian})}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$DR = \frac{0.45}{0.23}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$DR \approx 1.9565$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

A $DR \approx 1.96$ means African-American defendants were flagged as high-risk at **roughly 1.96 times** the rate of Caucasian defendants.

**Step 5 — Ethical interpretation using the Justice axis.**

A disparity ratio deviating significantly from 1.0 indicates a violation of the **Justice principle** (Beauchamp & Childress). Under GDPR Article 22 and the proposed EU AI Act, such a system would require:

- Mandatory **bias audit** before deployment
- **Human-in-the-loop** review for all adverse decisions
- **Right to explanation** under Article 22(3)

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's Note:** The ProPublica authors later published a counter-analysis (Flores, Bechtel, Lowenkamp, 2016) showing the algorithm was *calibrated* across groups. This is the **Calibration vs. Fairness paradox** — a favorite KTU question. The lesson: there is no single "fair" metric; fairness itself is contested.

## 3.4 The Five Landmark Case Studies (KTU Board Favorites)

### Case A — Cambridge Analytica (2018)
- **What:** Harvested Facebook profiles of 87M users without consent via a personality quiz app.
- **Failure node:** Stage 3 (Sharing) — data shared with third party in violation of platform policy.
- **Principles breached:** Consent, Autonomy, Transparency.
- **Outcome:** FTC fined Facebook \$5B; Cambridge Analytica declared bankruptcy.

### Case B — Equifax Data Breach (2017)
- **What:** 147M Americans' PII exposed due to an unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability.
- **Failure node:** Stage 2 (Storage) — failure to apply a known security patch for 2 months.
- **Principles breached:** Non-maleficence, Security-by-design.
- **Outcome:** \$700M settlement; CISO indicted.

### Case C — Clearview AI (2020-present)
- **What:** Scraped 30B+ facial images from social media without consent for law-enforcement use.
- **Failure node:** Stage 1 (Collection) — scraped data without lawful basis.
- **Principles breached:** Lawfulness, Purpose limitation, Biometric privacy.
- **Outcome:** Banned in EU, Canada, Australia; ongoing litigation.

### Case D — Aadhaar Data Leaks (India, 2018)
- **What:** 1.1B Indian residents' Aadhaar biometric data exposed via insecure government websites.
- **Failure node:** Stage 2 (Storage) — no access controls on public dashboards.
- **Principles breached:** Data minimization, State surveillance, Non-maleficence.
- **Outcome:** India's Supreme Court (Puttaswamy II, 2017) established a *fundamental right to privacy*; DPDP Act 2023 enacted.

### Case E — Royal Free NHS / DeepMind (UK, 2016-2017)
- **What:** 1.6M patient records shared with Google DeepMind for a kidney injury app without proper consent.
- **Failure node:** Stage 3 (Sharing) — improper data-sharing agreement under GDPR.
- **Principles breached:** Lawfulness, Fairness, Transparency.
- **Outcome:** ICO ruled the Royal Free violated the Data Protection Act 1998; revised UK GDPR guidance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six-Stage Forensic Analysis Framework (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Incident Cartography] --> A1[What When Where Whom]
    A1 --> B[Stage 2: Stakeholder Mapping]
    B --> B1[Data Subjects]
    B --> B2[Data Controllers]
    B --> B3[Data Processors]
    B --> B4[Regulators]
    B --> B5[Society at Large]
    B1 --> C[Stage 3: Data Flow Reconstruction]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> C1[Collection]
    C --> C2[Storage]
    C --> C3[Processing]
    C --> C4[Sharing]
    C --> C5[Deletion]
    C5 --> D[Stage 4: Harm Vector Identification]
    D --> D1[Privacy Harm]
    D --> D2[Economic Harm]
    D --> D3[Psychological Harm]
    D --> D4[Social Harm]
    D --> D5[Autonomy Harm]
    D5 --> E[Stage 5: Legal and Ethical Mapping]
    E --> E1[GDPR Articles]
    E --> E2[DPDP Act 2023]
    E --> E3[OECD Principles]
    E --> E4[Four Bioethics Principles]
    E4 --> F[Stage 6: Remediation and Lessons]
    F --> F1[Technical Controls]
    F --> F2[Organizational Policies]
    F --> F3[Legal Reforms]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`A1`, `B3`). No reserved keywords used. No markdown formatting inside labels.

## 4.2 Data Flow Architecture — Cambridge Analytica Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Facebook User] --> B[This Is Your Digital Life App]
    B --> C[Personality Quiz Data]
    B --> D[Friend Network Scraping]
    C --> E[Cambridge Analytica Server]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Psychographic Profiling]
    F --> G[Targeted Political Ads]
    G --> H[2016 US Election Influence]
    E --> I[Breach Discovery 2018]
    I --> J[FTC Investigation]
    J --> K[Facebook Fine 5 Billion USD]
    J --> L[Cambridge Analytica Bankruptcy]
```

## 4.3 Ethical Triage Triangle — Visual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Triage[Ethical Triage Triangle]
        A1[Autonomy] --- A2[Was informed consent obtained]
        B1[Non-maleficence] --- B2[Did the system cause avoidable harm]
        C1[Justice] --- C2[Were burdens and benefits fairly distributed]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    B1 --> B2
    C1 --> C2
    A2 --> D{All Three Satisfied}
    B2 --> D
    C2 --> D
    D -->|Yes| E[Ethically Defensible Deployment]
    D -->|No| F[Requires Remediation or Withdrawal]
```

## 4.4 GDPR Fine Tier Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GDPR Violation Detected] --> B{Which Article Violated}
    B -->|Article 5 6 7 9 22 etc| C[Tier 1 Maximum Fine]
    B -->|Article 25 32 33 34 etc| D[Tier 2 Maximum Fine]
    C --> E[Fmax equals max of 0.04 times R or 20M EUR]
    D --> F[Fmax equals max of 0.02 times R or 10M EUR]
    E --> G{DPC Discretion}
    F --> G
    G -->|Aggravating Factors| H[Fine at Upper End]
    G -->|Mitigating Factors| I[Fine at Lower End]
    H --> J[Final Fine Amount]
    I --> J
```

## 4.5 Stakeholder Power-Interest Grid (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HighPowerLowInterest[High Power Low Interest - Keep Satisfied]
        A1[Regulators]
        A2[Government Agencies]
    end
    subgraph HighPowerHighInterest[High Power High Interest - Manage Closely]
        B1[Data Controllers Board of Directors]
        B2[Major Shareholders]
    end
    subgraph LowPowerHighInterest[Low Power High Interest - Keep Informed]
        C1[End Users Data Subjects]
        C2[Civil Society NGOs]
        C3[Journalists]
    end
    subgraph LowPowerLowInterest[Low Power Low Interest - Monitor]
        D1[General Public]
        D2[Academic Researchers]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test *Recall (L1)* and *Understanding (L2)* in Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Keep answers to **50-80 words** with at least **one numerical fact** for top marks.

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Define a "real-world case study" in the context of data science privacy and ethics. Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**

A real-world case study in data science privacy and ethics is a structured forensic analysis of an actual data-driven incident, examining stakeholders, data flows, harms, legal violations, and remediation. It converts abstract ethical theory into analyzable evidence.

**Example:** The **2017 Equifax breach** exposing **147M Americans' PII** due to an unpatched vulnerability, resulting in a **\$700M settlement** and indictment of the CISO.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three harm categories that a privacy case study typically identifies.**

**Model Answer:**

The three harm categories are:

1. **Privacy Harm** — loss of control over personal information (e.g., Aadhaar leaks).
2. **Economic Harm** — financial loss to data subjects (e.g., Equifax enabling identity theft).
3. **Social Harm** — discrimination and exclusion of vulnerable groups (e.g., COMPAS algorithm bias).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B answers must follow a **structured format with sub-parts**. Examiners allocate marks for headings, diagrams, and numerical facts — not just prose.

---

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Analyze the **Cambridge Analytica case (2018)** using the six-stage Forensic Analysis Framework. Identify the stakeholders, the data flow failure node, and the ethical principles breached.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the maximum possible GDPR fine that could have been levied on Facebook (Meta) in 2018, given a global annual turnover of **\$55.8 billion USD** (2017) and an exchange rate of **0.85 USD/EUR**. Comment on why the actual fine (\$5B FTC, separate from GDPR) differed from the GDPR ceiling.

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Stage 1 — Incident Cartography:**
- **What:** Unauthorized harvesting of Facebook profile data
- **When:** 2014-2015 (data harvest); March 2018 (public disclosure)
- **Where:** United States, United Kingdom, with global impact
- **Whom:** **87 million** Facebook users, predominantly US voters

**Stage 2 — Stakeholder Mapping:**
- Data Subjects: 87M Facebook users
- Data Controller: Facebook (Meta)
- Data Processor: Cambridge Analytica, Aleksandr Kogan's "This Is Your Digital Life" app
- Platform: Facebook Open Graph API
- Society: Democratic processes in US/UK

**Stage 3 — Data Flow Reconstruction:**
- **Failure node:** Stage 3 (Sharing) — Facebook's Open Graph API allowed the app to harvest data not just of consenting users but also their friends, in violation of the platform's 2014 policy update. Kogan's app then transferred this data to Cambridge Analytica, breaching the data-use agreement.

**Stage 4 — Harm Vector Identification:**
- **Privacy Harm:** Loss of control over personal data
- **Psychological Harm:** Chilling effect on political expression
- **Autonomy Harm:** Manipulation of voter behavior through psychographic targeting

**Stage 5 — Legal & Ethical Mapping:**
- **GDPR Article 6** (Lawful basis for processing) — violated; consent was not freely given or informed
- **GDPR Article 5** (Purpose limitation) — violated; data used for political profiling, not academic research as claimed
- **FTC Consent Decree (2011)** — violated; Facebook deceived users about privacy controls
- **Beauchamp & Childress Principle of Autonomy** — violated; no meaningful consent

**Stage 6 — Remediation & Lessons:**
- Facebook restricted third-party app data access (April 2018)
- Imposed a **\$5 billion FTC fine** (2019)
- Cambridge Analytica filed for bankruptcy (May 2018)
- Industry-wide adoption of stricter API access controls

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Convert USD turnover to EUR.**

$$R_{EUR} = \$55.8 \times 10^{9} \times 0.85 = €47.43 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Tier 1 maximum fine formula.**

$$F_{max} = \max(0.04 \cdot R_{EUR}, 20{,}000{,}000)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the percentage component.**

$$0.04 \times €47.43 \times 10^{9} = €1.8972 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the fixed component.**

$$€1.8972 \times 10^{9} \gg €20 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 5 — Final maximum fine.**

$$F_{max} \approx €1.8972 \times 10^{9} \approx €1.90 \text{ billion}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on the actual penalty.**

The actual fine of **\$5B from the FTC** was imposed under **US Federal Trade Commission jurisdiction**, not GDPR. The **Irish DPC** did not levy a fine in 2018 because:
1. The breach occurred in **2014-2015**, before GDPR's enforcement date (**May 25, 2018**)
2. GDPR's **territorial scope** (Article 3) was not yet established for extraterritorial processing
3. Facebook's EU establishment (Ireland) had not yet finalized its regulatory procedure

This illustrates the **regulatory lag problem**: data harms outpace the legal frameworks designed to prevent them.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Examine the **Aadhaar data leak case (India, 2018)**. Identify the data flow failure node, the legal precedent that emerged, and how the **DPDP Act 2023** addresses the gap.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a suitable diagram, explain the **Ethical Triage Triangle** and apply it to the **COMPAS recidivism algorithm case**. Compute the bias disparity ratio given:

$$P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=\text{African-American}) = 0.45, \quad P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=\text{Caucasian}) = 0.23$$

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Stage 1 — Incident Cartography:**
- **What:** Mass exposure of Aadhaar numbers, biometric data, and personal details
- **When:** Disclosed publicly in **early 2018** by journalist Rachna Khaira (Tribune investigation)
- **Where:** India, via insecure government websites and dashboards
- **Whom:** **1.1 billion** Indian residents enrolled in Aadhaar

**Stage 2 — Stakeholder Mapping:**
- Data Subjects: 1.1B Aadhaar holders
- Data Controller: UIDAI (Unique Identification Authority of India)
- Data Processors: State governments, banks, telecom operators, welfare agencies
- Regulator: None effective at the time
- Society: India's democratic and welfare architecture

**Stage 3 — Data Flow Reconstruction:**
- **Failure node:** Stage 2 (Storage) — government dashboards and third-party websites allowed unauthenticated access to Aadhaar numbers. The Tribune journalist paid **₹500** to access the entire database, demonstrating a complete absence of access controls.

**Stage 4 — Harm Vector Identification:**
- **Privacy Harm:** Aadhaar numbers function as universal identifiers, enabling cross-database linkage
- **Economic Harm:** Identity theft, fraudulent bank accounts
- **Social Harm:** Exclusion of marginalized groups from welfare schemes due to biometric authentication failures
- **Psychological Harm:** Surveillance anxiety among citizens

**Stage 5 — Legal & Ethical Mapping:**
- **Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)** — Supreme Court of India declared the **Right to Privacy as a fundamental right** under Article 21 of the Constitution
- **Aadhaar Act 2016** — though it mandated data protection, lacked enforceable penalty mechanisms
- **OECD Privacy Principles** — Purpose limitation violated; data used for purposes beyond subsidy delivery

**Stage 6 — Remediation & Lessons — DPDP Act 2023:**
- Imposes **fiduciary duty** on data collectors
- Establishes the **Data Protection Board of India** for adjudication
- Mandates **consent-based processing** with explicit exceptions
- Grants **right to erasure** and **right to grievance redressal**
- Penalizes breaches with fines up to **₹250 crore**

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Diagram (described):**
The Ethical Triage Triangle is an equilateral triangle with three vertices: **Autonomy (top)**, **Non-maleficence (bottom-left)**, and **Justice (bottom-right)**. A deployment is ethically defensible only if all three principles are satisfied.

**Step 1 — Apply the Bias Disparity Ratio formula.**

$$DR = \frac{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=\text{African-American})}{P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=\text{Caucasian})}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$DR = \frac{0.45}{0.23}$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$DR \approx 1.9565$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation using the Triage Triangle:**

- **Autonomy:** Defendants were not informed about the algorithm's role in sentencing recommendations — **partial violation**.
- **Non-maleficence:** The high false-positive rate for African-American defendants caused tangible harm (longer sentences, denied bail) — **clear violation**.
- **Justice:** A $DR \approx 1.96$ shows the burden of false positives was not distributed equally across demographic groups — **clear violation**.

**Conclusion:** The COMPAS deployment fails on **two of three** Triage axes. Under the proposed **EU AI Act**, such a system would be classified as **"high-risk"** and require conformity assessment, bias audit, and human oversight before deployment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT write narrative answers.** A case study answer without the six-stage structure loses 3-4 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT omit numerical facts.** "Cambridge Analytica affected users" scores 0.5 marks. "87 million users" scores full marks.
> 3. **Do NOT confuse legal jurisdictions.** GDPR ≠ FTC ≠ HIPAA. Always state which law and which article.
> 4. **Do NOT skip the Interpretation step.** After every computation (fine amount, disparity ratio), add a sentence explaining *what it means ethically*.
> 5. **Do NOT ignore vulnerable groups.** A case study answer that fails to mention marginalized communities (women, minorities, disabled persons) loses the "Justice" marks.
> 6. **Do NOT use vague phrases** like "privacy was violated." Use precise terms: *unlawful processing under GDPR Article 6*, *absence of informed consent under Article 7*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Final Page for the Night-Before Exam**

- ✅ A **case study** is a *structured forensic analysis*, not a news article.
- ✅ Use the **6-Stage Forensic Analysis Framework**: Cartography → Stakeholders → Data Flow → Harm → Legal Mapping → Remediation.
- ✅ **GDPR Tier 1** max fine = $\max(0.04R, €20M)$; **Tier 2** = $\max(0.02R, €10M)$.
- ✅ **GDPR Article 33** requires breach notification within **72 hours**.
- ✅ The **72-hour rule** is the global benchmark for breach disclosure.
- ✅ **Four Bioethics Principles** (Beauchamp & Childress): Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice.
- ✅ **Bias Disparity Ratio** > 1.0 indicates disparate treatment; perfect fairness = 1.0.
- ✅ **Five Landmark Cases to Memorize:** Cambridge Analytica, Equifax, Clearview AI, Aadhaar, Royal Free/DeepMind.
- ✅ **DPDP Act 2023** (India) addresses Aadhaar's pre-2018 regulatory vacuum.
- ✅ **EU AI Act** classifies biometric, judicial, and welfare AI as **high-risk**.
- ✅ The **Calibration vs. Fairness paradox**: A system can be statistically calibrated yet produce disparate impacts.
- ✅ Always quantify: **87M users**, **147M records**, **€1.2B fine**, **₹500 bribe price**.
- ✅ **Stakeholder Map** must include at least: data subjects, controllers, processors, regulators, society.
- ✅ The **failure node** (where in the data lifecycle) is the most important forensic finding.
- ✅ Map every case to **at least 2 laws** AND **at least 2 ethical principles** for full marks.
